EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting snow brought unusually strong currents and cold water to area waterways in the month of May.

Warm weather was occurring with highs in the region in the mid to upper 80s, while area waterways were cold with strong currents from mountain snowmelt.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ramona Wildfire burned 350 acres, prompting evacuation orders for populated areas in Riverside County. The fire ignited on May 22 and was 100% contained by 8:00 PM on May 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 350 acre vegetation fire burned in the San Jacinto Valley in Riverside County from the afternoon on May 22 through the evening on May 25. The ignition occurred in the Ramona Expressway and North Warren Road area. The fire approached residential area as well as dairy farms, prompting evacuation orders and warnings through the late evening on May 22 before being lifted. The fire was 100% contained by 8:00 PM on May 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four divers off Fanshell Beach in the Pebble Beach area were dragged out to sea by a rip current. Three of the divers were able to return to shore safely, but the other perished in the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Search and Rescue team were unable to locate the diver who was swept out to sea by a rip current. Point Sur Buoy (Station 46239) measured average seas between 8 and 9 feet that day. Nearby Cabrillo Point buoy (Station 46240) in Monterey Bay, measured average seas 3 to 4 feet. Winds were not measured by the buoys, but the official forecast for the coastal zone from Point Pinos to Point Piedras Blancas called for NW winds 10-20 kt, with gusts up to 30 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December finished up early on New Year's day.  This atmospheric river (AR) enhanced winter storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December with heavy rainfall continuing below that elevation.  But colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Year���s Eve that carried over into the overnight hours through early morning New Year���s day. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus of new snow with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus of accumulating snow above 7000 feet before the winter system exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COCORAHS Station (CA-NV-51) located 2.0 mi N Truckee, CA, and CO-OP observer located 1.0 mi SSW of Boca Reservoir, CA, reported 24 hour heavy rainfall totals of 5.57 and 4.62 inches respectively during the period from 12/31/22 through 1/1/23.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding was reported along Mark West Creek near the communities of Forestville and Trenton. A 43-year old woman from Ukiah was recovered on the 10th after her car got stuck in floodwaters. She had called 911 to report that her car was taking on water on Trenton-Healdsburg Road. The call cut out, and Sonoma County Fire sent swift water rescue gear around 10:10 AM on the 9th. A helicopter from the Sonoma County Sheriff's office was also involved. The search was called off at sunset, and by the morning of the 10th, the vehicle was found sitting in 8 to 10 feet of floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm produced several waves of new snow and high winds that impacted the region during the third week of January. A relatively long-duration atmospheric river from 9-11 January supported forcing and moisture for the first in the series of winter systems that brought heavy Sierra snowfall with accumulations ranging from 3-6 feet across the Sierra Crest. The heaviest snowfall occurred in Mono County over the 3-day period with heavy, unrelenting snowfall that forced a prolonged closure of US-395 from Bridgeport to Bishop from late morning January 9th through the afternoon January 11th.  On January 9th, heavy rain across the Lake Tahoe Basin changed to heavy snow below 7000 ft, leading to snowfall totals ranging from 8-20 inches through January 11th. Over 2 feet of snowfall was observed in parts of northeast California above 5000 feet. On January 9th mostly rain fell across western Nevada with storm total precipitation amounts ranging between 0.75-2 inches across much of Douglas, Carson City, southern Washoe, Mineral, and Lyon counties. The heavier rainfall across those contributed to a major rockfall and subsequently lead to an extended road closure on SR-208 in Wilson Canyon (Figure 4). Reports of areal flooding included I-80 near Lockwood, NN, Geiger Grade at Toll Rd in the Virginia City highlands, and along US-395 south of Reno with flooding from Steamboat Creek spilling over Towne Road. The changeover to snow in western Nevada arrived during the morning commute on January 10th which led to several related traffic incidents that included a 19-car pile-up on the Galena Bridge on I-580.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, and 7.0 W of Truckee, CA at the Central Sierra Snow Lab, reported 24 hour total snowfall of 6.0 and 4.3 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak short wave troughs brushed through the region and brought periods of gusty north-northeast ridge winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station located at Mammoth Mountain Summit, Chair 10 - Tower 20, reported a max wind gust of 99 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December finished up early on New Year's day.  This atmospheric river (AR) enhanced winter storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December with heavy rainfall continuing below that elevation.  But colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Year���s Eve that carried over into the overnight hours through early morning New Year���s day. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus of new snow with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus of accumulating snow above 7000 feet before the winter system exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (LOBC1-SNOTEL) located 8.4 mi S of Walker, CA and mesonet station (SRAC1-SNOTEL) located 2.0 mi SE of Sonora Pass, CA reported 24 hour heavy rainfall totals of 3.6 and 3.4 inches  respectively during the period from 12/31/22 through 1/1/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montague ASOS reported a peak gust of 71 mph on 1/4 at 353 pm.  North Weed reported a peak gust of 66 mph on 1/4 at 400 pm. Similarly, Weed Airport reported 60 mph on 1/4 at 548 pm and Gazelle with 60 mph on 1/4 at 640 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge of high pressure remained near the California coast during the morning of January 28. As a result, an area of dense fog formed in the San Joaquin Valley north of Fresno before sunrise which impacted travel along SR 99 until late morning when the fog dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater, Merced and Madera. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a traffic collision near the intersection of Libson Rd. and S. Bert Crane Rd. in Merced County. The response found the roadway to be completely flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a washed up tree blocking the roadway on Ave. 381 just east of Rd. 154 in Seville.

Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of roadway flooding at the intersection of Elm Ave. and Davis Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report that Ashlan Ave. was closed east of Fresno due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on Grantland Ave. at W. Shaw Ave. in Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding on SR 33 just north of Sullivan Rd. in Gustine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems in close succession brought very heavy snowfall to the Riverside County Mountains, with the first occurring along a cold front late on January 14 into the morning hours of January 15, with the second system close on its heels, producing additional snowfall late on January 15 to early on January 17. The first system produced up to 11 inches of snow neat Mt San Jacinto at 8400 feet at the Long Valley Ranger Station at the top of the Palm Springs Tram. The second system had lower snow levels, with 4 to 6 inches reported as low as 5700 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong Satna Ana winds impacted all of Southern California the morning of January 26. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were reported across the coast, with wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph in the valleys and gusts over 80 mph in the mountains. Major interstate closures, including I-8 in San Diego County, and I-15 and I-215 in San Bernardino County, occurred as the result of overturned semi trucks and fallen trees and powerlines. Dozens of trees were downed and snapped in Balboa Park, including one that fell on and injured a woman, resulting in the closure of the park for the remainder of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds impacted the Santa Ana Mountains through much of the morning on January 26. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph occurred, especially from 9 AM through noon. The following are the strongest wind gusts in the Santa Ana Mountains:||Fremont Canyon reported a peak wind gust of 81 mph.|Pleasants Peak reported a peak wind gust of 75 mph.|Chino Hills reported a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage on the large storm which moved through the area between December 30 and January 1, skies cleared out and winds diminished across the area on January 2. Patchy fog formed toward daybreak on January 3. The fog was most pronounced near Bakersfield where several webcams showed reduced visibility along SR 99 and SR 59 for a few hours in the morning. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Bakersfield. Several webcams along SR 99 and SR 58 indicated visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although a cold upper low pressure system approached the area from the north during the morning of January 29, an area of dense fog developed around sunrise in the center portion of the San Joaquin Valley between Fresno and Visalia. The fog impacted travel along SR 99 through Fowler, Selma and Kingsburg where web cameras indicated visibility below 1000 feet for a few hours. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported closing the mouth of Kern Canyon with two sections of large boulders blocking the eastbound lanes of SR 178 near NM 17 and NM 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope low clouds formed along the east side of the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra Foothills in Fresno and Tulare Counties in the wake of a cold winter storm which brought some light precipitation to the area during the morning of January 30. The low clouds lowered into a fog layer by sunrise which disrupted the morning commute in the after mentioned areas. Some dense fog also formed as a result of upslope along the valley facing slopes of the Tehachapi Mountains during the afternoon. The fog dissipated by the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley and Porterville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Television stations showed visibility below 1000 feet in the lower Sierra Foothills in Fresno and Tulare Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January 4th through the 5th, a medium-strength winter storm brought 1-2 feet of new snow to higher Sierra elevations with 4-10 inches of snow below 7000 feet across the Tahoe Basin and slightly higher amounts in Mono County. Northeast California received several inches of accumulating snow while western Nevada valleys generally received only some amount less than an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Heavenly Ski Base and Homewood Mountain Ski Base reported 24 hour total snowfall of 12.0 and 8.0 inches respectively.

Northeast California received several inches of accumulating snow while western Nevada valleys generally received only some amount less than an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the June Mountain Ski base and the Mammoth Mountain Ski base reported 24 hour total snowfall of 18.0 and 16.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a pole downed with power lines across the roadway at the intersection of E. Mission Ave. and S. Arboled Ln.

The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report that the right lane of eastbound SR 198 at the SR 99 exchange is completely flooded with 8 feet of water into the lane.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Joaquin Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of mud, dirt and rock slide blocking northbound lane of Tollhouse Rd. 2 miles south of Beal Fire Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on northbound SR 99 and the W. Cleveland Ave. offramp.

The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Burnt Corral SNOTEL (9700 feet) picked up and estimated 20 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported  a tree washed up on the roadway on eastbound SR 140 east of Guadalupe Fire Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occurred across northern San Diego County on January 15-16, resulting in flooding near Rainbow. County officials announced the closure of Pala Mission Road near Highway 76, and Pala Temecula Road. Rainfall of 1 to 1.5 inch had been reported leading up to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across the San Joaquin Valley during the early morning of January 6  behind the strong winter storm which brought moderate to heavy rainfall to much of the area on January 4 and 5. In addition, diminishing winds made conditions favorable for areas of dense fog to form.  The fog was most noticeable around Hanford where visibility was reduced to below 1000 feet and webcams in he area showed reduced visibility for much of the night through late morning when the fog dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several webcams along SR 198 indicated visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a few feet of mud on part of SR 41 near Tenaya Lodge in Oakhurst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope low clouds formed along the east side of the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra Foothills in Fresno and Tulare Counties in the wake of a cold winter storm which brought some light precipitation to the area during the morning of January 30. The low clouds lowered into a fog layer by sunrise which disrupted the morning commute in the after mentioned areas. Some dense fog also formed as a result of upslope along the valley facing slopes of the Tehachapi Mountains during the afternoon. The fog dissipated by the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports posted on Facebook and Twitter showing visibility below 1000 feet on SR 58 west of Tehachapi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January 4th through the 5th, a medium-strength winter storm brought 1-2 feet of new snow to higher Sierra elevations with 4-10 inches of snow below 7000 feet across the Tahoe Basin and slightly higher amounts in Mono County. Northeast California received several inches of accumulating snow while western Nevada valleys generally received only some amount less than an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 7.0 mi W of Truckee, CA at the Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 48 hour total snowfall of 9.8 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported power to traffic signals at the intersection of N. Chester Ave and E. Norris Rd. in Oildale was knocked out by wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Panoche Road RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of flooding and potholes on the offramp of SR 41 onto Shields Ave. in Fresno.

Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report that Leonard Ave. was flooded just north of Alluvial Ave. east of Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported Rd. 236 was closed between Ave. 196 and Ave. 200 in Strathmore due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding along W. Sandy Mush Rd. near S. Gurr Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported water flowing across Cole Grade Road from the San Luis Rey River. Rainfall of 8 to 11 inches had been received in the 72 hours prior to the flood report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak atmospheric river brings heavy rain to Orange, northern Riverside and San Bernardino Counties. San Bernardino County saw the heaviest rainfall, with reports of 4 to 10 inches. Multiple reports of flooding were received with several swiftwater rescues. Breezy winds occurred as well from the evening of January 9 through January 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Palm Springs reported the closure of North Indian Canyon Drive at the wash due to flooding. The Palm Springs Fire Department conducted a swift water rescue, with at least one person rescued from the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cottonwood Creek measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass build into the area during the morning of January 31. Although low clouds and fog kept minimum temperatures up across much of the southern portion of the San Joaquin Valley, nearly all of Merced County and much of the west side of the valley had minimum temperatures drop below the freeing mark for a few hours during the late night and morning of January 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 31 degrees on January  31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a pole was blown down across Ave. 25 near the intersection of Rd. 9 in Madera County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of flooding on the eastbound SR 198 offramp onto Mooney Blvd. in Visalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Pelato Peak measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a washed up tree in the roadway with a large branch blocking the eastbound lanes of SR 198 just west of Mehrten Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported Rd. 184 was closed between Ave. 24 to Ave. 32 due to flooding.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beach Meadows SNOTEL (7650 feet) picked up and estimated 23 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported standing water on SR 99 near Cecil Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems in close succession brought very heavy snowfall to the San Bernardino County Mountains, with the first occurring along a cold front late on January 14 into the morning hours of January 15, with the second system close on its heels, producing additional snowfall late on January 15 to early on January 17. The first system produced light to locally moderate snowfall, with 4 inches of snow reported near Green Valley Lake, and up to 6 inches of snow near Big Bear with blowing snow also reported.||The second, colder, system produced very heavy snowfall, leading to impressive multi-day snow accumulations:|Broadcast media reported 20-28 inches of snow accumulation at 7,000-8,000 feet at Snow Valley.|Broadcast media reported 21 inches of snow accumulation at 7,100 feet at Bear Mountain Snow Summit.|The Department of Highways reported 16 inches of snow accumulation at 6,900 feet near Running Springs.|The Department of Highways reported 13 inches of snow accumulation at 6,900 feet at Barton Flats.|The Department of Highways reported 12 inches of snow accumulation at 6,800 feet at the Big Bear Yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm impacted Southern California January 30th through the 31st. A closed upper level low pressure system led to widespread rain in the coasts and valleys as well as a few inches of snow in the mountains, with the heaviest snow in the San Bernardino Mountains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger two-part storm aided by an enhanced influx of subtropical moisture from a moderate atmospheric river continued the current series of Pacific winter storms passing through the region January 14-16. The initial thrust of AR enhanced moisture with the first storm brought heavy snowfall to the Sierra on 14 January with 2 to 3 feet of accumulating snow across higher elevations above 7000 feet.  An additional 15-30 inches of new snow fell across the California side of the Lake Tahoe Basin below 7000 feet with 8-16 inches falling on the Nevada side. Periods of heavy snow forced the closure of Sierra passes with the primary Sierra travel routes (I-80, US-50) being closed for much of the day. Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS station (CA-MN-6) located 1.9 mi SW of Mono City, CA, reported a 24 hour snowfall amount  of 8.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported rocks washed up on the roadway at the intersection of N. Piedra Rd. and Elwood Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass build into the area during the morning of January 31. Although low clouds and fog kept minimum temperatures up across much of the southern portion of the San Joaquin Valley, nearly all of Merced County and much of the west side of the valley had minimum temperatures drop below the freeing mark for a few hours during the late night and morning of January 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 31 degrees on January  31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As skies cleared out over the center portion of the San Joaquin Valley with light winds prevailing, an area of dense fog reducing visibility to a few hundred feet formed along a strip from Tulare northward to Madera. The fog impacted travel along SR 99 during the morning commute and resulted in several school districts either opening late or delaying or cancelling bus service. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Madera. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The series of atmospheric rivers continued across Southern California in early January of 2023. Widespread rain and high-elevation snow occurred, leading to several instances of rock and mud slides, as well as a swiftwater rescue near Palm Springs. Very high surf and coastal flooding occurred across Orange and San Diego County Beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf was reported at Orange County Beaches, with tide overflowing onto the Pacific Coast Highway. Elevated to high surf of 5 to 9 feet was reported by early afternoon on January 5, with sets of 8 to 12 feet reported on the afternoon on January 6. Surf slowly diminished through the day on January 7.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf was reported at San Diego County Beaches, with high tide resulting in the closure of the Ocean Beach Pier. Elevated to high surf of 4 to 8 feet was reported by early afternoon on January 5, with sets of 8 to 12 feet reported in the morning on January 6. Surf slowly diminished through the day on January 7.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swiftwater rescue of two vehicles stuck in wash on North Indian Canyon Drive near Sunrise Parkway. Whitewater and Morongo Canyon (Whitewater Trout Farm) and Lower Snow Creek received 0.5-1 inch from 6am-12pm. Video of vehicles in lower water crossing submerged in flowing water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate rainfall occurred across the San Bernardino County Mountains on January 5, from the early morning through the mid-afternoon hours. Multiple instances of debris flows were recorded, with mud and large rocks/boulders covering portions of Highway 330 and large boulders on Highway 38. Often, these were the result of the repeated freeze, snow, thaw and rain in the mountains this winter, with small amounts of rain then causing the movement of boulders.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree fell across Fairmont Ave near the intersection of Arcade Ave. in Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Yosemite 49 measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Miami Mountain measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of roadway flooding in the eastbound lanes of SR 140 near Franklin Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on Healy Rd. at McMaster Rd.
