EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 0.85 inches of rainfall measured in one hour accompanied by 0.25 inch hail by the Bear Creek at McKee Rd. River Gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific storm systems produced widespread heavy rainfall across the San Diego metro area, producing rainfall totals of 3-5 inches near Fashion Valley from January 14-17, with the heaviest occurring the morning of January 15- the morning of January 16. The San Diego River reached 12.4-12.7 feet at Fashion Valley.||Every cross street in Mission Valley along the San Diego River was flooded, including toward the San Diego Trolley. Three vehicles were abandoned and under 2-3 feet of flowing water on Camino Del Este, Qualcomm Way and Fashion Valley Road. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported rocks washed up on the road at the intersection of SR 198 and SR 216 in Lemoncove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As skies cleared out over the center portion of the San Joaquin Valley with light winds prevailing, an area of dense fog reducing visibility to a few hundred feet formed along a strip from Tulare northward to Madera. The fog impacted travel along SR 99 during the morning commute and resulted in several school districts either opening late or delaying or cancelling bus service. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet. Several school districts opened late because of dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The series of atmospheric rivers continued across Southern California in early January of 2023. Widespread rain and high-elevation snow occurred, leading to several instances of rock and mud slides, as well as a swiftwater rescue near Palm Springs. Very high surf and coastal flooding occurred across Orange and San Diego County Beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High-elevation snowfall occurred across the San Bernardino County Mountains from early January 5-January 6. Snow levels generally remained above 7,000 feet, but several inches of accumulation were noted as low as 6,000 feet. Snow amounts of 2-8 inches were received above 7,000 feet, with accumulations of 2-4 inches as low as 6,000 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred across the San Bernardino County Mountains late January 4 through the morning of January 5. Widespread wind gusts of 35-55 mph were recorded, with several notable gusts listed below:|Mesonet station 424SCE Silverwood Lake reported a peak wind gust of 72 mph.|Burns Canyon reported a peak wind gust of 72 mph.|Mesonet station Cajon Lookout SCE338 reported a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported power lines down across the roadway on August Ave. near the intersection of Hinton Ave. in Delhi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Yosemite Pilot Peak measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported Miles Creek was flooding the roadway and SR 140 was closed to the east of the Merced/Mariposa County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of roadway flooding at the at Jones Creek near the intersection of Triangle Rd. and Carter Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree washed up on the roadway on the northbound lane of Locan Ln. near Paul Ave.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Joaquin Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 79 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the San Diego County coastal areas through the early morning hours on January 16. Numerous trees were reported downed across the metro area:||A large pine tree fell on apartments on Marathon Drive in Mission Valley; some units were tagged as residents were unable to return.||A tree fell at 4059 Newton Ave. No injuries were reported, but residents were evacuated from the building.||A tree was downed into the roadway at Fortuna Ave and Ingraham St in Pacific Beach.||A large tree was downed at Archer and Cass St. Power outages of 2000 reported by SDG&E for Bonita and Pacific Beach.||A large tree landed on a vehicle at Playa Del Sur off of La Jolla Blvd.||A tree was reported down and blocking the road at 16292 Rambla De Las Flores.||Downed trees onto powerlines resulted in a road closure in Vista.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dry upper high pressure ridge built into California on January 20. With a cool northerly flow aloft and mainly clear skies and light winds prevailing over the San Joaquin |Valley, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for a few hours across the rural areas during the mornings of January 21, 22, 23 and 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on January 21, 22, 23 and 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds prevailed over the San Joaquin Valley underneath high pressure during the morning of January 21. As a result, dense fog formed for a few hours across the central portion of the valley which impacted travel on SR 99 and SR 198. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree blocking the roadway on Rd. 426 near Rd. 222. at Bass Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported  at tree blocking the southbound lanes of Golden State Blvd. near the intersection of Jefferson Ave. in Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catheys Valley RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a washed up tree covering half of Lacey Blvd just west of 8th Ave.

Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of roadway flooding a the intersection of Taft Hwy. and Panama Rd in Bakersfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree washed up on the roadway at Rd. 140 near Ave. 192.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS SOUTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Las Yeguas measured a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the San Diego County Valleys the evening of January 14. The Department of Highways reported a downed tree, which took down cable wires and partially blocked vehicles on Eucalyptus Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm impacted Southern California January 30th through the 31st. A closed upper level low pressure system led to widespread rain in the coasts and valleys as well as a few inches of snow in the mountains, with the heaviest snow in the San Bernardino Mountains. A few thunderstorms also occurred over the San Diego County Valleys, enhancing rainfall in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain with a few embedded thunderstorms occurred mainly over the San Diego County Valleys on January 30th, generally between the hours of 11 AM and 6 PM. Rainfall totals of 0.50-1.00 inch were common, with a few localized areas near San Marcos and Escondido receiving about 1.10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong Satna Ana winds impacted all of Southern California the morning of January 26. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were reported across the coast, with wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph in the valleys and gusts over 80 mph in the mountains. Major interstate closures, including I-8 in San Diego County, and I-15 and I-215 in San Bernardino County, occurred as the result of overturned semi trucks and fallen trees and powerlines. Dozens of trees were downed and snapped in Balboa Park, including one that fell on and injured a woman, resulting in the closure of the park for the remainder of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the San Bernardino County Mountains through much of the morning on January 26. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph occurred, especially from 7 AM through 11 AM. The following are the strongest wind gusts recorded that morning:||Arrowhead Springs recorded a peak wind gust of 83 mph.|Marshall Peak recorded a peak wind gust of 80 mph.|Running Springs recorded a peak wind gust of 72 mph.|Lytle Creek South recorded a peak wind gust of 64 mph.|San Sevaine Flats recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph.||Multiple reports of damage were reported, including a downed tree that brought powerlines down blocking a roadway; an electric pole broken in half; a semi truck with a 53-foot trailer flipped, blocking northbound I-15 at the Devore offramp; and an overturned big rig blocking all northbound lanes of I-215, resulting in a complete shutdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure remained situated over California during the morning of January 22. Mainly clear skies and light winds prevailing over the San Joaquin Valley along with lingering moisture from recent storms made for an ideal situation for widespread dense fog to form in the valley during the early morning hours and persist until late morning when the fog dissipated. Several webcams in the valley showed visibility below 1000 feet and travel along SR 99 was impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater, Merced and Madera. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on Buchanan Hallow Rd. south of LeGrand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large rockslide blocking the roadway on SR 41 southwest of the intersection with SR 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree on the roadway on Rd. 200 west of Mountain View Peak Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding on Seminole Ave. near Delaware Rd. caused the roadway to collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Tejon Hills measured a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Lake Elsinore, resulting in structure damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was rescued by San Diego lifeguard after being stranded in her vehicle in four feet of water near the Tijuana River. 7 inches of rain had been reported at Otay Mountain in the preceding 3 days, with 4.2 inches of rain in Bonita.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down across roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge of high pressure persisted near the California coast during the morning of January 25. With clear skies and winds prevailing across the San Joaquin Valley, fog formed along the central portion of the valley with visibility being reduced to below a quarter mile along the SR 43 corridor between Selma and Corcoran with travel being impacted along SR 43 as well as along a portion of SR 198 from Hanford eastward to the Kings-Tulare County border. The fog completely dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 4th to the 5th, a strong atmospheric river combined with a bomb cyclone led to widespread heavy rain, damaging wind gusts, and high surf across the Bay Area and Central Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eucalyptus tree fell onto an apartment building on Lynde Street in Oakland, forcing the evacuation of five families. The building was later red tagged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree blocking part of the intersection of SR 168 and Tollhouse Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree down at the intersection of Fairfax Rd. and College Ave. in Bakersfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree in the roadway in north Merced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding near the intersection of Ave. 168 and Rd. 166 in Woodville.

Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green Mountain SNOTEL (7900 feet) picked up an estimated 25 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Postpile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 31 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported the closure of Snelling Rd. north of La Paloma Rd. due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Bear Valley Springs measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds impacted the San Diego County Mountains from the morning on January 15 into early January 16. Two notable peaks in the winds occurred, with the first occurring near sunrise on January 15; the following are the peak gust reported from January 15:||Laguna Launch (SDHGPA) reported a peak wind gust of 75 mph.|Volcan Mountain reported a peak wind gust of 66 mph.||The second peak occurred before dawn on the 16th. The following are some of the top wind gust reports from the second surge event:||Palomar Mountain Lookout reported a peak wind gust of 74 mph.|Boucher Hill reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.|Harrison Park reported a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retaining wall collapsed in Corona around 4:30 AM on January 16 resulting in twenty-four residents who had to evacuate their homes in nearby areas. One home was damaged immediately below the wall, and cracked the foundation above. Broadcast media reported that concrete blocks crashed down into adjacent properties, burying backyards and patios, and smashing through glass doors. Corona has received 1.5 to 3 inches of rain in the preceding 48 hours before the collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a high profile vehicle was damaged by a fallen tree at the intersection of SR 58 and Tower Line Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree in the roadway on E. Gettysburg Ave. at N. College Ave. in Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a tree downed across the roadway on S. Fork Dr. in Three Rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through central California on January 9 and 10. This system picked up a deep moisture fetch of tropical origin while tracking rapidly eastward across the Pacific and produced widespread heavy precipitation across the area between the morning of January 9 and the evening of January 10 while the deep surge of moisture moved inland across central California. This storm produced widespread liquid precipitation totals of 3 to 6 inches in the Sierra Nevada. For much of this event, the snow level was around 7500 feet which resulted in heavy runoff of snow accumulations from recent colder storms. This runoff combined with rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in the Sierra foothills and 1 to 2.5 inches in the San Joaquin Valley to produce widespread flooding across the area with at least 150 separate incidents of flooding being reported to the Hanford NWS office. The flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed and several evacuations took place including the entire town of Planada in Merced County which was inundated with flood waters. In addition, Bear Creek overflowed it's banks and reached a new record peak flood of 26.2 feet at McKee Bridge east of Merced on January 10. Above the snow line, there were several reports of 2 to 4 feet of new snowfall above 7500 feet in the Sierra Nevada. This system also produced strong downslope winds on January 9 with several stations in the Tehachapi Mountains, West Side Hills and Sierra foothills measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph and resulting in several power outages across the area as power lines were blown down by the strong winds. Thunderstorms broke out across the much of the area on January 10 as colder air aloft moved over the area resulting in numerous issuances of Significant Weather Advisories along with a few warnings. The storm moved to the east of the area on January 11 with precipitation ending and winds diminishing. However, flooding continued across much of the area due to residual runoff from the heavy precipitation that fell over the previous two days. Several county Emergency Operations Centers were staffed by NWS personnel during this event do to the unusual severity with this storm as numerous warnings were issued for flooding, strong winds and heavy mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Volcanic Knob SNOTEL (10050 feet) picked up an estimated 36 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong low pressure system impacted central California as it moved eastward across northern California on January 14. The deeper moisture associated with this system arrived during the evening on January 13 and the precipitation continued across the area until the evening of January 14 when it finally tapered off. Strong upper jet dynamics associated with this system produced a period of widespread heavy precipitation across the area during the afternoon of January 14. Between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation fell over the Sierra Nevada while 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell over the adjacent foothills. Most of the San Joaquin Valley only picked up between a quarter of an inch and three quarters of an inch of rain due to rain shadowing effects. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall above 6000 feet with a few inches of snow falling as low as 4500 feet in the Sierra Nevada as cooler air pushed into the area during the evening of January 14. The main impacts from this system came from flooding which was a result of significant rainfall taking place over already saturated soil and continued runoff from previous storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding along SR 14 near Redrock-Randsburg Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm that brought widespread precipitation and additional flooding to the area on January 14 was followed by another storm which moved through the area between the afternoon of January 15 and the afternoon of January 16. While precipitation amounts from this storm were significantly less across most of the area than with the previous two storms which impacted the area over the previous week, this system produced a period of damaging wind gusts in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 16 as the cold front from this system pushed through the area. There were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 40 mph in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills and wind gusts exceeding 60 mph in the Tehachapi Mountains and the West Side Hills. The strong winds resulted in numerous power outages as power poles and power lines were toppled as well as road closures as the winds brought trees down across roads. Some more flooding occurred in the area as a result of additional rainfall on soil which had been heavily saturated by previous recent storms. The winds did taper off by the afternoon of January 16, but the precipitation was very slow to taper off as the storm didn't completely move out of the area until January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Tejon Ranch-Sebastian Road measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper tough dropped southward into the Great Basin on January 22 and January 23. This trough interacted with high pressure centered offshore which extended into California to produce and offshore gradient which resulted in strong northeast winds over the mountains and deserts of Kern County. Several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph with some low impact indicator sites reporting gusts in excess on 60 mph. The winds diminished by the afternoon of January 23 as the trough moved east and gradients relaxed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of atmospheric rivers impact Southern California January 14-16. Widespread heavy rainfall occurred in back-to-back two waves, with the first occurring the afternoon of January 14 into the early morning hours on January 15, and the second occurring the night of January 15 through January 16.||The first storm system resulted in numerous reports of flooding in San Diego County, Orange County and Riverside County and high-elevation snow in the mountains of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches for the coast, 1 to 2.5 inches in the valleys, 2 to 5 inches in the mountains and up to one-half of an inch in the deserts.||The second, colder system again produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, with the heaviest rain focusing on San Diego County. Many reports of flooding were received in San Diego County, with numerous swiftwater rescues performed, with 19 alone by San Diego City. The San Diego River overflowed, with the highest impacts reported in Fashion Valley on January 16, with a dozen swiftwater rescues performed before dawn. Nearby areas in Hotel Circle were evacuated due to the threat of flooding.||Impressive precipitation amounts were reported from the two back-to-back system, with 2 to 4 inches of rain received at the coast, 2 to 5 inches for the inland valley and 3 to 8+ inches for the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain fell from late morning on January 14 through much of the afternoon. Rain accumulations of 1.25 inch to 2 inches were common across inland Orange County, resulting in widespread flooding reports. The following are reports of flooding from the Department of Highways.||Both lanes of the I-5 north offramp was 4th Ave was flooded in Santa Ana. Four vehicles were stuck in the flood waters.||Farther south, three vehicles were stuck in the flooded onramp to northbound I-5 at Crown Valley Pkwy in Laguna Niguel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful atmospheric river brought heavy rainfall, widespread flooding and gusty winds to the area. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2-6 inches across the coastal and valley areas with 6-16 inches across the mountains and foothills. With the heavy rainfall, widespread flooding, especially across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties. Strong southerly winds, gusting up to 94 MPH, were reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding reported near Dolliver and Highway 1 intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system brought moderate to heavy precipitation, widespread flooding and strong winds to the area on January 4 and 5. This system had an atmospheric river associated with it which contained a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin resulting in widespread moderate to heavy precipitation across the area with the snow level ranging between 7000 and 8000 feet for much of the event. Several stations above 8000 feet picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall from this storm. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up an inch and a half to three inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley received around a half inch to an inch from Fresno County northward and a quarter to a half inch further southward. This rainfall came on top of already saturated soils from the late December storms resulting in widespread nuisance flooding across the area. The Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while rain shadowing effect limited rainfall in the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less at most stations. This system also produced very strong winds across much of the area with several stations in the Kern County Mountains and West Side hills measuring gusts in excess of 60 mph with several ridge top stations in Kern County reporting peak gusts in excess of 75 mph. Several reports of wind damage were received by WFO Hanford during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a power line pole and lines down in the northbound lanes of Edison Rd. near the intersection of Hermosa Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system which moved through northern California brought another round of light to moderate precipitation along with strong winds gusts to the between the evening of January 7 and the early afternoon of January 8. While the strong winds were not as prevalent as they were during the January 4 and 5 storm, several stations did measure brief gusts exceeding 60 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally not as significant as they had been with the previous storm, but a half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation was received Sierra Nevada near Yosemite Park with a quarter to a half inch further south between Yosemite Park and Kings Canyon. Amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less south of Kings Canyon. The snow level was around 5000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation stations in Yosemite Park picking up between 5 and 10 inches of new snowfall. Rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was generally aa quarter to a half an inch north of Fresno with a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Much of the south end of the valley in Kern County picked up a tenth of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of low hanging wires over Huntsman Ave. near the Bethel Ave. exchange in Fresno.
