EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of atmospheric river systems impacted Interior Northern California and produced substantial flooding and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tehama County completed a preliminary estimate of damage from flooding. There was substantial flooding, with a rough estimate of $14 million in damages, mainly from 3 bridges, 1 completely destroyed by under cutting, two others under cut and sink holes, but still standing. Kirkwood Bridge $6,110,000.00 , Rawson Bridge $4,000,000.00. Remainder in trees, bridge clearing, road damage, labor $4,000,000.00||The city of Red Bluff estimated $60,000.00 bridge, trees, sandbags, labor (still climbing as they find more). The city of Corning estimated $100,000.00 road damage, creek and bridge clearing and labor. The city of Tehama estimated $27,000.00 road damage to East Gyle, tree clean up and debris clean up from roads.

SR 99 between Dillard Rd and Grantline Rd was flooded with numerous vehicles stuck in flood waters, requiring water rescues and was closed around midnight due to flooding. Flooding continued well into January. Sacramento County issued an evacuation warning overnight for the Wilton area, changed to a shelter-in-place warning a couple of hours later due to unsafe travel conditions with surrounding flooded areas. There were an estimated 3,200 to 3,500 people in the impacted area. There were three drownings attributed to the flooding, each entered as an individual event.

There was flooding of roadways, urban areas, rivers, streams, and creeks. There were dangerous mountain travel conditions at Sierra pass levels with 6 to 18 inches of snow above 6500 feet. The warm nature of the storm limited snow amounts further down, with just a few inches of accumulation down to 6000 feet. Winds gusted up to 50-60 mph in the Valley, with gusts up to 70 to 100 mph in the mountains. There were widespread trees down, blocking roads and causing numerous power outages across the area. Rainfall amounts were 1 to 3 inches in the Central Valley and 2 to 6 inches in the foothills and mountains. There were 2 fatalities reported, one due to drowning, and the other due to a rain-related car accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On SR 32 near Platt Mountain Road, a very large tree was reported in the roadway blocking both lanes. Oroville Airport reported a gust from the south of 33 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds impacted mountains of Southern California. Peak gusts reached 95 MPH in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds impacted the mountains of Los Angeles county. Some peak wind gusts from the local mesonet include: Magic Mountain Truck Trail (gust 95 MPH), Barley Flats (gust 71 MPH), Sierra Pelona (gust 70 MPH) and Camp Nine RAWS (gust 62 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought strong winds, moderate to heavy rainfall and high elevation snowfall to the area. Overall, rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches across coastal and valley areas with 4 to 8 inches across the foothills and mountains. Rainfall intensities were moderate, resulting in widespread nuisance urban flooding. Snow levels remained very high, above 6000 feet, with the resort level receiving 8-16 inches of snowfall. Along with the rain and snow, gusty southerly winds were reported with gusts up to 87 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. Snow levels remained above 6000 feet and resort level elevations (7000+ feet) reported between 8 and 16 inches of snowfall. Additionally, very strong and gusty winds developed. Some peak wind gusts include: Magic Mountain Truck Trail (gust 97 MPH), Yucca Circle (gust 75 MPH) and Sandberg (gust 59 MPH).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds impacted the Santa Lucia Mountains in San Luis Obispo county. Some peak wind gusts from the local mesonet include: TV Towers (gust 63 MPH), Davis Peak (gust 62 MPH), Green Valley Road (gust 59 MPH) and High School Hill (gust 58 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought strong winds with moderate to heavy rain bringing renewed flooding of already elevated waterways. There was flooding of roadways, urban areas, rivers, streams and creeks, with rockslides and mudslides also reported. There were dangerous mountain travel conditions with snow levels around 4000 feet and 3 to 5 feet of storm total snowfall above 6000 feet.  Winds gusted up to 60-70 mph in the Central Valley. In the mountains there were gusts up to 80 to 115 mph, which coupled with heavy snow, brought whiteout conditions. There were widespread trees down across the area, blocking roads and causing numerous power outages. There were thunderstorms on the 10th with a tornado in Calaveras County and straight line wind damage and small hail reported in the northern San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down across SR120. There was a gust to 42 mph measured at Modesto Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From January 7th through 8th, a weaker but quick-hitting winter system brought another 5-10 inches of new snow to higher Sierra elevations with several more inches falling below 7000 feet around the Lake Tahoe Basin. A mix of light rain-snow fell across western Nevada valleys, limiting snow accumulations to higher elevation areas. Rain shadowing and limited spillover across western Nevada allowed strong winds to develop during the early morning hours of January 8th which included a 98 mph gust at Galena Creek Bridge and a 78 mph gust at Walker Lake, NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site (HVRTM) Blue Streak Weather station located 1.0 mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 96 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the southwest on the 1st, dragging a cold front from west to east. Along with some light rainfall activity, strong winds were also observed, mainly behind the cold front as it moved through during the late morning hours, with the strongest winds observed during the afternoon hours. The strongest winds were observed across portions of Imperial County, where gusts over 60 mph were measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was measured at El Centro Naval Facility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inslide slider storm system dove across the Western US on February 14th and 15th. This led to high winds occurring over much of the mountains and desert foothills, as well as caused snow levels to fall to around 3000-4000 feet in Southern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred across the San Gorgonio Pass mainly during the morning and early afternoon hours on February 14th. Gusts of 55-65 mph were common, along with some localized higher gusts. The Whitewater RAWS site recorded a peak wind gust to 77 mph, and the Snowcreek Canyon SoCal-Edison site recorded a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred across the San Diego County Mountains mainly during the morning and early afternoon hours on February 14th. Gusts of 55-70 mph were common, along with some localized higher gusts. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger two-part storm aided by an enhanced influx of subtropical moisture from a moderate atmospheric river continued the current series of Pacific winter storms passing through the region January 14-16. The initial thrust of AR enhanced moisture with the first storm brought heavy snowfall to the Sierra on 14 January with 2 to 3 feet of accumulating snow across higher elevations above 7000 feet.  An additional 15-30 inches of new snow fell across the California side of the Lake Tahoe Basin below 7000 feet with 8-16 inches falling on the Nevada side. Periods of heavy snow forced the closure of Sierra passes with the primary Sierra travel routes (I-80, US-50) being closed for much of the day. Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COCORAHS station (CA-MN-6) located  1.9 mi SW of Mono City, as well as the summits of the June Mountain Ski Base, CA, and Mammoth Mountain Ski Base, CA, reported 24 hour  snowfall amounts of 13.1, 23, and 36 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a powerful winter storm brought historic amounts of snow to the mountains in Southern California, an avalanche on the north-facing slopes of Mount San Jacinto in Riverside County was observed by nearby travelers. Three feet of snow had fallen at elevations near 8500 feet on the mountain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche on Mount San Jacinto was captured in photos and video by nearby travelers, which were then shared by the Riverside County Mountain Rescue Organization. No other specific impacts are known at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger two-part storm aided by an enhanced influx of subtropical moisture from a moderate atmospheric river continued the current series of Pacific winter storms passing through the region January 14-16. The initial thrust of AR enhanced moisture with the first storm brought heavy snowfall to the Sierra on 14 January with 2 to 3 feet of accumulating snow across higher elevations above 7000 feet.  An additional 15-30 inches of new snow fell across the California side of the Lake Tahoe Basin below 7000 feet with 8-16 inches falling on the Nevada side. Periods of heavy snow forced the closure of Sierra passes with the primary Sierra travel routes (I-80, US-50) being closed for much of the day. Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located at the Homewood Mountain Ski Base, CA, reported 24 hour snow amount of 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric storm brought moderate to heavy rain and a period of strong winds New Years Eve into early New Years Day, along with heavy high-elevation Sierra snow. There were 3 fatalities reported due to drowning. Precipitation totals by 8 am (over 24 hours) were around 1 to 3 inches for the southern Sacramento and northern San Joaquin Valleys, 3 to 8 inches for the Motherlode foothills and Northern Sierra from around SR 70 southward, with widespread flooding in those areas. Rainfall further north was generally an inch or less. There was significant river flooding on the Cosumnes River in Sacramento County around Wilton, due to multiple levee breaks. The river reached flood stage on the morning of December 31, and stayed in flood stage past midnight and into January 1. The flooding resulted in the evacuation of Wilton as the river went out-of-bank, with area highways and roads being closed, including SR-99. Significant flooding occurred from Wilton downstream through Interstate 5. River flooding was also reported along the Mokelumne River near Benson's Ferry and Mormon Slough at Bellota. Flooding of streams, in low areas and from clogged storm drains also closed many other roads across Northern California, with several small communities being evacuated. These events occurred mainly on December 31st and were reported on that date. Heavy snow disrupted mountain travel, with a total of 20 to 40 inches of snow above 6000 feet by the morning of the 1st. There were multiple spinouts causing Interstate 80 to be shut down for 18 hours. Highway 50 was closed in El Dorado County due to a combination of flooding and snow. High winds gusting to 50-65 mph in the Central Valley caused large numbers of trees to fall, bringing widespread power failures across the area, with hundreds of thousands of customers impacted. Most of the strong winds occurred on New Years Eve, with the damage events being reported on December 31, 2022, but power outage issues continued for tens of thousands of customers into the new year. Many local roads were also closed to downed trees blocking them, as well as many trees were reported to have fallen on homes and automobiles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sacramento County Sheriff confirmed a drowning on January 4 associated with the December 31 - January 1 flash flood event. A female body was found inside a vehicle. This was the third drowning associated with the event. This flooding was due to levee breaks on the Cosumnes River.

There were dangerous mountain travel conditions with snow levels around 4000 feet and 3 to 5 feet of storm total snowfall above 6000 feet.  Winds gusted up to 60-70 mph in the Central Valley. In the mountains there were gusts up to 80 to 115 mph, which coupled with heavy snow, brought whiteout conditions. There were widespread trees down across the area, blocking roads and causing numerous power outages. There were thunderstorms on the 10th with a tornado in Calaveras County and straight line wind damage and small hail reported in the northern San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans District 3 reported SR16 in Yolo County closed at the SR20 junction due to mudslides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 13th, a weaker winter storm system produced periods of moderate snow across the Sierra above 7000 feet with snowfall totals of 10-20 inches near the Sierra crest, and 4-10 inches below 7000 feet for the Carson Range and the Tahoe Basin respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the summit of Homewood Mountain Ski Base reported 24 hour snowfall of 12 inches.  The public located 1.0 mi SSW of Northstar Ski Base and the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base both reported 24 hour total snowfall of 14.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger two-part storm aided by an enhanced influx of subtropical moisture from a moderate atmospheric river continued the current series of Pacific winter storms passing through the region January 14-16. The initial thrust of AR enhanced moisture with the first storm brought heavy snowfall to the Sierra on 14 January with 2 to 3 feet of accumulating snow across higher elevations above 7000 feet.  An additional 15-30 inches of new snow fell across the California side of the Lake Tahoe Basin below 7000 feet with 8-16 inches falling on the Nevada side. Periods of heavy snow forced the closure of Sierra passes with the primary Sierra travel routes (I-80, US-50) being closed for much of the day. Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 1.0 mi SSW of the Northstar Ski Base, CA, as well as the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, CA, Heavenly Ski base, CA, and Homewood Mountain Ski Base, CA,  reported 24 hour upper summit snow amounts of 17.0, 21.0, 23.0, and 25.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From January 7th through 8th, a weaker but quick-hitting winter system brought another 5-10 inches of new snow to higher Sierra elevations with several more inches falling below 7000 feet around the Lake Tahoe Basin. A mix of light rain-snow fell across western Nevada valleys, limiting snow accumulations to higher elevation areas. Rain shadowing and limited spillover across western Nevada allowed strong winds to develop during the early morning hours of January 8th which included a 98 mph gust at Galena Creek Bridge and a 78 mph gust at Walker Lake, NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH24) Woollys located 2.0 mi E of the Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, CA, reported a maximum wind gusts of 115.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful atmospheric river brought heavy rainfall, widespread flooding and gusty winds to the area. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2-6 inches across the coastal and valley areas with 6-16 inches across the mountains and foothills. With the heavy rainfall, widespread flooding, especially across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties. Strong southerly winds, gusting up to 94 MPH, were reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding along Highway 46 near Painted Cave Road. The flash flooding resulted in a mudslide across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful atmospheric river brought very strong winds with moderate to heavy rain and renewed flooding of the already elevated waterways. There was flooding of roadways, urban areas, rivers, streams, and creeks. There were dangerous mountain travel conditions at Sierra pass levels with 6 to 18 inches of snow above 6500 feet. The warm nature of the storm limited snow amounts further down, with just a few inches of accumulation down to 6000 feet. Winds gusted up to 50-60 mph in the Valley, with gusts up to 70 to 100 mph in the mountains. There were widespread trees down, blocking roads and causing numerous power outages across the area. Rainfall amounts were 1 to 3 inches in the Central Valley and 2 to 6 inches in the foothills and mountains. There were 2 fatalities reported, one due to drowning, and the other due to a rain-related car accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closure at Southgate Dr. due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rain, heavy snow and strong winds to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches across coastal and valleys areas with 4 to 10 inches across the foothills and mountains. Snow levels dropped down, resulting in significant snowfall across the mountains. Below 5000 feet, 6 to 12 inches of snow was reported while the resort level (greater than 6000 feet) received 2 to 5 feet of snowfall. With the snowfall and strong winds, a Blizzard Warning was issues for the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. As for winds, peak gusts of 60 to 80 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding on Highway 150 at the intersection of Santa Ana Road near Lake Casitas. A couple of vehicles were stuck in the flood waters.

The warm nature of the storm limited snow amounts further down, with just a few inches of accumulation down to 6000 feet. Winds gusted up to 50-60 mph in the Valley, with gusts up to 70 to 100 mph in the mountains. There were widespread trees down, blocking roads and causing numerous power outages across the area. Rainfall amounts were 1 to 3 inches in the Central Valley and 2 to 6 inches in the foothills and mountains. There were 2 fatalities reported, one due to drowning, and the other due to a rain-related car accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding was reported on northbound Interstate 5 near Benjamin Holt Drive by the California Highway Patrol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought strong winds, moderate to heavy rainfall and high elevation snowfall to the area. Overall, rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches across coastal and valley areas with 4 to 8 inches across the foothills and mountains. Rainfall intensities were moderate, resulting in widespread nuisance urban flooding. Snow levels remained very high, above 6000 feet, with the resort level receiving 8-16 inches of snowfall. Along with the rain and snow, gusty southerly winds were reported with gusts up to 87 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds impacted the mountains of San Luis Obispo county. The strongest gusts were recorded at Mount Lowe with southerly gusts peaking at 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought strong winds with moderate to heavy rain bringing renewed flooding of already elevated waterways. There was flooding of roadways, urban areas, rivers, streams and creeks, with rockslides and mudslides also reported. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rain, heavy snow and strong winds to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches across coastal and valleys areas with 4 to 10 inches across the foothills and mountains. Snow levels dropped down, resulting in significant snowfall across the mountains. Below 5000 feet, 6 to 12 inches of snow was reported while the resort level (greater than 6000 feet) received 2 to 5 feet of snowfall. With the snowfall and strong winds, a Blizzard Warning was issues for the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. As for winds, peak gusts of 60 to 80 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall generated flash flooding on Highway 101 near Campo de Cahuenga. All lanes of the 101 experienced significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior Northern California saw extensive heavy rainfall and mountain snow through January which along with above-average precipitation in December brought significant improvement in drought conditions. For the month of January, areas that were in drought severity level D3 (Extreme Drought) were dropped to D2 (Severe Drought).  Areas that were in drought severity level D2 (Severe Drought) were dropped to D1 (Moderate Drought). All of interior Northern California remained in at least moderate drought through the month. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries projected for 2023 have been increased, though, with the California Department of Water Resources increasing projected deliveries to 30 percent of requested water supplies, 1.27 million acre-feet, up from the initial 5 percent announced on December 1, 2022. The state-wide drought declaration by Governor Gavin Newsom continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines for violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have had less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of January, the U.S. Drought Monitor removed all of Zone 13 from extreme drought (D3) and put it into moderate drought (D2) due to significant improvements from heavy precipitation.

They also removed the eastern half of Zone 14 from severe drought (D3) and placed it into the moderate drought (D2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger two-part storm aided by an enhanced influx of subtropical moisture from a moderate atmospheric river continued the current series of Pacific winter storms passing through the region January 14-16. The initial thrust of AR enhanced moisture with the first storm brought heavy snowfall to the Sierra on 14 January with 2 to 3 feet of accumulating snow across higher elevations above 7000 feet.  An additional 15-30 inches of new snow fell across the California side of the Lake Tahoe Basin below 7000 feet with 8-16 inches falling on the Nevada side. Periods of heavy snow forced the closure of Sierra passes with the primary Sierra travel routes (I-80, US-50) being closed for much of the day. Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer (TPZN2) located 8.4 mi NW of the Bridgeport Reservoir, CA and the public located at Mammoth Mountain Ski Base, CA, both reported 24 hour snow amounts of 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm in mid-January brought widespread rain, heavy mountain snow, gusty winds, flooding and significant mountain travel impacts. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful atmospheric river brought very strong winds with moderate to heavy rain and renewed flooding of the already elevated waterways. There was flooding of roadways, urban areas, rivers, streams, and creeks. There were dangerous mountain travel conditions at Sierra pass levels with 6 to 18 inches of snow above 6500 feet. The warm nature of the storm limited snow amounts further down, with just a few inches of accumulation down to 6000 feet. Winds gusted up to 50-60 mph in the Valley, with gusts up to 70 to 100 mph in the mountains. There were widespread trees down, blocking roads and causing numerous power outages across the area. Rainfall amounts were 1 to 3 inches in the Central Valley and 2 to 6 inches in the foothills and mountains. There were 2 fatalities reported, one due to drowning, and the other due to a rain-related car accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The California Highway Patrol reported rocks along the Oroville Quincy Highway due to flooding near Simmons Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior Northern California saw extensive heavy rainfall and mountain snow through January which along with above-average precipitation in December brought significant improvement in drought conditions. For the month of January, areas that were in drought severity level D3 (Extreme Drought) were dropped to D2 (Severe Drought).  Areas that were in drought severity level D2 (Severe Drought) were dropped to D1 (Moderate Drought). All of interior Northern California remained in at least moderate drought through the month. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries projected for 2023 have been increased, though, with the California Department of Water Resources increasing projected deliveries to 30 percent of requested water supplies, 1.27 million acre-feet, up from the initial 5 percent announced on December 1, 2022. The state-wide drought declaration by Governor Gavin Newsom continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines for violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have had less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of January, the U.S. Drought Monitor removed the middle portion of Zone 15 from extreme drought (D3) and placed it into the severe drought (D2) category due to significant improvements from heavy precipitation. They also removed the far eastern and western portions of Zone 15 from severe drought (D2) and placed them into the moderate drought (D1).

US Highway 50 at Echo Summit was also closed for avalanche control. Caltrans also reported closures of other mountain highways due to heavy snow. |Caltrans and area ski resorts reported multiple feet of snow from January 7-12, 2023. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 56.6 inches, Bear Valley resort reported 64 inches, Boreal Ski Resort reported 46 inches, Dodge Ridge Ski Resort reported 60 inches, Kirkwood Ski Resort reported 50 inches, Sierra-at-Tahoe Ski Resort reported 45 inches, Soda Springs Ski Resort reported 46 inches, Sugar Bowl Ski Resort reported 56 inches, and Caltrans reported 48 inches at Kingvale for storm total snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A jogger near the intersection of JFK Drive and 30th Avenue within the Golden Gate Park noticed a woman down next to a fallen tree branch. Firefighters arrived to provide life-saving measures, but said that the victim had died. At the time of the incident, a Special Weather Statement was valid for the area, with wind gusts up to 40 mph and pea sized hail possible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of atmospheric river systems impacted Interior Northern California and produced substantial flooding and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sacramento County completed a preliminary estimate of a total of $133 million from public storm damage from January 1 through January 17, and a total of almost $700,000 in private property damage. There was extensive levee damage and flooding along the Cosumnes River from Rancho Murieta to Wilton. There was also widespread flooding of streams, creeks, and roadways across the county. Large numbers of trees were reported down from strong wind events, with widespread power outages and reports of damage to homes and cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful atmospheric river brought heavy rainfall, widespread flooding and gusty winds to the area. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2-6 inches across the coastal and valley areas with 6-16 inches across the mountains and foothills. With the heavy rainfall, widespread flooding, especially across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties. Strong southerly winds, gusting up to 94 MPH, were reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding along Highway 135 at the intersection of Batchelder Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary westerly upper flow regime yielded multiple atmospheric rivers over Northwest California during the first two weeks of January. Impacts from these events were widespread, both in terms of flooding and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down blocking portions of HWY 101 near Big Lagoon to near Patrick's Point. Additional trees down on secondary roads. Wind speeds estimated based on tree damage including trees completely snapped at the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper low pressure system moved eastward across northern California on February 5 with precipitation from this system extending southward into central California between the evening of February 4 and the evening of February 5. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada with the snow level running around 6500 feet during the evening of February 4 lowering to around 4000 feet by time the precipitation tapered off during the evening of February 5. SNOTEL estimates indicated between 9 and 15 inches of new snow fell above 6500 feet from Fresno County northward. Between 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rain fell in the Sierra foothills and 0.10 to 0.25 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley. Precipitation in the Tehachapi Mountains varied due to the showery nature of the precipitation by the time it arrived in Kern County, but many stations picked up between 1 and 3 tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Traffic was paced along Interstate 5 by the evening a February 5 as light snow began to accumulate along the Tejon Pass. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the Mohave Desert slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites briefly measuring gusts above 60 mph. As the colder post-frontal airmass pushed into the area during the afternoon of February 5, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley with a few reports of trees being knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts and pea sized hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of an estimated 9 to 15 inches of new snowfall.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough pushed into central California between the afternoon of February 21 and the early morning of February 22. A cold front associated with this trough produced strong winds throughout much of the area. Several stations in the wind prone areas of the West Side Hills and in the Mountains and Deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 60 mph with a few low impact indicator stations reporting gusts above 80 mph. There were also several reports of downed trees in the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree blocking the northbound lane of Del North Ave. just north of Whitesbridge Ave. in Kerman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold upper trough moved across central California on February 27 and 28 which resulted in another period of widespread precipitation across the area. The snow level was between 3000 and 4000 feet or much of this event which resulted in heavy snowfall for the  Sierra Nevada where several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall. Below the snow line, much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter to a half inch of additional rainfall over already saturated soil while the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of combined liquid precipitation. Several roads in the mountains remained closed as a result of the continued snowfall across the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was reported all the way to the coast with heavy snow in the mountains as the coldest in a series of storms impacted the region beginning February 20/21. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper low pressure system moved eastward across northern California on February 5 with precipitation from this system extending southward into central California between the evening of February 4 and the evening of February 5. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada with the snow level running around 6500 feet during the evening of February 4 lowering to around 4000 feet by time the precipitation tapered off during the evening of February 5. SNOTEL estimates indicated between 9 and 15 inches of new snow fell above 6500 feet from Fresno County northward. Between 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rain fell in the Sierra foothills and 0.10 to 0.25 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley. Precipitation in the Tehachapi Mountains varied due to the showery nature of the precipitation by the time it arrived in Kern County, but many stations picked up between 1 and 3 tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Traffic was paced along Interstate 5 by the evening a February 5 as light snow began to accumulate along the Tejon Pass. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the Mohave Desert slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites briefly measuring gusts above 60 mph. As the colder post-frontal airmass pushed into the area during the afternoon of February 5, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley with a few reports of trees being knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts and pea sized hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of an estimated 9 to 15 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough pushed into central California between the afternoon of February 21 and the early morning of February 22. A cold front associated with this trough produced strong winds throughout much of the area. Several stations in the wind prone areas of the West Side Hills and in the Mountains and Deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 60 mph with a few low impact indicator stations reporting gusts above 80 mph. There were also several reports of downed trees in the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Walker Basin Sand Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 14 inches of new snowfall in North Fork (3200 feet) in the previous 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense period of heavy snow and blizzard conditions, damaging winds, low-elevation snow and widespread flooding rainfall plagued Southern California February 21-27.||Very strong, and locally damaging, winds developed in the evening on February 21 and continued through February 22, especially impacting the coast and the mountains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper low pressure system moved eastward across northern California on February 5 with precipitation from this system extending southward into central California between the evening of February 4 and the evening of February 5. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada with the snow level running around 6500 feet during the evening of February 4 lowering to around 4000 feet by time the precipitation tapered off during the evening of February 5. SNOTEL estimates indicated between 9 and 15 inches of new snow fell above 6500 feet from Fresno County northward. Between 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rain fell in the Sierra foothills and 0.10 to 0.25 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley. Precipitation in the Tehachapi Mountains varied due to the showery nature of the precipitation by the time it arrived in Kern County, but many stations picked up between 1 and 3 tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Traffic was paced along Interstate 5 by the evening a February 5 as light snow began to accumulate along the Tejon Pass. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the Mohave Desert slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites briefly measuring gusts above 60 mph. As the colder post-frontal airmass pushed into the area during the afternoon of February 5, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley with a few reports of trees being knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts and pea sized hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful winter storm produced additional heavy snow and high wind over portions Southern California still recovering from historic amounts of snowfall. This event eventually set multiple multi-day snowfall records, and required an extensive recovery period that lasted through March. Accumulating snow and high winds continued over into March 1st and 2nd, the totals and reports of which are reflected in a separate episode and event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass starting on the afternoon of February 28th and lasted overnight, with high winds continuing over into March 1st as well. A peak gust of 61 mph occurred at the Whitewater RAWS site on the 28th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional rounds of light to moderate snow occurred in the San Bernardino County Mountains on February 27th, with more moderate to heavy snow occurring on February 28th. Numerous locations at elevations above 4000 feet received an additional 1 to 3 feet of snow during this time. Bear Mountain and Lake Arrowhead received upwards of 31 inches during the last two days of February. Crestline Yard and Mount Baldy received between 2 and 3 feet of snow during this time as well. Lower snow levels contributed to accumulating snow at elevations between 3000 and 4000 feet as well. A trained spotter reported moderate snow accumulating up to 1 inch at the top of the Cajon Pass. Accumulating snow was also reported near Devore during this time. Accumulating snow continued over into March 1st and 2nd, the totals and reports of which are reflected in a separate episode and event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough dropped southward from the Pacific Northwest into the Great Basin on February 14. This system was moisture deficient with precipitation amounts in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi mountains generally a tenth of an inch or less of liquid precipitation. However, strong pressure gradients accompanying the cold front which pushed through the area during the day produced period of increased winds across the area. Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several highways were proactively closed across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts due to the strong winds.  Increased winds also impacted the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts between 35 and 45 mph. Although impacts from blowing dust were minimal due to the abundant rainfall in December in January, an area of blowing dust west of Chowchilla did result in a 12 vehicle accident which resulted in the closure of SR 152 west of SR 99 for much of the day. The winds decreased during the evening as the trough moved to east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cottonwood Creek West measured a peak wind gust of 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported the eastbound lane of Manning Ave. in Parlier is flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful winter storm produced additional heavy snow and high wind over portions Southern California still recovering from historic amounts of snowfall. This event eventually set multiple multi-day snowfall records, and required an extensive recovery period that lasted through March. Accumulating snow and high winds continued over into March 1st and 2nd, the totals and reports of which are reflected in a separate episode and event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional rounds of light to moderate snow occurred in the Riverside County Mountains on February 27th, with more moderate to heavy snow occurring on February 28th. Numerous locations at elevations above 4000 feet received an additional 1 to 2 feet of snow during this time. A trained spotter in Idyllwild reported total snowfall of 17 inches on February 28th. Snow carried over into March 1st as well, with storm total reports reflected in a separate episode.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was reported all the way to the coast with heavy snow in the mountains as the coldest in a series of storms impacted the region beginning February 20/21. For the next two weeks storms continuously impacted the region with bouts of heavy snow in the mountainous terrain. Anecdotal reports from people who have lived in the area for a long time said this cold period into early March resulted in the heaviest snow at elevations between 1500 and 4000 feet since the 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall of 6 inches in Willits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough dropped southward from the Pacific Northwest into the Great Basin on February 14. This system was moisture deficient with precipitation amounts in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi mountains generally a tenth of an inch or less of liquid precipitation. However, strong pressure gradients accompanying the cold front which pushed through the area during the day produced period of increased winds across the area. Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several highways were proactively closed across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts due to the strong winds.  Increased winds also impacted the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts between 35 and 45 mph. Although impacts from blowing dust were minimal due to the abundant rainfall in December in January, an area of blowing dust west of Chowchilla did result in a 12 vehicle accident which resulted in the closure of SR 152 west of SR 99 for much of the day. The winds decreased during the evening as the trough moved to east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cache Creek measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper low pressure system moved eastward across northern California on February 5 with precipitation from this system extending southward into central California between the evening of February 4 and the evening of February 5. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada with the snow level running around 6500 feet during the evening of February 4 lowering to around 4000 feet by time the precipitation tapered off during the evening of February 5. SNOTEL estimates indicated between 9 and 15 inches of new snow fell above 6500 feet from Fresno County northward. Between 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rain fell in the Sierra foothills and 0.10 to 0.25 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley. Precipitation in the Tehachapi Mountains varied due to the showery nature of the precipitation by the time it arrived in Kern County, but many stations picked up between 1 and 3 tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Traffic was paced along Interstate 5 by the evening a February 5 as light snow began to accumulate along the Tejon Pass. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the Mohave Desert slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites briefly measuring gusts above 60 mph. As the colder post-frontal airmass pushed into the area during the afternoon of February 5, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley with a few reports of trees being knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts and pea sized hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Little Oak Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm in early February brought widespread rain with heavy mountain snow for the northern  Sierra, which along with gusty winds brought mountain travel impacts. Thunderstorms produced small hail and locally heavy rain in the northern San Joaquin Valley and Motherlode foothills. Rainfall amounts in the Valley generally ranged from a tenth of an inch in the northern San Joaquin Valley to around an inch over the northern Sacramento Valley. In the foothills rainfall totals were around a half inch to two and a half inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lower Lassen Peak weather station reported 10 inches of storm total snow. Shingletown 5.4 ENE CoCoRaHS reported 0.2 inches of snow, and Chester Cocorahs reported 1 inch of snow. Gusty winds were observed with this storm, with 70 mph reported at 2.2 NW Pulga (PG&E), 46 mph reported at Carpenter Ridge RAWS, and 58 mph reported at Camel Peak (PG&E).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans reported traffic was held off and on through the late afternoon and evening of the 5th due to spin-outs. Ski resorts reported more than a foot of snow through February 4-6, 2023. For storm total snowfall UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 20.9 inches, Kirkwood Ski Resort reported 17 inches, Sierra-at-Tahoe Ski Resort reported 15 inches, Sugar Bowl Ski Resort reported 21 inches, and 18 inches at Palisades Tahoe. Caltrans reported 11 inches at Kingvale, 11 inches at Eagle Lakes, and 8 inches of Blue Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy mountain and low elevation snow with blizzard conditions in the Sierra, where 5 to 7 feet of storm total snow was reported, with locally higher amounts. Lower elevations observed widespread rain, gusty winds, and isolated thunderstorms.

A cold front associated with this trough produced strong winds throughout much of the area. Several stations in the wind prone areas of the West Side Hills and in the Mountains and Deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 60 mph with a few low impact indicator stations reporting gusts above 80 mph. There were also several reports of downed trees in the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a 100 foot tree in the westbound lanes of E. Bullard Ave. and Amber Ave.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR 58 was closed through Tehachapi from the evening of February 22 until the morning of February 23 due to snow and icy road conditions which caused several vehicle spinouts and jackknifed tractor trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported Rd. 100 south of Parkway Rd. was completely flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense period of heavy snow and blizzard conditions, damaging winds, low-elevation snow and widespread flooding rainfall plagued Southern California February 21-27.||Very strong, and locally damaging, winds developed in the evening on February 21 and continued through February 22, especially impacting the coast and the mountains. Numerous trees were uprooted as a result of the strong winds. Temperatures plummeted on February 22 leading to low-elevation snow and cold-core thunderstorms.||The second, and highly impactful, next wave of the storm system enveloped Southern California starting on February 23. This is the period of heaviest snowfall, and a first-ever Blizzard Warning issued by NWS San Diego was in effect for the San Bernardino County Mountains. Heavy snowfall persisted through early morning on February 26, resulting in FEET of snowfall in the mountains, with many locations in the San Bernardino County Mountains seeing over 50 of snowfall. This resulted in hard closures of many highways in the San Bernardino County Mountains and left people trapped in their homes. Snow levels once again fell on February 25, with snow levels as low as 1000-2000 feet in the Inland Empire and inland Orange County. Widespread heavy thunderstorms occurred leading to local flooding, especially across San Diego County, inland Orange County and portions of the Inland Empire, as well as produced pea size hail.||The impacts of this system, especially in San Bernardino County, would be further exacerbated by an additional significant storm system that would hit only days later, leading to additional FEET of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Bernardino County Fire conducted a swift water rescue of one occupant from a truck. Report of rushing water enveloping the lower half of the truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy mountain and low elevation snow with blizzard conditions in the Sierra, where 5 to 7 feet of storm total snow was reported, with locally higher amounts. Significant power outages, school closures, and road closures were observed. 1 fatality occurred from a collapsed porch due to heavy snow loading. Lower elevations observed widespread rain, gusty winds, and isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans reported storm total snow amounts of 68 inches at Kingvale and 83 inches at Soda Springs. Ski resorts reported 67 inches at Kirkwood Mountain Resort, 63 inches at Sierra-at-Tahoe, 92 inches at Palisades Tahoe, and 62 inches at Sugar Bowl Resort. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 87.2 inches storm total. California Highway Patrol reported the closure of mountain highways due to heavy snow and strong wind, including Highway 50 and Interstate 80. Local weather stations reported strong winds, with gusts to 52 mph on Cole Creek Road (PGE 5854 feet), 41 mph on Spring Valley Road (PGE 3946 feet), 57 mph on Donner Pass Road (PGE 7636 feet). Local broadcast media reported the closure of numerous schools in the Sierra, with extensive power outages.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough dropped southward from the Pacific Northwest into the Great Basin on February 14. This system was moisture deficient with precipitation amounts in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi mountains generally a tenth of an inch or less of liquid precipitation. However, strong pressure gradients accompanying the cold front which pushed through the area during the day produced period of increased winds across the area. Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several highways were proactively closed across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts due to the strong winds.  Increased winds also impacted the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts between 35 and 45 mph. Although impacts from blowing dust were minimal due to the abundant rainfall in December in January, an area of blowing dust west of Chowchilla did result in a 12 vehicle accident which resulted in the closure of SR 152 west of SR 99 for much of the day. The winds decreased during the evening as the trough moved to east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper low pressure system moved eastward across northern California on February 5 with precipitation from this system extending southward into central California between the evening of February 4 and the evening of February 5. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada with the snow level running around 6500 feet during the evening of February 4 lowering to around 4000 feet by time the precipitation tapered off during the evening of February 5. SNOTEL estimates indicated between 9 and 15 inches of new snow fell above 6500 feet from Fresno County northward. Between 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rain fell in the Sierra foothills and 0.10 to 0.25 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley. Precipitation in the Tehachapi Mountains varied due to the showery nature of the precipitation by the time it arrived in Kern County, but many stations picked up between 1 and 3 tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Traffic was paced along Interstate 5 by the evening a February 5 as light snow began to accumulate along the Tejon Pass. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the Mohave Desert slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites briefly measuring gusts above 60 mph. As the colder post-frontal airmass pushed into the area during the afternoon of February 5, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley with a few reports of trees being knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts and pea sized hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree and large rocks washed up in the roadway on SR 140 and the Ferguson Slide Bridge.

CZ_NAME: WESTERN SAN GABRIEL MOUNTAINS INCLUDING THE HIGHWAY 14 CORRIDOR
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty north to northeast winds impacted portions of Southwestern California. Widespread wind gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range were reported with even some localized gusts over 100 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty north to northeast winds impacted the western San Gabriel Mountains. Some peak wind gusts include: Magic Mountain Truck Trail (gust 104 MPH), Chilao RAWS (gust 65 MPH), Mesa Vincent (gust 62 MPH) and Camp Nine RAWS (gust 70 MPH).

Lower elevations observed widespread rain, gusty winds, and isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS reported heavy storm total snow amounts, with 29.4 inches near Nevada City, 15.6 inches near Volcano, 10.4 inches near Camino, 26.3 inches near Grass Valley, and5 inches in Placerville. An 80 year old woman was killed when the porch of a house collapsed in Foresthill under the weight of snow, with about 4 feet of snow reported on the ground by Foresthill spotter. Widespread power outages were reported with multiple schools closed. Broadcast media reported the roof collapse of a church in Foresthill due to snow load, with no injuries reported.

This resulted in hard closures of many highways in the San Bernardino County Mountains and left people trapped in their homes. Snow levels once again fell on February 25, with snow levels as low as 1000-2000 feet in the Inland Empire and inland Orange County. Widespread heavy thunderstorms occurred leading to local flooding, especially across San Diego County, inland Orange County and portions of the Inland Empire, as well as produced pea size hail.||The impacts of this system, especially in San Bernardino County, would be further exacerbated by an additional significant storm system that would hit only days later, leading to additional FEET of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of flooding at Country Club Dip south of Harmony Grove Road, resulting in its closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved southeast into Oregon.  An associated cold front swept across the region with high elevation snow for northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snowfall at elevations above 5000 feet.  This includes 6 inches reported at the Sand Flat SNOTEL and National Gridded Snowfall estimates of 4 to 10 inches in the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough dropped southward from the Pacific Northwest into the Great Basin on February 14. This system was moisture deficient with precipitation amounts in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi mountains generally a tenth of an inch or less of liquid precipitation. However, strong pressure gradients accompanying the cold front which pushed through the area during the day produced period of increased winds across the area. Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several highways were proactively closed across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts due to the strong winds.  Increased winds also impacted the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts between 35 and 45 mph. Although impacts from blowing dust were minimal due to the abundant rainfall in December in January, an area of blowing dust west of Chowchilla did result in a 12 vehicle accident which resulted in the closure of SR 152 west of SR 99 for much of the day. The winds decreased during the evening as the trough moved to east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mojave Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inslide slider storm system dove across the Western US on February 14th and 15th. This led to high winds occurring over much of the mountains and desert foothills, as well as caused snow levels to fall to around 3000-4000 feet in Southern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred across the Apple and Lucerne Valleys mainly during the morning and early afternoon hours on February 14th. Multiple SoCal-Edison sites in the Lucerne Valley recorded peak wind gusts of 58 mph. In Phelan, a street sign was blown over in 40-50 mph winds per a report from CalTrans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inslide slider storm system dove across the Western US on February 14th and 15th. This led to high winds occurring over much of the mountains and desert foothills, as well as caused snow levels to fall to around 3000-4000 feet in Southern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow levels fell to as low as around 3000 feet in portions of the Inland Empire on the evening of February 14th, allowing for about 2-4 inches of accumulating snow at these elevations. Icy road conditions led to multiple vehicles being trapped near Pine Valley, Warner Springs, and in Julian near S2 and S22 off of Highway 78. A semi truck also jackknifed near Warner Springs. CalTrans issued a Sigalert on San Felipe Road due to the treacherous travel conditions in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inslide slider storm system dove across the Western US on February 14th and 15th. This led to high winds occurring over much of the mountains and desert foothills, as well as caused snow levels to fall to around 3000-4000 feet in Southern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow levels fell to as low as around 3000 feet in portions of the San Bernardino County Mountans on the evening of February 14th, allowing for about 1-3 inches of accumulating snow at these elevations. Multiple CalTrans reports of vehicles spinning out and sliding off of Highway 189 were received from the Lake Arrowhead and Crestline areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper low pressure system moved eastward across northern California on February 5 with precipitation from this system extending southward into central California between the evening of February 4 and the evening of February 5. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada with the snow level running around 6500 feet during the evening of February 4 lowering to around 4000 feet by time the precipitation tapered off during the evening of February 5. SNOTEL estimates indicated between 9 and 15 inches of new snow fell above 6500 feet from Fresno County northward. Between 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rain fell in the Sierra foothills and 0.10 to 0.25 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley. Precipitation in the Tehachapi Mountains varied due to the showery nature of the precipitation by the time it arrived in Kern County, but many stations picked up between 1 and 3 tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Traffic was paced along Interstate 5 by the evening a February 5 as light snow began to accumulate along the Tejon Pass. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the Mohave Desert slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites briefly measuring gusts above 60 mph. As the colder post-frontal airmass pushed into the area during the afternoon of February 5, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley with a few reports of trees being knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts and pea sized hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree blown down onto the roadway near the intersection of Usry and Central Avenues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Tejon Hills measured a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rain, heavy snow and strong winds to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches across coastal and valleys areas with 4 to 10 inches across the foothills and mountains. Snow levels dropped down, resulting in significant snowfall across the mountains. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding on Interstate 5 near Lankershim Boulevard near Burbank. A couple of vehicles were stuck in the flooded roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Homewood Mountain Ski Base, CA, reported a storm total snowfall of 15.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low pressure system produced light to moderate rainfall, isolated thunderstorms and light snow in the mountains February 12 to 13, with most of the impacts felt in San Diego County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled in over central California behind the trough which brought strong winds to the area on February 14. With clear skies and decreasing winds, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for several hours during the mornings of February 15 and 16 in the San Joaquin Valley. In addition, Interstate 5 and SR 58 were closed in the Tehachapi Mountains for a portion of the morning of February 15 due to black ice which resulted from light precipitation from the previous day freezing over on the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR 58 was closed from 15 miles west of Tehachapi to 2.4 miles west of Mojave due to black ice which resulted from precipitation freezing over on the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold upper trough moved across central California on February 27 and 28 which resulted in another period of widespread precipitation across the area. Snow levels dropped down, resulting in significant snowfall across the mountains. Below 5000 feet, 6 to 12 inches of snow was reported while the resort level (greater than 6000 feet) received 2 to 5 feet of snowfall. With the snowfall and strong winds, a Blizzard Warning was issues for the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. As for winds, peak gusts of 60 to 80 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rare blizzard conditions developed in the mountains of Santa Barbara county. Heavy snowfall was reported with totals between 1 and 3 feet reported above 4500 feet. Along with the heavy snowfall, strong southerly winds, gusting up to 65 MPH, produced dangerous winter weather conditions with visibilities of near zero miles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A big rig was blown over on Highway 395 south of Pearsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled in over central California behind the trough which brought strong winds to the area on February 14. With clear skies and decreasing winds, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for several hours during the mornings of February 15 and 16 in the San Joaquin Valley. In addition, Interstate 5 and SR 58 were closed in the Tehachapi Mountains for a portion of the morning of February 15 due to black ice which resulted from light precipitation from the previous day freezing over on the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 27 and 31 degrees on February 15 and 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Beartrap Road measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold upper trough moved across central California on February 27 and 28 which resulted in another period of widespread precipitation across the area. The snow level was between 3000 and 4000 feet or much of this event which resulted in heavy snowfall for the  Sierra Nevada where several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall. Below the snow line, much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter to a half inch of additional rainfall over already saturated soil while the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of combined liquid precipitation. Several roads in the mountains remained closed as a result of the continued snowfall across the area. The precipitation ended by the morning of March 1 as a colder and drier airmass pushed into central California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gin Flat SNOTEL (7201 feet) picked up an estimated 36 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense period of heavy snow and blizzard conditions, damaging winds, low-elevation snow and widespread flooding rainfall plagued Southern California February 21-27.||Very strong, and locally damaging, winds developed in the evening on February 21 and continued through February 22, especially impacting the coast and the mountains. Numerous trees were uprooted as a result of the strong winds. Temperatures plummeted on February 22 leading to low-elevation snow and cold-core thunderstorms.||The second, and highly impactful, next wave of the storm system enveloped Southern California starting on February 23. This is the period of heaviest snowfall, and a first-ever Blizzard Warning issued by NWS San Diego was in effect for the San Bernardino County Mountains. Heavy snowfall persisted through early morning on February 26, resulting in FEET of snowfall in the mountains, with many locations in the San Bernardino County Mountains seeing over 50 of snowfall. This resulted in hard closures of many highways in the San Bernardino County Mountains and left people trapped in their homes. Snow levels once again fell on February 25, with snow levels as low as 1000-2000 feet in the Inland Empire and inland Orange County. Widespread heavy thunderstorms occurred leading to local flooding, especially across San Diego County, inland Orange County and portions of the Inland Empire, as well as produced pea size hail.||The impacts of this system, especially in San Bernardino County, would be further exacerbated by an additional significant storm system that would hit only days later, leading to additional FEET of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anomalously strong winds impacted the San Diego County coastline from the very early morning hours on February 22 through the afternoon, peaking during the overnight hours through sunrise.||The following are the strongest wind reports from the coast:|Mesonet station XZUN Silver Strand Beach reported a peak wind gust of 58 mph.|Mesonet station XOCJ Oceanside Jetty reported a peak wind gust of 57 mph.|AWOS Station KNXF Red Beach Airport reported a peak wind gust of 56 mph.||Tree damage was also reported during the early morning hours, with a toppled tree resulting in car damage in Mission Valley; a large Torrey Pine tree fell on a home in Del Mar Heights with a branch crashing through the roof in the living room; and a large Torrey Pine tree toppled over at the Torrey Pines Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and cold storm system with heavy snow and gusty winds brought blizzard conditions. Snow amounts of 2 to near 5 feet were reported in the mountains. Significant snow fell down into the northern Sacramento Valley, with 3 to 8 inches of accumulation impacting travel. Many schools were closed or had delays. Wind gusts of 40-60 mph were recorded across the area, with gusts locally higher in the high elevations of the northern Sierra. The winds and low snow combined to cause dangerous travel conditions numerous road closures, including a closure of Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, on US Highway 50 and an extended closure of Interstate 80 across the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and wind brought dangerous driving conditions with area road closures. Trained spotters in Quincy, CA reported storm total snow amounts of 16 to 22 inches. Lower Lassen Peak weather station reported 40 inches of storm total snow. Quincy COOP reported 20 inches of storm total snow, and Chester COOP reported 15 inches of snow. Gusty winds were observed with this storm, with 54 mph reported at Camel Peak, 35 mph reported at Humbug Summit RAWS, 45 mph reported at Colby Mountain RAWS, and 40 mph reported at Hatchet Mountain. Area power outages were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Sims reported a storm total snow of 36 inches at an elevation of 2800 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 12 to 36 inches fell for storm total snow in the mountains of northern Shasta County. Interstate 5 northbound was closed at Fawndale due to winter weather conditions from 8:53 pm 2/23 to 1:00 am 2/24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was reported all the way to the coast with heavy snow in the mountains as the coldest in a series of storms impacted the region beginning February 20/21. For the next two weeks storms continuously impacted the region with bouts of heavy snow in the mountainous terrain. Anecdotal reports from people who have lived in the area for a long time said this cold period into early March resulted in the heaviest snow at elevations between 1500 and 4000 feet since the 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 5 inches of snow on Humboldt Hill at 500 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a storm which brought some light rain on February 3 to the northern San Joaquin Valley with clearing skies and light winds, dense fog formed in Merced County by the morning of February 4 and impacted travel along SR 99.  The fog extended southward into the central San Joaquin Valley toward sunrise then dissipated later in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater and Merced. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and cold storm system with heavy snow and gusty winds brought blizzard conditions. Snow amounts of 2 to near 5 feet were reported in the mountains. Significant snow fell down into the northern Sacramento Valley, with 3 to 8 inches of accumulation impacting travel. Many schools were closed or had delays. Wind gusts of 40-60 mph were recorded across the area, with gusts locally higher in the high elevations of the northern Sierra. The winds and low snow combined to cause dangerous travel conditions numerous road closures, including a closure of Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, on US Highway 50 and an extended closure of Interstate 80 across the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low elevation snow fell over the northern Sacramento Valley. Snowfall reports included 6 inches at Red Bluff (300 feet), 6.5 inches for northern Redding (580 feet), 5 inches at Redding Fire (near Redding Airport), and 4 inches at Red Bluff water treatment. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 2 to 8 inches fell for storm total snow. Interstate 5 northbound was closed 10 miles north of Redding at Fawndale due to winter weather conditions from 8:53 pm 2/23 to 1:00 am 2/24.
