EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was reported all the way to the coast with heavy snow in the mountains as the coldest in a series of storms impacted the region beginning February 20/21. For the next two weeks storms continuously impacted the region with bouts of heavy snow in the mountainous terrain. Anecdotal reports from people who have lived in the area for a long time said this cold period into early March resulted in the heaviest snow at elevations between 1500 and 4000 feet since the 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Fort Dick reported 3 inches of snow with light snow still falling at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled in over central California behind the trough which brought strong winds to the area on February 14. With clear skies and decreasing winds, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for several hours during the mornings of February 15 and 16 in the San Joaquin Valley. In addition, Interstate 5 and SR 58 were closed in the Tehachapi Mountains for a portion of the morning of February 15 due to black ice which resulted from light precipitation from the previous day freezing over on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding at the intersection of Kelso Valley Rd. and Kelso Creek Rd. resulting in the closures of both roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold upper trough moved across central California on February 27 and 28 which resulted in another period of widespread precipitation across the area. The snow level was between 3000 and 4000 feet or much of this event which resulted in heavy snowfall for the  Sierra Nevada where several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall. Below the snow line, much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter to a half inch of additional rainfall over already saturated soil while the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of combined liquid precipitation. Several roads in the mountains remained closed as a result of the continued snowfall across the area. The precipitation ended by the morning of March 1 as a colder and drier airmass pushed into central California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devil's Postpile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 34 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stream of high clouds kept minimum temperatures above the freezing mark across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of February 17. However, skies cleared out toward daybreak during the early morning of February 18 while cold and dry airmass and light winds prevailed over the area. As a result, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark across portions of the central and eastern San Joaquin Valley for a few hours during the morning of February 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 31 degrees on February 18.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough pushed into central California between the afternoon of February 21 and the early morning of February 22. A cold front associated with this trough produced strong winds throughout much of the area. Several stations in the wind prone areas of the West Side Hills and in the Mountains and Deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 60 mph with a few low impact indicator stations reporting gusts above 80 mph. There were also several reports of downed trees in the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Joaquin Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on February 24 and 25. This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported mud, dirt and rocks blocking the eastbound lane of SR 140 near the intersection of Hites Mine Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold upper trough moved across central California on February 27 and 28 which resulted in another period of widespread precipitation across the area. The snow level was between 3000 and 4000 feet or much of this event which resulted in heavy snowfall for the  Sierra Nevada where several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall. Below the snow line, much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter to a half inch of additional rainfall over already saturated soil while the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of combined liquid precipitation. Several roads in the mountains remained closed as a result of the continued snowfall across the area. The precipitation ended by the morning of March 1 as a colder and drier airmass pushed into central California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mitchell Meadow SNOTEL (9900 feet) picked up an estimated 34 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense period of heavy snow and blizzard conditions, damaging winds, low-elevation snow and widespread flooding rainfall plagued Southern California February 21-27.||Very strong, and locally damaging, winds developed in the evening on February 21 and continued through February 22, especially impacting the coast and the mountains. Numerous trees were uprooted as a result of the strong winds. Temperatures plummeted on February 22 leading to low-elevation snow and cold-core thunderstorms.||The second, and highly impactful, next wave of the storm system enveloped Southern California starting on February 23. This is the period of heaviest snowfall, and a first-ever Blizzard Warning issued by NWS San Diego was in effect for the San Bernardino County Mountains. Heavy snowfall persisted through early morning on February 26, resulting in FEET of snowfall in the mountains, with many locations in the San Bernardino County Mountains seeing over 50 of snowfall. This resulted in hard closures of many highways in the San Bernardino County Mountains and left people trapped in their homes. Snow levels once again fell on February 25, with snow levels as low as 1000-2000 feet in the Inland Empire and inland Orange County. Widespread heavy thunderstorms occurred leading to local flooding, especially across San Diego County, inland Orange County and portions of the Inland Empire, as well as produced pea size hail.||The impacts of this system, especially in San Bernardino County, would be further exacerbated by an additional significant storm system that would hit only days later, leading to additional FEET of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass from the evening on February 21 through the early morning hours on February 22, when widespread wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were observed.||The following are the strongest wind gusts reported:|Whitewater RAWS reported a peak wind gust of 79 mph.|Mesonet station 015SCE Whitewater reported a peak wind gust of 73 mph.|Mesonet station 289SCE Whitewater reported a peak wind gust of 69 mph.|Mesonet station SCE1010 Cabazon reported a peak wind gust of 68 mph.|Mesonet station Whiteater Canyon SCE581 reported a peak wind gust of 65 mph.|Mesonet station Desert Hot Springs reported a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was reported all the way to the coast with heavy snow in the mountains as the coldest in a series of storms impacted the region beginning February 20/21. For the next two weeks storms continuously impacted the region with bouts of heavy snow in the mountainous terrain. Anecdotal reports from people who have lived in the area for a long time said this cold period into early March resulted in the heaviest snow at elevations between 1500 and 4000 feet since the 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulated on Highway 101 near Trinidad resulting in an automobile accident. Injuries unknown. The time is estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and cold storm system with heavy snow and gusty winds brought blizzard conditions. Snow amounts of 2 to near 5 feet were reported in the mountains. Significant snow fell down into the northern Sacramento Valley, with 3 to 8 inches of accumulation impacting travel. Many schools were closed or had delays. Wind gusts of 40-60 mph were recorded across the area, with gusts locally higher in the high elevations of the northern Sierra. The winds and low snow combined to cause dangerous travel conditions numerous road closures, including a closure of Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, on US Highway 50 and an extended closure of Interstate 80 across the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans reported a storm total snow amount of 42 inches at Soda Springs. Ski resorts reported 47 inches at Kirkwood Mountain Resort, 39 inches at Sierra-at-Tahoe, 53 inches at Palisades Tahoe, and 45 inches at Sugar Bowl Resort. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 57.1 inches storm total. California Highway Patrol reported the closure of mountain highways due to heavy snow and strong wind, including Highway 50 and Interstate 80. Interstate 80 was closed in both directions from Colfax to CA/NV stateline from 1:30 PM 2/23 to 10:40 AM 2/24. Traffic was held on US Highway 50 eastbound to South Lake Tahoe for around 4 hours on 2/23. Several other highways across the mountains were also closed. Local media reported area schools were closed. Local weather stations reported strong winds, with gusts to 59 mph at Duncan RAWS, 54 mph at Tahoe Donner RAWS (along I-80), and 45 mph at Alpha DRI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper low pressure system moved eastward across northern California on February 5 with precipitation from this system extending southward into central California between the evening of February 4 and the evening of February 5. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada with the snow level running around 6500 feet during the evening of February 4 lowering to around 4000 feet by time the precipitation tapered off during the evening of February 5. SNOTEL estimates indicated between 9 and 15 inches of new snow fell above 6500 feet from Fresno County northward. Between 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rain fell in the Sierra foothills and 0.10 to 0.25 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley. Precipitation in the Tehachapi Mountains varied due to the showery nature of the precipitation by the time it arrived in Kern County, but many stations picked up between 1 and 3 tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Traffic was paced along Interstate 5 by the evening a February 5 as light snow began to accumulate along the Tejon Pass. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the Mohave Desert slopes with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites briefly measuring gusts above 60 mph. As the colder post-frontal airmass pushed into the area during the afternoon of February 5, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley with a few reports of trees being knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts and pea sized hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of an estimated 9 to 15 inches of new snowfall.

This system brought 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation between the early morning of February 24 and the late morning of February 25 to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills as well as to the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley. This was a cold storm with snow levels lowering from around 3000 feet during the morning of February 24 lowering to 1500 to 2000 feet during the morning of February 25 with some locations near 1000 feet picking up some light snowfall while higher elevations generally picked up between 2 and 4 feet of new snowfall. The snowfall resulted in several mountain roads being closed for extended periods of time. In addition several reports of flooding were observed in the San Joaquin Valley and the lower Sierra foothills resulting in additional road closures. The low moved across southern California during the evening of February 25 and east of the area during the morning of February 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a vehicle spun out on SR 198 and became stuck in mud at the SR 198 and Plaza Dr. offramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold upper trough moved across central California on February 27 and 28 which resulted in another period of widespread precipitation across the area. The snow level was between 3000 and 4000 feet or much of this event which resulted in heavy snowfall for the  Sierra Nevada where several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall. Below the snow line, much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter to a half inch of additional rainfall over already saturated soil while the Sierra foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of combined liquid precipitation. Several roads in the mountains remained closed as a result of the continued snowfall across the area. The precipitation ended by the morning of March 1 as a colder and drier airmass pushed into central California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Bull SNOTEL (8073 feet) picked up an estimated 25 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense period of heavy snow and blizzard conditions, damaging winds, low-elevation snow and widespread flooding rainfall plagued Southern California February 21-27.||Very strong, and locally damaging, winds developed in the evening on February 21 and continued through February 22, especially impacting the coast and the mountains. Numerous trees were uprooted as a result of the strong winds. Temperatures plummeted on February 22 leading to low-elevation snow and cold-core thunderstorms.||The second, and highly impactful, next wave of the storm system enveloped Southern California starting on February 23. This is the period of heaviest snowfall, and a first-ever Blizzard Warning issued by NWS San Diego was in effect for the San Bernardino County Mountains. Heavy snowfall persisted through early morning on February 26, resulting in FEET of snowfall in the mountains, with many locations in the San Bernardino County Mountains seeing over 50 of snowfall. This resulted in hard closures of many highways in the San Bernardino County Mountains and left people trapped in their homes. Snow levels once again fell on February 25, with snow levels as low as 1000-2000 feet in the Inland Empire and inland Orange County. Widespread heavy thunderstorms occurred leading to local flooding, especially across San Diego County, inland Orange County and portions of the Inland Empire, as well as produced pea size hail.||The impacts of this system, especially in San Bernardino County, would be further exacerbated by an additional significant storm system that would hit only days later, leading to additional FEET of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anomalously strong winds impacted the Orange County coastline from the late evening hours on February 21 through the early morning on February 22.The following are the strongest wind reports from the coast:||Mesonet station SE024 Laguna Beach reported a peak wind gust of 65 mph.|San Clemente 3 reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.|Huntington Beach Pier reported a peak wind gust of 57 mph.|Newport Pier reported a peak wind gust of 57 mph.|Huntington Beach reported a peak wind gust of 50 mph.||A trained spotter reported tree damage as well as shingles blown off of a structure.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard impacted the San Bernardino County Mountains February 23-26, prompting the first-ever Blizzard Warning issued by NWS San Diego. The heaviest snowfall occurred on February 24-25. Snow accumulations on the order of FEET inundated mountain communities with 70-90+ inches recorded at ski resorts, and 2-3 feet occurred at mountain communities located at 5000-7000 feet.||This snowfall severely incapacitated communities, resulting in hard closures of Highway 2, Highway 18, Highway 138 and Highway 330 from Friday, February 24 through Monday, February 27. Prior to the road closures, multiple vehicles had been stuck or disabled.  Vehicles also became stuck on the heavily traveled I-15 through the Cajon Pass Saturday morning, which ended up being shut down due to snow and ice early Saturday afternoon. As of the evening on February 27, these highways partially reopened to escort residents back up the mountain and allow for food and fuel delivery to mountain communities. 500 6th and 7th grade students who were attending a Science Camp in the mountains were unable to return home as scheduled on Feb 24th.||The following are some of the highest recorded snow totals from this region:||Mountain High (6600-8000'): 93|Snow Valley (7000-8000'): 78-90|End of Mt Baldy Road (6360'): 77|Running Springs (6225'): 71|Green Valley Lake (6900'): 70|Lake Arrowhead (5200'): 68|Arrowbear Lake (6220'): 65|Big Bear Mountain Snow Summit (7100'): 63-75|Crestline Yard (4850'): 63|Long Valley Ranger Station (4850'): 60|Wrightwood Acorn Canyon (6600'): 50-55||Impacts extended well beyond the end time of this round of snow, especially as another storm system produced additional FEET of snowfall in the following days. Residents remained trapped, transportation remained halted and resources ran dry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was reported all the way to the coast with heavy snow in the mountains as the coldest in a series of storms impacted the region beginning February 20/21. For the next two weeks storms continuously impacted the region with bouts of heavy snow in the mountainous terrain. Anecdotal reports from people who have lived in the area for a long time said this cold period into early March resulted in the heaviest snow at elevations between 1500 and 4000 feet since the 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 2 to 3 inches of snow in Hydesville and still snowing at the time of the report at 5:51 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From January 8th to January 9th, a strong atmospheric river led to widespread flooding across the San Francisco Bay Area and Central Coast. This was followed by convection on the 10th that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS weather station at the San Francisco International Airport reported a gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy mountain and low elevation snow with blizzard conditions in the Sierra, where 5 to 7 feet of storm total snow was reported, with locally higher amounts. Significant power outages, school closures, and road closures were observed. 1 fatality occurred from a collapsed porch due to heavy snow loading. Lower elevations observed widespread rain, gusty winds, and isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in Quincy, CA reported storm total snow amounts of 16 to 22 inches. Lower Lassen Peak weather station reported 21.8 inches of storm total snow. Shingletown Cocorahs reported 20 inches of storm total snow, and Chester Cocorahs reported 30 inches of snow. Gusty winds were observed with this storm, with 47 mph reported at Humbug Summit RAWS, 45 mph reported at Colby Mountain RAWS, and 48 mph reported at Hatchet Mountain. Widespread power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stream of high clouds kept minimum temperatures above the freezing mark across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of February 17. However, skies cleared out toward daybreak during the early morning of February 18 while cold and dry airmass and light winds prevailed over the area. As a result, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark across portions of the central and eastern San Joaquin Valley for a few hours during the morning of February 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 31 degrees on February 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm continued to produce heavy snowfall across the Riverside County Mountains on March 1st. Additional snowfall of 1-2 feet occurred across many mountain communities above 5,000 feet in elevation. Notable 7 day storm total snowfall amounts include 64 at the top of the Palm Springs tram on Mount San Jacinto, and 34 near Idyllwild.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, even damaging west-southwest winds increased travel hazards especially to high profile trucks but also increased the danger for destructive avalanches across the region. Snow started at all elevations Thursday evening with snow levels rapidly rising Friday morning.  Heavy, wet snow (Sierra Nevada Cement) fell across higher elevation areas as snow levels rose to 7000 feet over the region.  Heavy rainfall increased the possibility of flooding for lower elevation creeks, streams, along with urban drainage issues due to snow berms. Significant rises on mainstem rivers were monitored for flooding. Deep, cold snowpack absorbed most liquid precipitation during this second AR supported storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that Olympic Valley road and Alpine Meadows road were closed for operations due to flooding and lift operations.  Palisades Tahoe at Alpine Meadows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southwest winds, especially Monday evening through Tuesday night, brought the potential for possible travel disruptions, tree and fence damage, as well as local power outages. Mountain snow showers increased intensity during the evening above 7000 feet dumping heavy wet snow over mountain communities which brought heightened snow load  concerns for those areas. With the attendant stronger AR bringing relatively higher snow levels and more heavy rain to an already primed heavy snowpack, flood concerns further increased for larger streams and creeks, as well as for mainstream rivers. Temperatures for the first March AR supported storm system remained mostly below seasonal averages, and contributed to limited flooding issues.  Temperatures for the subsequent two storm systems remained mostly near to slightly above average in response to the warmer tap of sub-tropical moisture. Therefore, flooding issues increased for these two follow up AR driven storm systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Homewood Meadows Ski Resort reported 3-day storm snowfall totals of 30 inches at 7880 feet elevation and 16 inches at 6230' elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southwest winds 30-40 mph preceded the storm system and attendant cold front, bringing the increased potential for travel, aviation, and recreation impacts, especially across higher elevations of the eastern Sierra.  Snow began in the Sierra & northeast California Saturday morning with peak snowfall Saturday afternoon through evening, and again overnight into Sunday morning.  Sunday morning rain and snow began to spillover into western Nevada. Impacts included snow load on infrastructure, roofalanche potential, and travel /ski commute disruptions. Heavy, deep snow covering most road ways lead to extended road closures for many Mono county state highways and roads, especially along US 395 from Bridgeport to Mammoth Mountain valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mammoth Mountain Ski base reported a major power outage across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet system brought periods of heavy rain with flooding. There was 1 to 2 feet of snow accumulated above 6,500 feet, locally higher on some peaks. Heavy rain fell bringing widespread roadway flooding, as well as local mudslides, flash flooding, with evacuations ordered in some areas. Thunderstorms with large hail, strong damaging winds and tornadoes were reported. Strong winds gusting to 55 to 75 mph brought down numerous trees and powerlines across the area, causing widespread and extended power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1 inch hail in Downtown Sonora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This AR fed winter system shaped up to be a major heavy snow event for Mono, Mineral, and Southern Lyon Counties,  that extended south into Inyo and Esmeralda Counties. A deformation axis wrapped into the region with moist subtropical air transported by a moderate to strong AR that was directed into central and southern California. A strong southeast to east-southeast upper flow also aided in setting up the convergence zone in addition to upslope flow into the eastern Sierra. Impacts were considerable for routes between western and southern NV, that included closures of US-395, US-95, and Hwy-6 due to heavy snow in addition to strong south-southeast winds creating blowing and drifting snow and periods as well as near zero visibility. Dangerous wind chills occurred across the High Sierra with additional impacts for more vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS Observer (CA-MN-6) located 5.0 mi NNW of Lee Vining, CA, reported storm total snowfall of 40.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01-MAMMOTH) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA,  reported a peak wind gust 144 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southwest winds, especially Monday evening through Tuesday night, brought the potential for possible travel disruptions, tree and fence damage, as well as local power outages. Mountain snow showers increased intensity during the evening above 7000 feet dumping heavy wet snow over mountain communities which brought heightened snow load  concerns for those areas. With the attendant stronger AR bringing relatively higher snow levels and more heavy rain to an already primed heavy snowpack, flood concerns further increased for larger streams and creeks, as well as for mainstream rivers. Temperatures for the first March AR supported storm system remained mostly below seasonal averages, and contributed to limited flooding issues.  Temperatures for the subsequent two storm systems remained mostly near to slightly above average in response to the warmer tap of sub-tropical moisture. Therefore, flooding issues increased for these two follow up AR driven storm systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Blue Streak Weather Station (HVRTM) located 1.0 mi NNW of  Heavenly Ski Summit reported a peak wind gust of 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter AR driven storm brought stronger winds especially for mountain tops, heavy Sierra snowfall, and valley rain or snow showers through the forecast period. Travel impacts were also expected for regional roadways which included Sierra mountain passes to include the I-80 corridor and along US-50 east of US-95 as rain changed to snow after sunset. Strong southwesterly winds were ongoing across the region with gusts exceeding 100 mph along Sierra ridgelines and widespread gusts to 50 mph in lower western Nevada valleys. Mono County was expected to see the snow last up to 15 hours with the heaviest snow area generally directed along the Sierra for about 9-12 hours, resulting in a solid swath of 1-2 feet along the crest, with some higher Mono County peaks projected to receive 30-36 inches of new snow. Through this challenging forecast period, brief whiteout conditions along with blowing/drifting snow were expected across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Central Sierra Snow Lab 6.0 mi W of Truckee, CA, 1.0 mi SSW of Northstar Ski Base, CA, and the Heavenly Ski Base, CA, reported 24-hour snowfall totals of 20.7, 16, and 11 inches respectfully.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This AR fed winter system shaped up to be a major heavy snow event for Mono, Mineral, and Southern Lyon Counties,  that extended south into Inyo and Esmeralda Counties. A deformation axis wrapped into the region with moist subtropical air transported by a moderate to strong AR that was directed into central and southern California. A strong southeast to east-southeast upper flow also aided in setting up the convergence zone in addition to upslope flow into the eastern Sierra. Impacts were considerable for routes between western and southern NV, that included closures of US-395, US-95, and Hwy-6 due to heavy snow in addition to strong south-southeast winds creating blowing and drifting snow and periods as well as near zero visibility. Dangerous wind chills occurred across the High Sierra with additional impacts for more vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mammoth Mountain Ski Base reported a storm total snowfall of 47.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall across Southern California March 14-15. Rain amounts of 1 to 3 inches were observed across the coastal basin, with heavier amounts up to 4 inches reported in the mountains. Rain fell on an extremely deep snowpack in the mountains, resulting in flooding. Flooding also occurred across the coast and valleys, where several debris flows occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant landslide occurred following heavy rain in San Clemente on the 1500 block of Buena Vista the morning of March 15. All buildings were immediately evacuated, with no injuries reported. At least four apartment buildings had been red-tagged by the afternoon (deemed too dangerous to enter).

Rainfall totals were marginal for most lower elevation areas across western Nevada  outside of Mono and Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southwest winds 30-40 mph preceded the storm system and attendant cold front, bringing the increased potential for travel, aviation, and recreation impacts, especially across higher elevations of the eastern Sierra.  Snow began in the Sierra & northeast California Saturday morning with peak snowfall Saturday afternoon through evening, and again overnight into Sunday morning.  Sunday morning rain and snow began to spillover into western Nevada. Impacts included snow load on infrastructure, roofalanche potential, and travel /ski commute disruptions. Heavy, deep snow covering most road ways lead to extended road closures for many Mono county state highways and roads, especially along US 395 from Bridgeport to Mammoth Mountain valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer located at the Truckee-Tahoe Airport in Truckee, CA,  reported a 24 hour total snowfall amount of 12.0 inches. At the airport, one inch of liquid equivalent and a current snow depth of 43 inches were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another AR enhanced winter storm brought travel impacts to the region later Monday night through Wednesday, with strong winds, heavy Sierra snowfall, and periods of moderate valley rain or snow. A boom-bust scenario was projected to express the uncertainty of the track of a surface low track along the California coast. If the low tracked more south and offshore, this would limit the amount of moisture and mesoscale lift necessary for heavier snowfall across the region and lead to the higher chance bust scenario.  The lower probability boom scenario included if the low tracked closer to the Sierra Nevada and crossed into western Nevada. This projected track was expected to lead to stronger frontogenesis and moisture flux that would generate more persistent bands of heavier snowfall. With the high spread of potential scenarios, the official forecast split the difference.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 1.0 mi E of Sierraville, CA, and in Loyalton, CA both reported a 24-hour snowfall amount of 1.0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another atmospheric river brought heavy rain and snow to the Sierra Nevada and the Owens Valley on the 9th and 10th. Flooding persisted for several days after.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and melting snow resulted in a mix of flooding and flash flooding throughout the Owens Valley. Highway 395 and nearby roads were flooded in areas from 15 miles NW of Bishop all the way down to Pearsonville. Several roads were flooded with water, rocks, and/or debris, and a few vehicles were stuck. Southern Inyo Hospital in Lone Pine was flooded. The Los Angeles Aqueduct failed in multiple areas, resulting in one dam failing and flooding open desert, as well as flooding of Highway 136 due to releases from Alabama Gates.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the northern Sierra. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 60.9 inches storm total snow. Caltrans reported storm total snow amounts of 40 inches at Castle Peak, but was unable to report measurements on the 6th due to road conditions. Ski resorts reported 44 inches at Kirkwood Mountain Resort, 32 inches at Sierra-at-Tahoe, 52 inches at Palisades Tahoe, and 73 inches at Sugar Bowl Resort. Caltrans reported the closure of mountain highways due to heavy snow and strong wind, including Interstate 80 and State Route 49 due to heavy blowing snow bringing very low visibility and causing multiple spinouts. Interstate 80 was closed from Colfax to the Nevada State Line at 1:15 pm on the 4th and remained closed for nearly 34 hours. Several ski resorts were closed due to heavy snow and whiteout conditions. Winds gusted to 40 to 60 mph. Local broadcast media reported the closure of numerous schools in the Sierra, with extensive power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southwest winds, especially Monday evening through Tuesday night, brought the potential for possible travel disruptions, tree and fence damage, as well as local power outages. Mountain snow showers increased intensity during the evening above 7000 feet dumping heavy wet snow over mountain communities which brought heightened snow load  concerns for those areas. With the attendant stronger AR bringing relatively higher snow levels and more heavy rain to an already primed heavy snowpack, flood concerns further increased for larger streams and creeks, as well as for mainstream rivers. Temperatures for the first March AR supported storm system remained mostly below seasonal averages, and contributed to limited flooding issues.  Temperatures for the subsequent two storm systems remained mostly near to slightly above average in response to the warmer tap of sub-tropical moisture. Therefore, flooding issues increased for these two follow up AR driven storm systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS, CA-ED-51, located 2.0 mi N of Truckee, CA reported a 24-hour rainfall total of 1.05 inches and a snowfall total of 3.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.

Snow levels were high, with impacts limited to minor travel delays for Sierra pass levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This AR fed winter system shaped up to be a major heavy snow event for Mono, Mineral, and Southern Lyon Counties,  that extended south into Inyo and Esmeralda Counties. A deformation axis wrapped into the region with moist subtropical air transported by a moderate to strong AR that was directed into central and southern California. A strong southeast to east-southeast upper flow also aided in setting up the convergence zone in addition to upslope flow into the eastern Sierra. Impacts were considerable for routes between western and southern NV, that included closures of US-395, US-95, and Hwy-6 due to heavy snow in addition to strong south-southeast winds creating blowing and drifting snow and periods as well as near zero visibility. Dangerous wind chills occurred across the High Sierra with additional impacts for more vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 1.0 mi SSW of the Northstar Ski Base, CA, at the Homewood Mountain Ski Base, CA; the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, CA; and the Heavenly Ski Base, CA, reported storm total snowfall of 31 inches, 30 inches, 26 inches, and 23 inches respectively.

Sunday morning rain and snow began to spillover into western Nevada. Impacts included snow load on infrastructure, roofalanche potential, and travel /ski commute disruptions. Heavy, deep snow covering most road ways lead to extended road closures for many Mono county state highways and roads, especially along US 395 from Bridgeport to Mammoth Mountain valley.

Travel impacts were also expected for regional roadways which included Sierra mountain passes to include the I-80 corridor and along US-50 east of US-95 as rain changed to snow after sunset. Strong southwesterly winds were ongoing across the region with gusts exceeding 100 mph along Sierra ridgelines and widespread gusts to 50 mph in lower western Nevada valleys. Mono County was expected to see the snow last up to 15 hours with the heaviest snow area generally directed along the Sierra for about 9-12 hours, resulting in a solid swath of 1-2 feet along the crest, with some higher Mono County peaks projected to receive 30-36 inches of new snow. Through this challenging forecast period, brief whiteout conditions along with blowing/drifting snow were expected across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm continued to produce very heavy snowfall in the San Bernardino County Mountains on March 1st. The final round of snow with this storm resulted in additional snowfall totals of anywhere from a few inches at some mountain communities between 5000-6000 feet in elevation, to upwards of 2-3 feet at many ski resorts. This resulted in storm total snowfall amounts ranging from 9-12 feet in many ski resorts, 5-9 feet of snow in many mountain communities at or above 6000 feet in elevation, 2-5 feet of snow at elevations between 5000-6000 feet, 1-3 feet of snow at elevations between 3000-5000 feet, and some reports of accumulating snow at elevations as low as 1000 feet. Some notable reports from February 22 to March 1 include 150 at Snow Valley, 134 at Snow Summit on Bear Mountain, 115 at Mountain High near Mount Baldy, 108 near Lake Arrowhead (32 inches in 24 hours), End of Baldy Road at 6400 feet with 103 inches (40 inches in 24 hours February 25-26), 100 in Crestline (35 inches in 24 hours February 25-26), 95 in Arrowbear, 82 in Big Bear Lake yard, 76 in Forest Falls, 64 at Top of the Tram Mt San Jacinto 8400 feet, 63 Angelus Oaks, 56-62 in Wrightwood 5800 to 6200 feet, and 58 inches at Baldwin Lake (east Big Bear city) and Palomar Mountain at 5700 feet.  Multiple reports of roofs collapsing due to snow load were received from the Lake Arrowhead and Crestline areas, resulting in several rescues. The Goodwin Grocery Store in Crestline had its entire roof collapse. Recovery efforts from this winter storm spanned several weeks after the storm itself. State highway closures lasted up to 3 weeks including Highways 18 and 330. Several hundred rescues occurred, some by snowcat, of residence from February 28 to March 10. Gas service was interrupted to thousands with up to 700 service calls. There were 12 homes burned by fires related to gas pressure damage from the heavy snowfall. Only emergency personnel (up to 1000 at peak) for rescue and snow removal were allowed into the mountains on state highways through mid March. Initially helicopters from CalDART and California National Guard delivered food and supplies. Later meals were provided at selected locations for the entire month of March for those displaced or not able to drive. Ski areas were closed from a few days (Mt Baldy) to 2 1/2 weeks (Snow Valley).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet system brought periods of moderate to heavy rain with scattered thunderstorms. Lines of stronger thunderstorms brought local road flooding to portions of interior Northern California. There was also a confirmed EF-1 tornado touchdown in the Oroville area, with minor damage. Snow levels were high, with impacts limited to minor travel delays for Sierra pass levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported roadway flooding on I-80 Eastbound/Travis Blvd off-ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, even damaging west-southwest winds increased travel hazards especially to high profile trucks but also increased the danger for destructive avalanches across the region. Snow started at all elevations Thursday evening with snow levels rapidly rising Friday morning.  Heavy, wet snow (Sierra Nevada Cement) fell across higher elevation areas as snow levels rose to 7000 feet over the region.  Heavy rainfall increased the possibility of flooding for lower elevation creeks, streams, along with urban drainage issues due to snow berms. Significant rises on mainstem rivers were monitored for flooding. Deep, cold snowpack absorbed most liquid precipitation during this second AR supported storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Mammoth Mountain Ski Base,  a trained spotter located 1.0 mi SW of Toms Place, and the public located at June Mountain Ski base reported a 24-hour snowfall totals of 15.0, 10.0 (with 1.7 inches SWE), and 8.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system offshore the western US spun troughs over the region bringing periods of increased mountain snow and valley rain chances, as well as increased travel impacts through Sierra passes. Much of the precipitation was forecast to be confined to areas mostly south of I-80 with greatest snowfall staying along the Sierra crest in Alpine and Mono County. Rainfall totals were marginal for most lower elevation areas across western Nevada  outside of Mono and Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper low shifting eastward out of region leaving a moist and unstable airmass. The anomalous higher PWATS (90-95th percentile) and CAPE favored scattered thunderstorms across the region with heavy rainfall cores capable of flash flooding, gusty and erratic outflow winds, small hail, and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained weather spotter reported roughly an inch of accumulating hail (0.25 inch) in the Truckee, CA, area and along I-80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The start of a more active weather pattern culminated in the passage of a Pacific storm into the region. The incoming cold front brought strong, gusty west-southwest winds, increased chances for more widespread moderate rain showers,  and mountain snow. Snow-levels dropping to or near higher Sierra pass level brought increased travel difficulties. An incoming cold front and strong Pacific jet stream sustained the gusty conditions across the Sierra ridges into western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs station (CA-LS-2) located 3.5 mi SSW Susanville, CA, and a trained spotter located 5.0 mi WNW of Susanville, CA, reported storm total rainfall amounts of 0.99 and  0.81 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger two-part storm aided by an enhanced influx of subtropical moisture from a moderate atmospheric river continued the current series of Pacific winter storms passing through the region January 14-16. The initial thrust of AR enhanced moisture with the first storm brought heavy snowfall to the Sierra on 14 January with 2 to 3 feet of accumulating snow across higher elevations above 7000 feet.  An additional 15-30 inches of new snow fell across the California side of the Lake Tahoe Basin below 7000 feet with 8-16 inches falling on the Nevada side. Periods of heavy snow forced the closure of Sierra passes with the primary Sierra travel routes (I-80, US-50) being closed for much of the day. Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public at the Tahoe-Donner Ski Resort located 4.0 mi NW of Truckee, CA, reported 24 hour snowfall amounts of 10.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southwest winds, especially Monday evening through Tuesday night, brought the potential for possible travel disruptions, tree and fence damage, as well as local power outages. Mountain snow showers increased intensity during the evening above 7000 feet dumping heavy wet snow over mountain communities which brought heightened snow load  concerns for those areas. With the attendant stronger AR bringing relatively higher snow levels and more heavy rain to an already primed heavy snowpack, flood concerns further increased for larger streams and creeks, as well as for mainstream rivers. Temperatures for the first March AR supported storm system remained mostly below seasonal averages, and contributed to limited flooding issues.  Temperatures for the subsequent two storm systems remained mostly near to slightly above average in response to the warmer tap of sub-tropical moisture. Therefore, flooding issues increased for these two follow up AR driven storm systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Doyle (DYLC1) located 3.0 mi N of Doyle, CA reported a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (GRZC1) located 4.6 mi NE of Cromberg, CA, reported a rainfall storm total of 0.94 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter AR driven storm brought stronger winds especially for mountain tops, heavy Sierra snowfall, and valley rain or snow showers through the forecast period. Travel impacts were also expected for regional roadways which included Sierra mountain passes to include the I-80 corridor and along US-50 east of US-95 as rain changed to snow after sunset. Strong southwesterly winds were ongoing across the region with gusts exceeding 100 mph along Sierra ridgelines and widespread gusts to 50 mph in lower western Nevada valleys. Mono County was expected to see the snow last up to 15 hours with the heaviest snow area generally directed along the Sierra for about 9-12 hours, resulting in a solid swath of 1-2 feet along the crest, with some higher Mono County peaks projected to receive 30-36 inches of new snow. Through this challenging forecast period, brief whiteout conditions along with blowing/drifting snow were expected across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Blue Streak Weather Station (HVRTM) located 1.0 mi NNW of Heavenly Ski Summit reported a peak wind of 111 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southwest winds, especially Monday evening through Tuesday night, brought the potential for possible travel disruptions, tree and fence damage, as well as local power outages. Mountain snow showers increased intensity during the evening above 7000 feet dumping heavy wet snow over mountain communities which brought heightened snow load  concerns for those areas. With the attendant stronger AR bringing relatively higher snow levels and more heavy rain to an already primed heavy snowpack, flood concerns further increased for larger streams and creeks, as well as for mainstream rivers. Temperatures for the first March AR supported storm system remained mostly below seasonal averages, and contributed to limited flooding issues.  Temperatures for the subsequent two storm systems remained mostly near to slightly above average in response to the warmer tap of sub-tropical moisture. Therefore, flooding issues increased for these two follow up AR driven storm systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain Ski resort reported a peak wind of 102 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The start of a more active weather pattern culminated in the passage of a Pacific storm into the region. The incoming cold front brought strong, gusty west-southwest winds, increased chances for more widespread moderate rain showers,  and mountain snow. Snow-levels dropping to or near higher Sierra pass level brought increased travel difficulties. An incoming cold front and strong Pacific jet stream sustained the gusty conditions across the Sierra ridges into western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer (FARC1) located 2.3 mi NW of Floriston, CA, reported a total rainfall amount of 0.91 inches.

The anomalous higher PWATS (90-95th percentile) and CAPE favored scattered thunderstorms across the region with heavy rainfall cores capable of flash flooding, gusty and erratic outflow winds, small hail, and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lassen County Emergency Manager reported mudslide located 7.0 mi WNW of Honey Lake, CA, blocking southbound traffic along US-395 near Hicks Road within the Dixie Burn Scar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter AR driven storm brought stronger winds especially for mountain tops, heavy Sierra snowfall, and valley rain or snow showers through the forecast period. Travel impacts were also expected for regional roadways which included Sierra mountain passes to include the I-80 corridor and along US-50 east of US-95 as rain changed to snow after sunset. Strong southwesterly winds were ongoing across the region with gusts exceeding 100 mph along Sierra ridgelines and widespread gusts to 50 mph in lower western Nevada valleys. Mono County was expected to see the snow last up to 15 hours with the heaviest snow area generally directed along the Sierra for about 9-12 hours, resulting in a solid swath of 1-2 feet along the crest, with some higher Mono County peaks projected to receive 30-36 inches of new snow. Through this challenging forecast period, brief whiteout conditions along with blowing/drifting snow were expected across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Mammoth Mountain Ski Base and at June Mountain Ski Base reported a 24-hour snowfall totals of 30.0 and 22.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, even damaging west-southwest winds increased travel hazards especially to high profile trucks but also increased the danger for destructive avalanches across the region. Snow started at all elevations Thursday evening with snow levels rapidly rising Friday morning.  Heavy, wet snow (Sierra Nevada Cement) fell across higher elevation areas as snow levels rose to 7000 feet over the region.  Heavy rainfall increased the possibility of flooding for lower elevation creeks, streams, along with urban drainage issues due to snow berms. Significant rises on mainstem rivers were monitored for flooding. Deep, cold snowpack absorbed most liquid precipitation during this second AR supported storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS, CA-MN-6, located 5.0 mi NNW of Lee Vining, CA reported a 24-hour snowfall total of  9.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dropped into the Great Basin from the Pacific Northwest on April 3. This resulted in a period of strong winds across the ridge tops and downslope prone areas over the Sierra Nevada south of Sequoia Park and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several stations measured wind gusts exceeding 60 mph while a few low impact indicator stations measured gusts above 70 mph. This system also produced light precipitation over the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains with several SNOTEL stations above 6000 feet picking up between 2 and 5 inches of estimated new snowfall. Some light snowfall was also observed near the Tejon Pass along Interstate 5 during the morning of April 3. The trough moved east of the area by the morning of April 4 giving way to a warming and drying trend across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Tehachapi East Valley measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of cold fronts brought strong westerly winds on February 13 with winds turning more northerly Saturday. This double shot of winter weather impacted larger area lakes with rough, choppy conditions as well as very turbulent conditions for aviation interests, and strong crosswinds for high profile vehicles traveling along area roadways.  Some snow showers developed across the area east of the eastern Sierra into western Nevada south of Highway 50. But snow accumulations were generally spotty, as well as  light to moderate, with limited travel impacts except for brief reductions in visibility in heavier showers.  Area temperatures plunged behind the quickly moving cold fronts with daytime highs for warmer lower valley locations struggling to rise above freezing.  The colder, brisk northerly flow brought frigid wind chill temperatures overnight February 14 that dipped into the single digits for most valley locations. Higher Sierra elevations saw dangerous wind chills plunging into the  -20 to -30 degree through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01) Mammoth Mountain located 0.9 mi S of the Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, CA, and mesonet station (DDSUM) located at Conway Summit reported a maximum wind gusts of 127 mph and 73 mph respectively.

There were embedded thunderstorms with small hail in the foothills and heavy snow showers in the mountains. After a long period of minor flooding in March, the San Joaquin River at Vernalis receded from flood stage at 0700 on April 4, dropping into Monitor Stage.

UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 6 inches (6894 feet). Sugar Bowl Ski Resort reported 9 inches of new snow at the summit (8383 feet). Sierra at Tahoe Ski resort reported 4 inches of new snow (8852 feet). Kirkwood Ski Resort reported 5 inches of new snow (7690 feet). The snow brought chain controls and delays on Interstate 80 and US Highway 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm shaped out to be the strongest of our recent series of late February winter storms. A low positioned off-shore the Pacific Northwest set a favorable pattern for the transport of colder northern Pacific moisture that wrapped into an attendant cold front. This storm possessed a combination of a cold air mass, sufficient moisture, strong winds, instability, and dynamic forcing to produce heavy Sierra snowfall with periods of blizzard conditions Monday through early Wednesday. With snow rations approaching 15-17:1, the light fluffy snowfall coupled with wind gusts exceeding 50 mph brought near zero visibility at times to Sierra communities Monday afternoon through Tuesday afternoon. Sierra ridge tops saw wind gusts over 100 mph through this period. This storm system also contributed to prolonged winter travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mammoth Mountain Ski Base and June Mountain Ski Base reported a 24 hour snowfall totals of 20.0 inches and 15.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough in the Gulf of Alaska moved southeast across Washington, Oregon, and northern California with a trio of fronts.  Snow levels gradually lowered with each disturbance.  Eventually, light valley snow was produced. But, warning amounts were limited to elevations above 2500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National gridded snowfall analysis indicated 4 to 13 inches of snow at elevations above 2500 feet, with heaviest amounts west and southwest of Happy Camp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm shaped out to be the strongest of our recent series of late February winter storms. A low positioned off-shore the Pacific Northwest set a favorable pattern for the transport of colder northern Pacific moisture that wrapped into an attendant cold front. This storm possessed a combination of a cold air mass, sufficient moisture, strong winds, instability, and dynamic forcing to produce heavy Sierra snowfall with periods of blizzard conditions Monday through early Wednesday. With snow rations approaching 15-17:1, the light fluffy snowfall coupled with wind gusts exceeding 50 mph brought near zero visibility at times to Sierra communities Monday afternoon through Tuesday afternoon. Sierra ridge tops saw wind gusts over 100 mph through this period. This storm system also contributed to prolonged winter travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (TADC1) Tahoe-Donner located 4.0 mi WNW of  Truckee, CA,  reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave caused onshore winds to increase and become gusty across Southern California on April 3rd. The strongest winds were generally observed in the mountains, below mountain passes, and through wind-prone canyon locations. Some isolated instances of wind damage were also observed in coastal Southern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind impacted the San Gorgonio Pass near Banning on April 3rd. Wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common, with localized higher gusts. The Whitewater RAWS site recorded a peak wind gust of 73 mph, while the Desert Hot Springs SoCal-Edison mesonet site recorded a gust to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dropped into the Great Basin from the Pacific Northwest on April 3. This resulted in a period of strong winds across the ridge tops and downslope prone areas over the Sierra Nevada south of Sequoia Park and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several stations measured wind gusts exceeding 60 mph while a few low impact indicator stations measured gusts above 70 mph. This system also produced light precipitation over the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains with several SNOTEL stations above 6000 feet picking up between 2 and 5 inches of estimated new snowfall. Some light snowfall was also observed near the Tejon Pass along Interstate 5 during the morning of April 3. The trough moved east of the area by the morning of April 4 giving way to a warming and drying trend across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Valley RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 94 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching strong upper level low brought widespread damaging winds to the Bay Area and Central Coast on February 21. A Wind Advisory was issued for the entire region in advance of the event, and was upgraded to a High Wind Warning along the coast and in the higher elevations of the Central Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sweeney Ridge weather station operated by PGE reported a wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder early Spring storm brought another round of winter-like conditions to the region with strong winds today and snow showers. Impacts to travel and recreation included blowing dust, lake wind advisories for stronger breezes and choppy conditions for area lakes, and aviation concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01) Mammoth Mountain located 0.9 mi S of the Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, CA, and mesonet station (XMLC1) Mill Canyon located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA, reported maximum wind gusts of 134 mph, and 88 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early monsoonal moisture push occurred during the middle of May across Southern California. The heaviest activity occurred in the High Desert, where several reports of flooding were documented. Hot and humid conditions also occurred region-wide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey conducted by WFO San Diego. Several sections of Mountain View Road were covered in mud as much as two feet deep with damage on the side pavement. El Mirage Raws recorded 1.23 inches of rain during the afternoon on May 21. Radar total estimates of 2-4 inches occurred in surrounding areas. Standing water along El Mirage Road with one home surrounded by water. A nearby dry lake bed was partially water logged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall across Southern California March 14-15. Rain amounts of 1 to 3 inches were observed across the coastal basin, with heavier amounts up to 4 inches reported in the mountains. Rain fell on an extremely deep snowpack in the mountains, resulting in flooding. Flooding also occurred across the coast and valleys, where several debris flows occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighters responded to a vehicle submerged in 1 foot of water at Matthews Road and Palomar Road. Four occupants were rescued from the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy rain with with flooding of roadways,  streams and creeks, with mudslides also reported. There was continued river flooding along the already elevated Sacramento, San Joaquin, Cosumnes, Mokelumne, and Tuolumne rivers. The storm brought dangerous mountain travel conditions with snow levels around down to 2000 feet, locally 1000 feet in Shasta County. Storm totals reached 1 to 3 feet of above 5000 feet. Winds gusted up to 35-50 mph in the Central Valley. In the mountains there were gusts up to 50 to 70 mph, which coupled with heavy snow, brought whiteout conditions. The were numerous road closures, including Interstate 80, Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, as well as State Routes 88, 89 and 299. Widespread trees down across the area blocked roads and caused power outages. There were thunderstorms on the 28th with a funnel cloud reported visible from the tower at Redding Airport.  There was a fatality reported when man was trapped by a collapsed snow bank next to his home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ski resorts reported storm total snow amounts of 23 inches at Kirkwood, 19 inches at Sugar Bowl, and 17 inches at Sierra at Tahoe. Caltrans District 3 reported storm total snow amounts of 23.5 inches at Kingvale, 21 inches at Soda Springs, and 18 inches at Eagle Lakes. The National Operational Hydrological Remote Sensing Center's gridded snowfall analysis estimated 6 to 30 inches over the west slope northern Sierra Nevada over 72 hours. 1 death was reported by broadcast media in Alta, CA where a man died from a collapsed snow bank, trapping him next to a generator and exposing him to carbon monoxide. Interstate 80 was closed intermittently on 3/28 due to winter weather conditions and multiple spinouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds impacted sections of Southwestern California. The strongest winds, gusts up to 77 MPH, occurred in the mountains and across San Luis Obispo county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds impacted the Santa Lucia Mountains in San Luis Obispo county.  Some peak wind gusts from the local mesonet include: TV Towers (gust 66 MPH) and Green Valley Road (gust 58 MPH).

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on March 28 and 29 producing heavy snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada along with a brief period strong wind gusts over portions of the Sierra foothills. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 16 to 24 inches of new snowfall with several stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of liquid precipitation while the foothills picked up between 1 and 2 inches and rainfall and the San Joaquin generally picked up another 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rainfall before the precipitation tapered off as the trough moved inland during the afternoon and evening of March 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gin Flat SNOTEL (7201 feet) picked up an estimated 20 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a car spun out in high waters and was stuck in mud on E. Goodfellow Ave. near S. Channel Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and wet system brought periods of heavy rain with flooding. Snow levels were high, with 1 to 2 feet of snow accumulating above 6,500 feet, locally more on higher peaks. Heavy rain on already saturated areas fell bringing additional roadway flooding, as well as local mudslides. Evacuations were ordered in San Joaquin County due to flooding. Thunderstorms with large hail, strong damaging winds and funnel clouds were reported. Strong winds gusting to 40 to 58 mph brought down trees and powerlines across the area, causing power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Joaquin County Sheriff issued evacuation orders for 2 locations along the San Joaquin River starting on March 19, 2023. Those two locations include Airport Court, Manteca and Haven Acres Marina in Lathrop due to heavy rain and snowmelt. Evacuation orders were lifted on April 11, 2023.

With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report showing Riverside Park in Kernville flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cyclonic flow behind the departed trough that brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the area on March 21 and 22 produced downslope winds across the Kern County Deserts during the evening of March 23 and the morning of March 24. The winds were most noticible along the Mojave Desert Slopes where several stations measured gusts above 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Paradise Cove measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved across the region on March 21 and 22. This system produced strong winds across the West Side Hills, the Sierra Nevada and across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several stations measured gusts exceeding 60 mph. In addition, heavy snow fell in the Sierra Nevada where several high elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snow while liquid precipitation totals were generally between 1 and 2 inches. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of additional rainfall over an already saturated ground while thunderstorm outflow winds knocked down several trees in and around Fresno during the afternoon of March 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Joaquin Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on March 28 and 29 producing heavy snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada along with a brief period strong wind gusts over portions of the Sierra foothills. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 16 to 24 inches of new snowfall with several stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of liquid precipitation while the foothills picked up between 1 and 2 inches and rainfall and the San Joaquin generally picked up another 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rainfall before the precipitation tapered off as the trough moved inland during the afternoon and evening of March 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chilkoot Meadow SNOTEL (7150 feet) picked up an estimated 20 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported water and debris flooding SR 41 in the southern part of Oakhurst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak atmospheric river produced widespread moderate rainfall and high-elevation snow across Southern California March 29-30. Several stronger thunderstorm cells occurred across San Diego County, resulting in a Severe Thunderstorm Warning and a Tornado Warning. No damage was reported with these warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of precipitation impacted the San Bernardino County Mountains on March 29-30. The first round occurred during the day on March 29 had snow levels near 6000-7000 feet, before falling to 3500-4000 feet Wednesday night and Thursday with the second round of precipitation. The heaviest snow accumulations generally occurred above 6000 feet, where accumulations of 6-12 inches were observed. Lower elevations down to 4000 feet saw accumulations of 2-6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak atmospheric river produced widespread moderate rainfall and high-elevation snow across Southern California March 29-30. Several stronger thunderstorm cells occurred across San Diego County, resulting in a Severe Thunderstorm Warning and a Tornado Warning. No damage was reported with these warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the San Diego County Mountains late in the evening on March 29, persisting through the afternoon on March 30. The heaviest snow accumulations generally occurred above 5500 feet, where accumulations of 8-12 inches were observed. Lower elevations down to 4000 feet saw accumulations of 1-7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the warm and moist storm which produced widespread flooding across the area on March 9 and 10, another moist system moved through central California on March 11 and 12. While precipitation amounts were generally much lower with this system with most of the Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation and adjacent foothills picking up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall, the cooler unstable airmass on the back side of this system produced afternoon and evening thunderstorms across the lower elevations resulting in widespread flooding and wind damage across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Image posted on Facebook showing flooding of the Friant Kern Canal at SR 198.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Temesecal Valley reported storm total rainfall of 1.37 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak atmospheric river produced widespread moderate rainfall and high-elevation snow across Southern California March 29-30. Several stronger thunderstorm cells occurred across San Diego County, resulting in a Severe Thunderstorm Warning and a Tornado Warning. No damage was reported with these warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the Riverside County Mountains in two rounds: the first occurring through the day on Wednesday with snow levels near 6500 feet, and the second late Wednesday night through Thursday afternoon, when snow levels fell to 3500-4500 feet. Snow accumulations of 1 to 4 inches were reported in the mountains.
