EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reports potential wind damage near three homes, with downed powerlines and a damaged, though non-secure, tent. A small shower with weak rotation moved over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak atmospheric river produced widespread moderate rainfall and high-elevation snow across Southern California March 29-30. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported roadway flooding at SR-74W and College Grove Avenue, with 0.45-0.55 inch of rain reported in the previous 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong winter storm impacted Southwestern California. The storm brought winter storm conditions the local mountains with snowfall totals of 2 to 4 feet and southerly winds gusting in excess of 80 MPH. Other areas also experienced some strong winds. Additionally, scattered thunderstorms developed across the area, spawning two weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado developed in the community of Montebello. The EF1 tornado impacted the area around the intersection of Washington Boulevard and Maple Avenue. There was substantial damage to the industrial warehouse and commercial business district, including nearly total roof collapse in one building and a total of 17 damaged structures. Numerous vehicles were damaged due to flying debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers continued across the area during the morning of March 1 behind the storm which had brought rain and snow to the area during the previous two days. Heavier showers resulted in several reports of roadway flooding in the Fresno area while snow showers produced accumulating snowfall over the Tejon and Tehachapi Passes which resulted in the passes on both Interstate 5 and SR 58 being closed for much of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a tree across the roadway on E. Pontiac Way west of N. Maroa Ave in Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the warm and moist storm which produced widespread flooding across the area on March 9 and 10, another moist system moved through central California on March 11 and 12. While precipitation amounts were generally much lower with this system with most of the Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation and adjacent foothills picking up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall, the cooler unstable airmass on the back side of this system produced afternoon and evening thunderstorms across the lower elevations resulting in widespread flooding and wind damage across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media report on twitter showing a funnel cloud near the intersection of SR 180 and Panoche Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved across the region on March 21 and 22. This system produced strong winds across the West Side Hills, the Sierra Nevada and across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several stations measured gusts exceeding 60 mph. In addition, heavy snow fell in the Sierra Nevada where several high elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snow while liquid precipitation totals were generally between 1 and 2 inches. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of additional rainfall over an already saturated ground while thunderstorm outflow winds knocked down several trees in and around Fresno during the afternoon of March 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Beartrap Road measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward off the California coast on March 28 and 29 producing heavy snowfall over the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada along with a brief period strong wind gusts over portions of the Sierra foothills. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 16 to 24 inches of new snowfall with several stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of liquid precipitation while the foothills picked up between 1 and 2 inches and rainfall and the San Joaquin generally picked up another 0.25 to 0.75 inches of rainfall before the precipitation tapered off as the trough moved inland during the afternoon and evening of March 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Yosemite Pilot Peak measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresno County Fire Department reported water water over a bridge on Hopewell Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy rain with with flooding of roadways,  streams and creeks, with mudslides also reported. There was continued river flooding along the already elevated Sacramento, San Joaquin, Cosumnes, Mokelumne, and Tuolumne rivers. The storm brought dangerous mountain travel conditions with snow levels around down to 2000 feet, locally 1000 feet in Shasta County. Storm totals reached 1 to 3 feet of above 5000 feet. Winds gusted up to 35-50 mph in the Central Valley. In the mountains there were gusts up to 50 to 70 mph, which coupled with heavy snow, brought whiteout conditions. The were numerous road closures, including Interstate 80, Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, as well as State Routes 88, 89 and 299. Widespread trees down across the area blocked roads and caused power outages. There were thunderstorms on the 28th with a funnel cloud reported visible from the tower at Redding Airport.  There was a fatality reported when man was trapped by a collapsed snow bank next to his home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Operational Hydrological Remote Sensing Center's gridded snowfall analysis estimated 6 to 24 inches over Shasta Lake and northern Shasta County over 72 hours. A trained spotter at Sims at an elevation of 2500 ft reported 14 inches in 24 hours. Caltrans reported the closure of Interstate 5 northbound at Fawndale for about 10 hours due to winter weather conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS reported storm total amounts of 36 inches in Chester at 4526 ft, 38 inches at Meadow Valley at an elevation of 4380 ft, and 28 inches at Shingletown at an elevation of 3630 ft. The National Operational Hydrological Remote Sensing Center's gridded snowfall analysis estimated 12 to 36 inches over the Lassen Park and western Plumas County area over 72 hours. Lassen National Park reported 22 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm continued to impact the San Diego County Mountains on March 1st, producing heavy snowfall across the region. Additional snowfall of 1-2 feet occurred mainly near Palomar Mountain and Mount Laguna during this time. Storm total snowfall of around 58 was observed on Palomar Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers continued across the area during the morning of March 1 behind the storm which had brought rain and snow to the area during the previous two days. Heavier showers resulted in several reports of roadway flooding in the Fresno area while snow showers produced accumulating snowfall over the Tejon and Tehachapi Passes which resulted in the passes on both Interstate 5 and SR 58 being closed for much of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that flooding caused a spinout on SR 49 near Knickerbocker Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped below the freezing mark across portions of the central and eastern San Joaquin Valley for a few hours during the morning of March 2 as a cold airmass settled into the area with clearing skies and light winds prevailing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 31 degrees on March 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the warm and moist storm which produced widespread flooding across the area on March 9 and 10, another moist system moved through central California on March 11 and 12. While precipitation amounts were generally much lower with this system with most of the Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation and adjacent foothills picking up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall, the cooler unstable airmass on the back side of this system produced afternoon and evening thunderstorms across the lower elevations resulting in widespread flooding and wind damage across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported China Grade Loop west of Airport Dr. was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved across the region on March 21 and 22. This system produced strong winds across the West Side Hills, the Sierra Nevada and across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several stations measured gusts exceeding 60 mph. In addition, heavy snow fell in the Sierra Nevada where several high elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snow while liquid precipitation totals were generally between 1 and 2 inches. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of additional rainfall over an already saturated ground while thunderstorm outflow winds knocked down several trees in and around Fresno during the afternoon of March 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Tejon Overlook measured a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting snow brought unusually strong currents and cold water to area waterways in the month of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 22-year-old woman drowned in the Clavey River on May 29th, with her body found downstream of Clavey Bridge on June 3rd. Warm weather was occurring with highs in the region in the lower 70s, while area waterways were cold with strong currents from mountain snowmelt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cold upper level storm system caused snow to fall at several mountain communities on May 3rd and 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell at several communities in the San Bernardino County Mountains from the evening on May 3rd through the afternoon on the 4th. Communities at higher elevations near Mount Baldy and Running Springs received total snowfall of 7-8 inches. Numerous other communities including Big Bear Lake, Lake Arrowhead, and Forest Falls received anywhere from 1-5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of high astronomical tides and high surf led to instances of coastal flooding near Seal Beach on the evening of May 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media observed and reported coastal flooding near Seal Beach Pier up to 18 inches deep from high tide and high surf. At least one car was swamped. No confirmed damage to homes or businesses.

This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from CalTrans: SR-74 at Blackburn Road was closed due to the road being completely covered with mud about 1 foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved across the region on March 21 and 22. This system produced strong winds across the West Side Hills, the Sierra Nevada and across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several stations measured gusts exceeding 60 mph. In addition, heavy snow fell in the Sierra Nevada where several high elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snow while liquid precipitation totals were generally between 1 and 2 inches. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of additional rainfall over an already saturated ground while thunderstorm outflow winds knocked down several trees in and around Fresno during the afternoon of March 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kettlemen Hills RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported Lodge Rd. at Wiemiller Rd. flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy rain with with flooding of roadways,  streams and creeks, with mudslides also reported. There was continued river flooding along the already elevated Sacramento, San Joaquin, Cosumnes, Mokelumne, and Tuolumne rivers. The storm brought dangerous mountain travel conditions with snow levels around down to 2000 feet, locally 1000 feet in Shasta County. Storm totals reached 1 to 3 feet of above 5000 feet. Winds gusted up to 35-50 mph in the Central Valley. In the mountains there were gusts up to 50 to 70 mph, which coupled with heavy snow, brought whiteout conditions. The were numerous road closures, including Interstate 80, Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, as well as State Routes 88, 89 and 299. Widespread trees down across the area blocked roads and caused power outages. There were thunderstorms on the 28th with a funnel cloud reported visible from the tower at Redding Airport.  There was a fatality reported when man was trapped by a collapsed snow bank next to his home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding halfway into the slow lane on SR 99 near highway 50 eastbound junction due to heavy rain.

By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding on E. Fir St. between Foothill Ave. and Hillcrest Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video posted on Twitter showing flooding on Burrough Valley Rd. near Quartz Ln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy rain with with flooding of roadways,  streams and creeks, with mudslides also reported. There was continued river flooding along the already elevated Sacramento, San Joaquin, Cosumnes, Mokelumne, and Tuolumne rivers. The storm brought dangerous mountain travel conditions with snow levels around down to 2000 feet, locally 1000 feet in Shasta County. Storm totals reached 1 to 3 feet of above 5000 feet. Winds gusted up to 35-50 mph in the Central Valley. In the mountains there were gusts up to 50 to 70 mph, which coupled with heavy snow, brought whiteout conditions. The were numerous road closures, including Interstate 80, Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, as well as State Routes 88, 89 and 299. Widespread trees down across the area blocked roads and caused power outages. There were thunderstorms on the 28th with a funnel cloud reported visible from the tower at Redding Airport.  There was a fatality reported when man was trapped by a collapsed snow bank next to his home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on Scott Road and Latrobe Road by Indian Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting snow brought unusually strong currents and cold water to area waterways in the month of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 21-year-old man drowned in the Feather River after diving off of the Magalia Head Dam. His body was found down-stream the next day. Afternoon temperatures were 67-72 degrees, with water temperatures in the area ranging from 45-55 degrees that afternoon. Winds were 5-10 mph, and the West Branch Feather River - Magalia gauge flow was around 1540 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low that settled into the west coast the first few days of May brought showers and an increased chance (20-30%) of thunderstorms that also lowered rain-snow lines to 5500-7000 feet by early morning 3 May. Stronger showers and storms produced a mix of rain, snow, and ice pellets which lead to runoff issues in the lower elevations. High end chances (10-20%) of rainfall totals were expected to reach 0.50-1.00 inch. Although snow was projected to fall in the Sierra, the combination of warm road temperatures and the early May sun angle limited accumulations on paved surfaces. But bursts of convective heavy snow showers and/or snow occurring during the overnight hours were expected to bring overnight travel impacts to mountain passes. Tightening thermal and pressure gradients with the incoming low produced gusty south-southeast winds and travel impacts for portions of Churchill, Mineral, Lyon, and Mono Counties along US-395 and US-95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Heavenly Ski Base reported 24-hour snowfall totals of 7.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 1-inch tree limbs broken and roof shingles blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved eastward across central California on March 4 and March 5. This system produced snow at elevations down to 2000 feet with some higher elevation  SNOTELS north of Kings Canyon picking up between a foot and 2 feet of new snowfall. Thunderstorms broke out in the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening of March 5 producing widespread nuisance flooding and small hail. In addition, outflow gusts from some of the thunderstorms downed some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree in the roadway on northbound SR 99 south of the Road 284 off ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the warm and moist storm which produced widespread flooding across the area on March 9 and 10, another moist system moved through central California on March 11 and 12. While precipitation amounts were generally much lower with this system with most of the Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation and adjacent foothills picking up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall, the cooler unstable airmass on the back side of this system produced afternoon and evening thunderstorms across the lower elevations resulting in widespread flooding and wind damage across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee measured inch sized diameter hail east of Firebaugh on Hemlock Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved across the region on March 21 and 22. This system produced strong winds across the West Side Hills, the Sierra Nevada and across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several stations measured gusts exceeding 60 mph. In addition, heavy snow fell in the Sierra Nevada where several high elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snow while liquid precipitation totals were generally between 1 and 2 inches. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of additional rainfall over an already saturated ground while thunderstorm outflow winds knocked down several trees in and around Fresno during the afternoon of March 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Burnt Corral SNOTEL (9700 feet) picked up an estimated 23 inches of new snowfall.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm brought snowfall down to lower elevations in the Inland Empire on March 1st. Areas near Riverside, Chino, Moreno Valley, Corona, and Perris observed rain changing to snow, though much of the snow in these areas melted on impact with the ground. Areas near Yucaipa, Banning, and Beaumont reported accumulations of up to 1 inch occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the warm and moist storm which produced widespread flooding across the area on March 9 and 10, another moist system moved through central California on March 11 and 12. While precipitation amounts were generally much lower with this system with most of the Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation and adjacent foothills picking up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall, the cooler unstable airmass on the back side of this system produced afternoon and evening thunderstorms across the lower elevations resulting in widespread flooding and wind damage across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree on E. Adams Ave. at S. Maple Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved across the region on March 21 and 22. This system produced strong winds across the West Side Hills, the Sierra Nevada and across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several stations measured gusts exceeding 60 mph. In addition, heavy snow fell in the Sierra Nevada where several high elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snow while liquid precipitation totals were generally between 1 and 2 inches. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of additional rainfall over an already saturated ground while thunderstorm outflow winds knocked down several trees in and around Fresno during the afternoon of March 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The State Lakes SNOTEL (10419 feet) picked up an estimated 27 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Volcanic Knob SNOTEL (10050 feet) picked up an estimated 24 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical jet stream aided in advecting sub-tropical moisture plume from over the Central Pacific into the California Coast starting March 9th through the 11th. This led to widespread flooding around the CWA, numerous landslides, sharp rises in rivers in Monterey County, and widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines due to strong, gusty winds. Widespread wind gusts 45 to 55 mph were reported on the evening of the 9th and into the early hours of the 10th. Heavy rains during the same time flooded numerous streams, creeks, rivers, and roads. 4-7 inches of rain fell across the Santa Cruz Mountains while 5-10 inches of rain fell on the Santa Lucia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a warehouse on 650 85th Ave in Oakland collapsed at 3:20 AM. A 57-year old man was struck by falling debris and died; a woman suffered a broken nose. KTVU television reported that city inspectors found that water had been ponding on the roof and drains were not properly cleaned.

In addition, heavy snow fell in the Sierra Nevada where several high elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snow while liquid precipitation totals were generally between 1 and 2 inches. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of additional rainfall over an already saturated ground while thunderstorm outflow winds knocked down several trees in and around Fresno during the afternoon of March 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wet Meadows SNOTEL (9019 feet) picked up an estimated 23 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The State Lakes SNOTEL (10419 feet) picked up an estimated 24 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the recent events which produced widespread flooding across the central California Interior, another atmospheric river plunged through the region on March 14 and 15 producing heavy precipitation, flooding and strong winds with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up another 3 to 6 inches of liquid precipitation with snow levels above 8500 feet while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills generally picked up between 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of SR 178 were closed because of mud slides through the canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall across Southern California March 14-15. Rain amounts of 1 to 3 inches were observed across the coastal basin, with heavier amounts up to 4 inches reported in the mountains. Rain fell on an extremely deep snowpack in the mountains, resulting in flooding. Flooding also occurred across the coast and valleys, where several debris flows occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall occurred across the Inland Empire from the evening on March 14 through the morning on March 15. Rainfall of 2.00-3.50 fell, with local amounts up to 4 inches closer to the San Bernardino County Mountains. Flash flooding was reported early on the morning of March 15; firefighters responded to a vehicle in swift moving water, and the rescue was successful. Also, Grove Ave between Merrill Ave, Chino and Riverside Ave in Ontario saw 2 to 4 inches of running water covering the entire roadway. Kimball Ave in Chino between Quality Way and Euclid Ave were flooded with 2 to 4 inches of standing water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall fell across eastern portions of the Inland Empire the evening of March 14 through the afternoon on March 15. Rainfall of 1.00-2.25 was reported, and multiple reports of flooding were received via the Department of Highways: Cherry Valley Blvd at Roberts Road was flooded, as well as three lanes of Highway 60.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy rain with with flooding of roadways,  streams and creeks, with mudslides also reported. There was continued river flooding along the already elevated Sacramento, San Joaquin, Cosumnes, Mokelumne, and Tuolumne rivers. The storm brought dangerous mountain travel conditions with snow levels around down to 2000 feet, locally 1000 feet in Shasta County. Storm totals reached 1 to 3 feet of above 5000 feet. Winds gusted up to 35-50 mph in the Central Valley. In the mountains there were gusts up to 50 to 70 mph, which coupled with heavy snow, brought whiteout conditions. The were numerous road closures, including Interstate 80, Interstate 5 north of Fawndale, as well as State Routes 88, 89 and 299. Widespread trees down across the area blocked roads and caused power outages. There were thunderstorms on the 28th with a funnel cloud reported visible from the tower at Redding Airport.  There was a fatality reported when man was trapped by a collapsed snow bank next to his home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports on social media showed an estimated 2 inches of snow on the ground in Grass Valley with more snow falling. The National Operational Hydrological Remote Sensing Center's gridded snowfall analysis estimated 1 to 8 inches of storm total snow over portions of the the northeast foothills. CoCoRaHS reported 3.1 inches of snow near Nevada City at an elevation of 2390 ft, and 7 inches near Grass Valley at an elevation of 2782 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near El Cajon reported storm total rainfall of 0.75 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the warm and moist storm which produced widespread flooding across the area on March 9 and 10, another moist system moved through central California on March 11 and 12. While precipitation amounts were generally much lower with this system with most of the Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation and adjacent foothills picking up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall, the cooler unstable airmass on the back side of this system produced afternoon and evening thunderstorms across the lower elevations resulting in widespread flooding and wind damage across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree and power lines which caught fire at S. Clovis Ave. north of E. Floral Ave.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Cottonwood Creek West measured a peak wind gust of 81 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway under 2 feet of mud near Sunflower Ln. at George Smith Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the warm and moist storm which produced widespread flooding across the area on March 9 and 10, another moist system moved through central California on March 11 and 12. While precipitation amounts were generally much lower with this system with most of the Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation and adjacent foothills picking up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall, the cooler unstable airmass on the back side of this system produced afternoon and evening thunderstorms across the lower elevations resulting in widespread flooding and wind damage across the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding on Highland Ave. at Dinuba Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough pushed into central California and moved through the region on March 19. This system brought 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada, a quarter inch to an inch of rain to the Sierra foothills and a tenth to quarter inch of rain to much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills. This system cam in warmer than anticipated and the snow level ranged from 6500 to 7000 feet with this system. Several higher elevation SNOTELS in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 8 to 16 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of an estimated 8 to 16 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough off the Pacific Northwest coast pulled up a deep moisture fetch from the Pacific Ocean which spread inland into central California on March 9 and 10. This atmospheric river event produced 4 to 8 inches of liquid precipitation across much of the Sierra Nevada. With this moisture being of tropical origin, snow levels were high and were above 9500 feet for much of this event. The rain falling on top of a large snowpack over the Sierra Nevada produced a large amount of runoff resulting in widespread catastrophic flooding over the San Joaquin Valley, the valley facing slopes as well as across the Sierra foothills with over 100 reports of flooding being reported to WFO Hanford. The conditions were made worse by the fact that soils already very saturated over the lower elevations from previous storms. In addition, the Sierra foothills generally picked up between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley had between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. Flash Flood Emergencies were issued for the Springville area in Tulare County and for the Kernville and Wofford Heights area in Kern County near Lake Isabella for life threatening flooding caused by heavy rainfall on top of snowmelt runoff from higher elevations. By the time the deep moisture fetch moved out of the area during the evening of March 10, several communities were evacuated due to the widespread flooding and numerous roads were closed for several days either because of flooding or because repairs were required to make the roads usable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported mud, dirt and rocks on Elwood Rd. near Boren Ln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the recent events which produced widespread flooding across the central California Interior, another atmospheric river plunged through the region on March 14 and 15 producing heavy precipitation, flooding and strong winds with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up another 3 to 6 inches of liquid precipitation with snow levels above 8500 feet while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall while the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills generally picked up between 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Pelato Peak measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river produced widespread moderate to heavy rainfall across Southern California March 14-15. Rain amounts of 1 to 3 inches were observed across the coastal basin, with heavier amounts up to 4 inches reported in the mountains. Rain fell on an extremely deep snowpack in the mountains, resulting in flooding. Flooding also occurred across the coast and valleys, where several debris flows occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mudslides, rockslides and debris flows were reported across western portions of the San Bernardino County Mountains on March 15 as widespread heavy rainfall of 1.00-3.50+ inches occurred. Rockslides occurred along the upper passing lanes on SR-330 near Running Springs; mud and rocks blocked the upbound lanes of Highway 18 at Old Waterman Canyon Road near Crestline; rocks were reported across the road on SR-18 near Crestline, and a vehicle reportedly hit the rocks.

Heavy rain combined with snow melt to lead to flooding in the San Bernardino County Mountains. The Department of Highways reported the closure of Gildart Drive and Division Drive; Irvine Way and Barrett Way were flooded; the riverbed broke through and resulted in flooding at SR-38, with 3 inches of standing water reported between Baldwin and State Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After over 100 inches of snow fell in this area, an avalanche occurred on Mount Baldy at approximately 5:30 AM PST. According to reports, about 15 feet of snow came down off the mountain and blocked portions of Mount Baldy Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche occurred on Mount Baldy at approximately 5:30 AM PST. According to reports from local media, about 15 feet of snow came down off the mountain and blocked portions of Mount Baldy Road as well as the ski resort's parking lot. This caused closures of not just the road, but also at Mount Baldy Ski Resort which trapped residents above the avalanche area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one powerful storm struck Southern California in late February, a second storm arrived at the end of February and into early March. This second storm produced several additional feet of snow as well as high winds in the mountains, where many communities were already struggling due to snow and wind impacts from the first storm. This led to many gas leaks from failing lines that started home fires, extended road closures, numerous power outages, many downed trees, about 250 rescues from mountains in the area, as well as at least one confirmed storm-related death, and an extensive recovery process that lasted well beyond the event. About 12 persons were reported dead, though the causes of the other 11 are uncertain at this time. A Presidential Major Disaster Declaration was approved following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt Baldy ski resort was closed due to avalanche in Bowl Alley near the entrance to the ski area or the end of Mt Baldy road. The area received 110 inches of snow in the prior 7 days at 6500 feet and close to 150 inches at 8000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and wet system brought periods of heavy rain with flooding. Snow levels were high, with 1 to 2 feet of snow accumulating above 6,500 feet, locally more on higher peaks. Heavy rain on already saturated areas fell bringing additional roadway flooding, as well as local mudslides. Evacuations were ordered in San Joaquin County due to flooding. Thunderstorms with large hail, strong damaging winds and funnel clouds were reported. Strong winds gusting to 40 to 58 mph brought down trees and powerlines across the area, causing power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail, measured at 0.88 inches, associated with a thunderstorm.

Thunderstorms with large hail, strong damaging winds and funnel clouds were reported. Strong winds gusting to 40 to 58 mph brought down trees and powerlines across the area, causing power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab reported storm total amounts of 15.5 inches of snow, Bear Valley Resort reported 17 inches of storm total snow, Dodge Ridge Summit reported 36 inches of storm total snow, Kirkwood reported 20 inches of storm total snow, Sierra-at-Tahoe reported 21 inches of storm total snow, and Sugar Bowl Resort reported 19 inches of storm total snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of atmospheric river systems impacted Interior Northern California through the month of March and produced substantial flooding and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glenn County completed a preliminary estimate of damage from heavy rainfall and flooding. There was substantial flooding, resulting in approximately $600,000 in damage to public infrastructure, including roads, bridges and culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across the Oregon coast, Josephine County Oregon, and into northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National gridded snowfall analysis indicated 2 to 12 inches of snow at elevations above 1500 feet.  Route 3 over Scott Mountain was closed during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cold upper level storm system caused snow to fall at several mountain communities on May 3rd and 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell at some communities in the Riverside County Mountains from the evening on May 3rd through the afternoon on the 4th. A trained spotter near Idyllwild measured storm total snowfall of 1 inch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: National gridded snowfall analysis indicated 4 to 10 inches of snow.  This includes a report of 9 inches at the Sand Flat Snotel site.  A chain requirement was in effect for Interstate 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure strengthened off the California coast on June 29 then build inland into California on June 30. This resulted in the first heat wave of the season across the area between June 30 and July 3.  High temperatures across the San Joaquin Valley, lower Sierra foothills, West Side Hills and the Kern River Valley were generally between 102 to 107 degrees on June 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 102 and 107 degrees on June 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting snow brought unusually strong currents and cold water to area waterways in the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15-year-old man drowned in the Sacramento River near Sand Cove Park on June 4th, with his body found on June 5th. Strong currents pulled him and 2 others downstream. A bystander in a boat nearby was able to rescue two. Warm weather was occurring with highs of 96 at downtown Sacramento, 94 at Sacramento Executive Airport reported. A Sacramento Fire Department Captain confirmed that high water levels with strong currents and cold water temperatures were observed during search efforts. Water temperatures in the general area at the time measured between 55 and 65 degrees.  The cold water and strong currents were due to unusually heavy mountain snowmelt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Downpours from a slow moving thunderstorm created flash flooding on Humbug Creek Road in north central Siskiyou County.  This area is part of the McKinney burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downpours from a slow moving thunderstorm created flash flooding on Humbug Creek Road in north central Siskiyou County.  This area is part of the McKinney burn scar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed upper low pressure system approached the southern California coast on June 10. This system pushed a surge of moisture northward into central California and provided for enough instability for showers thunderstorms to develop across the area during the afternoon. While precipitation was generally light with most locations receiving less than a tenth of an inch, a few thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall. One thunderstorm produced localized roadway flooding in western Fresno County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding across SR 198 at Parkfield Junction along with rocks and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Merced River at Pohono Bridge peaked above it's flood stage of 10 feet each day between June 1 through June 7, June 10 through June 12 and June 14 through June 18. The Merced River at Happy Isles peaked near or above it's flood stage of 8 feet each day between June 14 through June 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yosemite National Park Rangers reported that the Merced River exceeded its flood stage of 10 feet at Pohono Bridge between June 1 and June 7, June 10 and June 12 and June 14 and 18. The Merced River exceeded its flood stage of 8 feet at Happy Isles between June 14 and 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low pressure system approached the southern California coast on June 5. This system provided enough moisture and instability for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. A few of the thunderstorms produced brief heavy rainfall. One thunderstorm produced hail as large as golf balls near Stallion Springs in Kern County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail was observed in Stallion Springs and posted on Facebook with pictures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed upper low pressure system moved eastward across southern California on June 11. This system pulled up wrap around moisture and spread it westward into central California. While most locations reported light rainfall, showers and thunderstorms did  produce locally heavy rainfall at some mountain RAWS stations during the afternoon and evening. One cell also produced roadway flooding in a flood prone area on the Mojave Desert resulting in some road closures during the evening of June 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at the intersection of Garlock Rd. and Redrock-Randsburg Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough moved into California on June 18 which brought much cooler temperatures to the area along with gusty westerly winds to the Kern County Mountains and Deserts between the evening of June 18 and the early morning of June 20. Several stations measured gusts exceeding 45 mph during this time frame and a few low impact indicator sites recorded gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: NORTHEAST SISKIYOU AND NORTHWEST MODOC COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough exited to Idaho while high pressure approached from offshore. The resultant light northerly flow brought clearing skies and freezing temperatures to northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing low temperatures in northeast Siskiyou and northwest Modoc counties included: DW9160 Dorris 31, Macdoel 26, Dorris 31, and Lower Klamath FTS 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough exited to Idaho while high pressure approached from offshore. The resultant light northerly flow brought clearing skies and freezing temperatures to northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing low temperatures in Modoc County included: Juniper Creek 32, Perez 29, Alturas 30, and Tickner Road 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful atmospheric river brought heavy rainfall, widespread flooding and gusty winds to the area. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2-6 inches across the coastal and valley areas with 6-16 inches across the mountains and foothills. With the heavy rainfall, widespread flooding, especially across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties. Strong southerly winds, gusting up to 94 MPH, were reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman and her 5 year old child were swept down a flooded roadway near San Marcos Creek. The woman survived, but her 5 year old child died.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE OF FRESNO AND KINGS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge built slowly inland into California between May 12 and May 14 which brought a significant warming trend to the area with temperatures rising to well above normal levels by May 13 after colder than normal temperatures prevailed for the first 11 days of the month. Portions of the southern San Joaquin Valley had high temperatures above 100 degrees F on May 14 which was the first day of triple digit heat in several months which resulted in elevated heat related impacts. Temperatures cooled a little by May 15 as the ridge weakened slightly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 99 and 102 degrees on May 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical jet stream aided in advecting sub-tropical moisture plume from over the Central Pacific into the California Coast starting March 9th through the 11th. This led to widespread flooding around the CWA, numerous landslides, sharp rises in rivers in Monterey County, and widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines due to strong, gusty winds. Widespread wind gusts 45 to 55 mph were reported on the evening of the 9th and into the early hours of the 10th. Heavy rains during the same time flooded numerous streams, creeks, rivers, and roads. 4-7 inches of rain fell across the Santa Cruz Mountains while 5-10 inches of rain fell on the Santa Lucia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Los Gatos RAWS reported a 79 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a warehouse on 650 85th Ave in Oakland collapsed at 3:20 AM. A 57-year old man was struck by falling debris and died; a woman suffered a broken nose. KTVU television reported that city inspectors found that water had been ponding on the roof and drains were not properly cleaned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Southern California brought significant amounts of atmospheric moisture northward over the mountains, producing showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rain and multiple reports of small hail. Heavy rain from thunderstorms over western Plumas County produced more than a dozen small debris flows along State Route 70, blocking the highway. There was also a rock slide with a large boulder at Donner Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The California Highway Patrol reported multiple slides mud/dirt/rock on SR 70 east of Twain, completely blocking the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The California Highway Patrol reported multiple mud/dirt/rock slides west of Hot Springs Ravine on SR 70/Feather River Scenic Byway.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Juniper Fire started near Perris, CA around 7:30 PM PDT on June 27th and burned 68 acres. Four structures, including two homes, as well as some vehicles were burned in the fire as well. Evacuation orders were also in place for roughly 6 hours as a result of the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juniper Fire started near Perris, CA on June 27th and burned 68 acres. Four structures were destroyed as a result of the fire, including two homes. Evacuation orders were in place for about 6 hours due to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued increased releases at Pine Flat dam due to heavy snow melt runoff resulted in a continuation of flooding near the Kings River downstream of Pine Flat Dam through most of May. The increased runoff produced flooding at Lindy's Landing campground near Reedley, and the Kings River Golf and Country Club near Kingsburg. The releases were cut back on May 30 which resulted in lowered impacts from flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresno, Kings and Tulare County Emergency Managers reported flooding along the Kings River downstream from Pine Flat Dam as a result of increased releases prompted by increased runoff from snow melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly inland across central California on May 4. The low brought enough moisture and instability to the area for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop across the area. While precipitation amounts were generally a quarter of an inch or less, some stations in Merced and Mariposa Counties measured between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall. In addition, some of the thunderstorms produced funnel clouds as the environment was highly sheared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMPH Channel 26 filmed a funnel cloud over Atwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly inland across central California on May 4. The low brought enough moisture and instability to the area for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop across the area. While precipitation amounts were generally a quarter of an inch or less, some stations in Merced and Mariposa Counties measured between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall. In addition, some of the thunderstorms produced funnel clouds as the environment was highly sheared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer observed a funnel cloud over Dos Palos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wildcat Fire ignited around 5:03 PM PDT on June 17th and burned roughly 171 acres until it was contained at 5:45 PM PDT on June 19th. Evacuation warnings were issued at San Diego Country Estates as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wildcat Fire ignited around 3:45 PM PDT on June 20th and burned roughly 171 acres. Evacuation warnings were issued at San Diego Country Estates as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Southern California brought significant amounts of atmospheric moisture northward over the mountains, producing showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rain and small hail. A period of heavy rain from thunderstorms over western Plumas County produced a debris flow over Feather River Canyon. Heavy rain also led to minor street and poor drainage flooding in the Quincy area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of a lightning strike that hit a tree, causing a branch to penetrate the roof of the building in the Shasta View neighborhood in eastern Redding, next to Mountain View. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Southern California brought significant amounts of atmospheric moisture northward over the mountains, producing showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rain and multiple reports of small hail. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical jet stream aided in advecting sub-tropical moisture plume from over the Central Pacific into the California Coast starting March 9th through the 11th. This led to widespread flooding around the CWA, numerous landslides, sharp rises in rivers in Monterey County, and widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines due to strong, gusty winds. Widespread wind gusts 45 to 55 mph were reported on the evening of the 9th and into the early hours of the 10th. Heavy rains during the same time flooded numerous streams, creeks, rivers, and roads. 4-7 inches of rain fell across the Santa Cruz Mountains while 5-10 inches of rain fell on the Santa Lucia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported a mud slide moving debris on California Highway 116.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge built slowly inland into California between May 12 and May 14 which brought a significant warming trend to the area with temperatures rising to well above normal levels by May 13 after colder than normal temperatures prevailed for the first 11 days of the month. Portions of the southern San Joaquin Valley had high temperatures above 100 degrees F on May 14 which was the first day of triple digit heat in several months which resulted in elevated heat related impacts. Temperatures cooled a little by May 15 as the ridge weakened slightly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 99 and 102 degrees on May 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This AR fed winter system shaped up to be a major heavy snow event for Mono, Mineral, and Southern Lyon Counties,  that extended south into Inyo and Esmeralda Counties. A deformation axis wrapped into the region with moist subtropical air transported by a moderate to strong AR that was directed into central and southern California. A strong southeast to east-southeast upper flow also aided in setting up the convergence zone in addition to upslope flow into the eastern Sierra. Impacts were considerable for routes between western and southern NV, that included closures of US-395, US-95, and Hwy-6 due to heavy snow in addition to strong south-southeast winds creating blowing and drifting snow and periods as well as near zero visibility. Dangerous wind chills occurred across the High Sierra with additional impacts for more vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS Observer (CA-ED-9) located 0.5 mi S of Tahoma, CA, and a COCORAHS Observer (CA-NV-20) located 2.0 mi NW of Meyers, CA, reported snowfall totals of 16.9 and 16.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder and wetter system brought periods of moderate to heavy snow and breezy conditions to the Sierra, northeast CA, and western NV.  Storm total snow across much of the Sierra and the Lake Tahoe basin saw a foot or more of accumulated snow. Accumulating snow fell along western Nevada foothills above 5000��� with a rain/snow mix for most  valleys.   Snow levels fell to valley floors after sunrise on Sunday, with scattered snow showers producing  quick bursts of snow during the remainder of the day region-wide.  Strong winds blasting along higher ridgetops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01) located 0.9 mi S  Mammoth Mountain, and mesonet station Mill Canyon (XMLC1) located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA reported a max wind gusts of 119 mph and 68 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm shaped out to be the strongest of our recent series of late February winter storms. A low positioned off-shore the Pacific Northwest set a favorable pattern for the transport of colder northern Pacific moisture that wrapped into an attendant cold front. This storm possessed a combination of a cold air mass, sufficient moisture, strong winds, instability, and dynamic forcing to produce heavy Sierra snowfall with periods of blizzard conditions Monday through early Wednesday. With snow rations approaching 15-17:1, the light fluffy snowfall coupled with wind gusts exceeding 50 mph brought near zero visibility at times to Sierra communities Monday afternoon through Tuesday afternoon. Sierra ridge tops saw wind gusts over 100 mph through this period. This storm system also contributed to prolonged winter travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP observers located at Tahoma, CA, Tahoe City, CA, and Boca Reservoir, CA, reported 24 hour snowfall totals of 25.0 inches, 17.5 inches, and 15.5 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued increased releases at Pine Flat dam due to heavy snow melt runoff resulted in a continuation of flooding near the Kings River downstream of Pine Flat Dam through most of May. The increased runoff produced flooding at Lindy's Landing campground near Reedley, and the Kings River Golf and Country Club near Kingsburg. The releases were cut back on May 30 which resulted in lowered impacts from flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresno, Kings and Tulare County Emergency Managers reported flooding along the Kings River downstream from Pine Flat Dam as a result of increased releases prompted by increased runoff from snow melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong upper ridge over the region which provided for much above normal temperatures across the area, and an easterly flow over central California  provided for some mid and upper level moisture to stream into the area. With moisture and instability sufficient for convection to take place, scattered thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon over the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains as well as across the Kern County Deserts. One thunderstorm in the desert produced heavy rainfall which resulted in debris flow flooding over a portion of U.S. 395 near Randsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported debris flow flooding along U.S. 395 near the intersection of Garlock Rd.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mother and her son from the Salinas area drowned at the Moss Landing State Beach on March 23. At the time of the incident, the Monterey offshore buoy reported 12.1 ft waves at an average period of 8.7 seconds, and a Small Craft Advisory was in effect for Monterey Bay. Dive teams noted that recent rainstorms and associated rainfall made for poor diving conditions and visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 7:47 PM on the 22nd, a person reported a 3-year old boy unaccompanied at Moss Landing State Beach. The Coast Guard states that the boy said his mother went swimming and his 7-year old brother was missing. Cal Fire retrieved the mother from near Moss Landing Harbor; on Thursday, Monterey County Sheriff's Office reported she was dead in the water. The Coast Guard, Monterey County Sheriff's Office, and North County Fire started to search for the 7-year old boy; the search was suspended at 2 AM Thursday and resumed Thursday morning. On Saturday, the Monterey County Sheriff's Department confirmed that the 7-year old boy was found dead in the water; a dive team found the missing child about 10 feet below the surface of the lagoon at 11:20 AM.

High Wind Warnings were issued for the entire region, and over 180,000 customers lost electricity during the event. Impacts were compounded by an earlier system that resulted in widespread damage, some areas of the Monterey Peninsula lost power again after having lost power for days from the previous system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fallen eucalyptus tree closed California Highway 92 near Half Moon Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying cyclone brought high winds to the region. A High Wind Warning was issued for the San Mateo Peninsula Coast, the Santa Cruz Mountains, and the Central Coast. In addition, a couple of Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued in light of the expected extreme winds for the Monterey and Santa Cruz areas. Over 110,000 customers lost power throughout the Bay Area and Santa Cruz County as a result of the cyclone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a white van traveling eastbound near the area of Alpine Road and La Cuesta Drive just west of Interstate 280 in Portola Valley. CHP responded to the scene at 1:34 PM, cleared some large branches, and found a man dead inside. Alpine Road was closed west of Interstate 280 in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of upper high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest region on July 21 and brought a period of dangerous heat to much of the area between July 21 and July 23. The ridge weakened by July 24 which allowed for a slight cooling trend to take place by July 25. Although temperatures remained above daily climatological normals for several days afterwards, temperatures remained below dangerous heat levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 101 and 106 degrees on July 21 and between 105 and 110 degrees on July 22 and 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat over a Mid-July weekend brought very hot temperatures to interior northern California and Major HeatRisk. Widespread triple digit temperatures were observed in the Valley and foothills. Redding Municipal Airport reached 111 degrees. Record high temperatures were set at Sacramento (109 degrees), Stockton (109) and Sacramento Executive Airport (107) for July 16th. Very warm overnight low temperatures were also observed, generally in the 70s to mid 80s. Records were set for overnight temperatures on July 17th, with Sacramento reaching 76 degrees, Sacramento Executive Airport reaching 72 degrees, and Modesto Airport reaching 75 degrees. Redding Municipal Airport tied it's previous record of 83 degrees for the morning low. Warmest lows were observed in northern Sacramento Valley and surrounding foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record high temperatures were set at Stockton on July 16th, reaching 109 degrees. Records were set for overnight low temperatures at Modesto Airport on July 17th, reaching 75 degrees.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four wildfires ignited in the Inland Empire July 14-15, in the midst of a very hot and dry period. The largest fire, the Rabbit Fire, burned 8,283 acres to the northwest of San Jacinto and southwest of Beaumont. All fires were contained within 3-8 days of ignition.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gavilan Fire started at 3:28 PM on July 15, and burned for six days before containment occurred by the evening of July 21. The fire burned 338 acres northwest of Perris, CA. Evacuation orders were issued during the height of the spread.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four wildfires ignited in the Inland Empire July 14-15, in the midst of a very hot and dry period. The largest fire, the Rabbit Fire, burned 8,283 acres to the northwest of San Jacinto and southwest of Beaumont. All fires were contained within 3-8 days of ignition.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Reche Fire started at 12:58 AM on July 14, and burned for seven days before containment occurred by early morning on July 21. The fire burned 437 acres east of Riverside, CA. A hard road closure at Perris Blvd N and Heacock Ave at Reche Vista was issued, with communities east of Perris Blvd to Moreno Beach Drive were placed under evacuation warnings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rabbit Fire started at 3:30 PM on July 14, and burned for eight days before containment occurred by the evening of July 22. The fire burned 8,283 acres northwest of San Jacinto, CA. Evacuation orders and warnings were issued.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Highland Fire started the afternoon on July 14, and burned for three days before containment occurred by the evening of July 17. The fire burned 105 acres near Beaumont, California.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the southwestern US led to increasing heat over Southern California in early July. High heat occurred each afternoon July 1st, 2nd, and 3rd, after which temperatures slowly cooled down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat wave impacted the San Diego County Deserts starting on July 1st and lasted through the 3rd. Highs each afternoon at Borrego Valley Airport topped out at 113 degrees on the 1st and 2nd, and hit 115 degrees on the 3rd. Excessive Heat Warnings were in effect during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical jet stream aided in advecting sub-tropical moisture plume from over the Central Pacific into the California Coast starting March 9th through the 11th. This led to widespread flooding around the CWA, numerous landslides, sharp rises in rivers in Monterey County, and widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines due to strong, gusty winds. Widespread wind gusts 45 to 55 mph were reported on the evening of the 9th and into the early hours of the 10th. Heavy rains during the same time flooded numerous streams, creeks, rivers, and roads. 4-7 inches of rain fell across the Santa Cruz Mountains while 5-10 inches of rain fell on the Santa Lucia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A levee on the Pajaro River east of the community of Pajaro was overtopped overnight, and subsequently failed, flooding the community of Pajaro and surrounding farmland. The area was evacuated, with residents only able to return after two weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure remained centered over California on July 1 and 2 maintaining much above normal temperatures overt the area with several locations nearing climatological daily records. Temperatures cooled by 4-5 degrees on July 3 as the large high pressure area shifted to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 106 and 111 degrees on July 1 and 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of upper high pressure build westward over the Desert Southwest region on July 13 and 14 and became centered over northern Arizona by July 15 resulting in near record heat taking place across the area. The high remained in place maintaining the near record heat through July 17 where an excessive Heat Warning was in effect for all of the area except for the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada, and a Heat Advisory was in effect for elevations below 7000 feet in the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 105 and 110 degrees each day between July 15 and July 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical jet stream aided in advecting sub-tropical moisture plume from over the Central Pacific into the California Coast starting March 9th through the 11th. This led to widespread flooding around the CWA, numerous landslides, sharp rises in rivers in Monterey County, and widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines due to strong, gusty winds. Widespread wind gusts 45 to 55 mph were reported on the evening of the 9th and into the early hours of the 10th. Heavy rains during the same time flooded numerous streams, creeks, rivers, and roads. 4-7 inches of rain fell across the Santa Cruz Mountains while 5-10 inches of rain fell on the Santa Lucia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 101 near Chualar flooded, causing the highway to be shut down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought widespread high winds and rain, leading to significant damage, power outages, and injuries. High Wind Warnings were issued for the entire region, and over 180,000 customers lost electricity during the event. Impacts were compounded by an earlier system that resulted in widespread damage, some areas of the Monterey Peninsula lost power again after having lost power for days from the previous system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Francisco International Airport ASOS reported a gust of 74 mph at 11:56 AM PDT. This resulted in a ground stop that paused airport operations, and a ground delay program.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure remained centered over California on July 1 and 2 maintaining much above normal temperatures overt the area with several locations nearing climatological daily records. Temperatures cooled by 4-5 degrees on July 3 as the large high pressure area shifted to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 106 and 111 degrees on July 1 and 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat over a Mid-July weekend brought very hot temperatures to interior northern California and Major HeatRisk. Widespread triple digit temperatures were observed in the Valley and foothills. Redding Municipal Airport reached 111 degrees. Record high temperatures were set at Sacramento (109 degrees), Stockton (109) and Sacramento Executive Airport (107) for July 16th. Very warm overnight low temperatures were also observed, generally in the 70s to mid 80s. Records were set for overnight temperatures on July 17th, with Sacramento reaching 76 degrees, Sacramento Executive Airport reaching 72 degrees, and Modesto Airport reaching 75 degrees. Redding Municipal Airport tied it's previous record of 83 degrees for the morning low. Warmest lows were observed in northern Sacramento Valley and surrounding foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs were in the upper 90s to 105 degree range on July 15 and 16. Overnight lows were in the 60s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying cyclone brought high winds to the region. A High Wind Warning was issued for the San Mateo Peninsula Coast, the Santa Cruz Mountains, and the Central Coast. In addition, a couple of Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued in light of the expected extreme winds for the Monterey and Santa Cruz areas. Over 110,000 customers lost power throughout the Bay Area and Santa Cruz County as a result of the cyclone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were injured when a tree fell on them near Post and Polk in the Nob Hill neighborhood of San Francisco. They were taken to the hospital where one of the people died.
