CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in the Sierra Nevada killed one hiker and injured two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per the Eastern Sierra Avalanche Center, an avalanche above Red Lake on the northeast side of Split Mountain killed one hiker and injured two others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from decaying thunderstorms produced gusty winds across the Coachella Valley and the San Diego County deserts early in the morning on July 31. Widespread wind gusts over 40 mph occurred for an hour across the region, and reduced visibility as low as 1/4 mile was noted at the Thermal Airport and the Palm Springs Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty outflow winds from a decaying thunderstorm produced strong winds and greatly reduced visibility across the San Diego County deserts early in the morning on July 31. AWOS station KL08 Borrego Valley Airport reported a wind gust of 40 mph, and the Borrego Springs AWOS reported 1/2 mile visibility in blowing dust for about 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from decaying thunderstorms produced gusty winds across the Coachella Valley and the San Diego County deserts early in the morning on July 31. Widespread wind gusts over 40 mph occurred for an hour across the region, and reduced visibility as low as 1/4 mile was noted at the Thermal Airport and the Palm Springs Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty outflow winds from a decaying thunderstorm produced strong winds and greatly reduced visibility across the Coachella Valley early in the morning on July 31. ASOS station KTRM Thermal Airport reported a peak wind gust of 51 mph, with gusts over 40 mph reported from 2:40 AM - 3:40 AM, and 1/2 mile visibility in blowing dust for 10 minutes; reduced visibility of 1 mile was reported for an hour at KTRM. Palm Springs ASOS reported 1.5 mi visibility in blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow between 330 and 415 am. Visibility briefly dropped to 1.25 mi at 405 am. Widespread wind gusts in excess of 45 mph were reported across the Coachella Valley. 1-inch tree limbs were broken and shingles were blown off near Anza.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal thunderstorms occurred on July 25 in the Riverside County Mountains and San Diego County Mountains. Locally heavy rainfall of 0.50-0.75 was reported in Riverside County, and a lightning strike ignited a small wildfire in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall across the Riverside County Mountains on July 25. Mountain Center CWOP reported 0.51 inch of rain in 30 minutes, and the Anza SE gauge reported 0.62 inch in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal thunderstorms occurred on July 25 in the Riverside County Mountains and San Diego County Mountains. Locally heavy rainfall of 0.50-0.75 was reported in Riverside County, and a lightning strike ignited a small wildfire in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small fire detected on camera from an apparent lightning strike near 5364 feet, around 9 miles west-southwest of Borrego Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures brought widespread Moderate HeatRisk and Areas of Major HeatRisk over a late July weekend. Sacramento set a new daily high of 108 degrees for July 21st. Redding Municipal Airport and Red Bluff Airport tied the previous record daily high of 112 degrees for July 21st. Redding then set a new daily record high temperature of 113 degrees on July 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sacramento set a new daily high of 108 degrees for July 21st.

The high remained in place maintaining the near record heat through July 17 where an excessive Heat Warning was in effect for all of the area except for the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada, and a Heat Advisory was in effect for elevations below 7000 feet in the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 105 and 110 degrees each day between July 15 and July 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat over a Mid-July weekend brought very hot temperatures to interior northern California and Major HeatRisk. Widespread triple digit temperatures were observed in the Valley and foothills. Redding Municipal Airport reached 111 degrees. Record high temperatures were set at Sacramento (109 degrees), Stockton (109) and Sacramento Executive Airport (107) for July 16th. Very warm overnight low temperatures were also observed, generally in the 70s to mid 80s. Records were set for overnight temperatures on July 17th, with Sacramento reaching 76 degrees, Sacramento Executive Airport reaching 72 degrees, and Modesto Airport reaching 75 degrees. Redding Municipal Airport tied it's previous record of 83 degrees for the morning low. Warmest lows were observed in northern Sacramento Valley and surrounding foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs reached 105 to 110 degrees on July 15 and 16. Overnight lows were in the upper 70s.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple wildfires ignited in Riverside County on July 27. The Sunset Fire burned for one day and was quickly contained. The Bonny Fire burned through August 9 and consumed 2,322 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sunset Fire ignited near the intersection of Mesa Street and Gilman Street in Banning just before 7 PM. An evacuation warning was issued that evening for a small portion of Banning. The fire burned 103 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple wildfires ignited in Riverside County on July 27. The Sunset Fire burned for one day and was quickly contained. The Bonny Fire burned through August 9 and consumed 2,322 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bonny Fire was reported the afternoon of July 27 near the 44000 block of Bonny Lane in Aguanga. The fire exhibited a critical rate of spread, reaching 225 across several hours after ignition, and up to 2,206 acres by the following evening. By the evening of July 31, the Bonny Fire was mapped at 2,305 acres and 30% containment. The fire burned until August 9, when 100% containment was achieved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-tropical jet stream aided in advecting sub-tropical moisture plume from over the Central Pacific into the California Coast starting March 9th through the 11th. This led to widespread flooding around the CWA, numerous landslides, sharp rises in rivers in Monterey County, and widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines due to strong, gusty winds. Widespread wind gusts 45 to 55 mph were reported on the evening of the 9th and into the early hours of the 10th. Heavy rains during the same time flooded numerous streams, creeks, rivers, and roads. 4-7 inches of rain fell across the Santa Cruz Mountains while 5-10 inches of rain fell on the Santa Lucia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Road to Ranches station operated by PGE reported an 83 mph wind gust.

Over 110,000 customers lost power throughout the Bay Area and Santa Cruz County as a result of the cyclone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oakland Airport ASOS reported a gust of 74 mph.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of upper high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest region on July 21 and brought a period of dangerous heat to much of the area between July 21 and July 23. The ridge weakened by July 24 which allowed for a slight cooling trend to take place by July 25. Although temperatures remained above daily climatological normals for several days afterwards, temperatures remained below dangerous heat levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 101 and 106 degrees on July 21 and between 105 and 110 degrees on July 22 and 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought widespread high winds and rain, leading to significant damage, power outages, and injuries. High Wind Warnings were issued for the entire region, and over 180,000 customers lost electricity during the event. Impacts were compounded by an earlier system that resulted in widespread damage, some areas of the Monterey Peninsula lost power again after having lost power for days from the previous system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lucas Valley station operated by PGE reported a gust of 90 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought widespread high winds and rain, leading to significant damage, power outages, and injuries. High Wind Warnings were issued for the entire region, and over 180,000 customers lost electricity during the event. Impacts were compounded by an earlier system that resulted in widespread damage, some areas of the Monterey Peninsula lost power again after having lost power for days from the previous system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monterey Peninsula Airport reported a wind gust of 51 mph. Across the Monterey Peninsula region, downed trees damaged 13 homes and closed California Highway 1 at the Point Lobos State Natural Reserve, California Highway 68 at the SFB Morse Gate, and other roads across the city of Pacific Grove. Southbound California Highway 1 was also closed at California Highway 68, or Holman Highway, due to downed power lines. On Highway 1, a man was injured when he stopped due to a downed tree and was hit by a second falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hilary, the first tropical storm to impact Southern California in 84 years, brought heavy rain and significant flooding to Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Rainfall totals were very impressive with mountain areas receiving 3 to 9 inches of rainfall while coastal and valley areas received 2.50 to 5 inches of rainfall. Rainfall rates were also very impressive with rates around 1 inch in less than an hour reported at times. With such heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flash flooding as well as mud and debris flows were received. As for winds, most areas reported gusts in the 30 to 50 MPH with local gusts in excess of 80 MPH in some mountains locations in Los Angeles county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain generated flash flooding near the community of Burbank. Northbound lanes of Highway 170 were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level area of high pressure caused very hot daytime temperatures to occur in late August 2023. It also caused the daily marine layer low clouds/fog to become more confined to the coast, which resulted in dense fog impacting San Diego International Airport for an extended period of time, resulting in a large number of delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot daytime temperatures occurred across the Inland Empire August 28-30th, when Excessive Heat Warnings were in effect. Highs between 100-107 occurred each afternoon, with the hottest temperatures occurring near Chino, Riverside, and San Bernardino.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure shifted westward from the four corners region into central California on August 14 resulting in a period of significant heat across the area between August 14 and August 17  High temperatures across much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and lower Sierra foothills ranged between 104 and 109 Degrees F each day during this period. The high weakened on August 18 which allowed for a cooling trend to take place beginning on August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 104 and 109 degrees each day between August 14 and August 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level area of high pressure caused very hot daytime temperatures to occur in late August 2023. It also caused the daily marine layer low clouds/fog to become more confined to the coast, which resulted in dense fog impacting San Diego International Airport for an extended period of time, resulting in a large number of delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot daytime temperatures occurred across the Riverside County Mountains on August 28-30th, when Heat Advisories were in effect. Highs in the lower 90s to just over 100 degrees occurred, with Anza and Idyllwild seeing the warmest temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surge of moisture associated with Hurricane Hillary pushed northward into central California on August 19. This moisture surge resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms mainly the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening hours with the most noticeable impact being outflow wind gusts. Hillary weakened into a Tropical Storm and moved into southern California on August 20 resulting in moderate to heavy rainfall across the mountains and deserts of Kern County with several stations picking up between 3 and 5 inches of rainfall with a few stations receiving more than 5.5 inches of rainfall. Much of the Sierra Nevada south of Kings Canyon picked up between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall while the south San Joaquin Valley measured between a half inch and an inch. Further north, rainfall amounts were lower, but many new daily rainfall records were set for August 20. Numerous reports of flash flooding and road closures were observed in Kern County during the afternoon of August 20 through the early morning of August 21. The rains dissipated during the morning of August 21, but an offshore trough provided for enough instability for thunderstorms to break out over the area during the afternoon of August 21 which produced locally heavy rainfall. The trough lifted northeast on August 22 resulting in the moisture being pushed out of our area and drier conditions prevailed for the remainder of the week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresno TV station showed a video of a funnel cloud in Northwest Fresno near the intersection of Shaw Ave. and Brawley Ave.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fast flowing water, rock, and debris covered Whitney Portal Rd, the Whitney Portal parking lots, and the road to Lone Pine Campground. Whitney Portal Rd was destroyed by high flows in Lone Pine Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eastern Pacific Hurricane Hilary made landfall on the Baja Peninsula early on the morning of the 20th and reached Southern California as a tropical storm that afternoon. The cyclone brought widespread flash flooding to southeast California, along with isolated high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Amargosa River flooded Old Spanish Trail Crossing in Tecopa for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surge of moisture associated with Hurricane Hillary pushed northward into central California on August 19. This moisture surge resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms mainly the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening hours with the most noticeable impact being outflow wind gusts. Hillary weakened into a Tropical Storm and moved into southern California on August 20 resulting in moderate to heavy rainfall across the mountains and deserts of Kern County with several stations picking up between 3 and 5 inches of rainfall with a few stations receiving more than 5.5 inches of rainfall. Much of the Sierra Nevada south of Kings Canyon picked up between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall while the south San Joaquin Valley measured between a half inch and an inch. Further north, rainfall amounts were lower, but many new daily rainfall records were set for August 20. Numerous reports of flash flooding and road closures were observed in Kern County during the afternoon of August 20 through the early morning of August 21. The rains dissipated during the morning of August 21, but an offshore trough provided for enough instability for thunderstorms to break out over the area during the afternoon of August 21 which produced locally heavy rainfall. The trough lifted northeast on August 22 resulting in the moisture being pushed out of our area and drier conditions prevailed for the remainder of the week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree blocking the roadway on N. Safford Ave. between E. Rialto Ave. and E. Santa Ana Ave.

Hillary weakened into a Tropical Storm and moved into southern California on August 20 resulting in moderate to heavy rainfall across the mountains and deserts of Kern County with several stations picking up between 3 and 5 inches of rainfall with a few stations receiving more than 5.5 inches of rainfall. Much of the Sierra Nevada south of Kings Canyon picked up between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall while the south San Joaquin Valley measured between a half inch and an inch. Further north, rainfall amounts were lower, but many new daily rainfall records were set for August 20. Numerous reports of flash flooding and road closures were observed in Kern County during the afternoon of August 20 through the early morning of August 21. The rains dissipated during the morning of August 21, but an offshore trough provided for enough instability for thunderstorms to break out over the area during the afternoon of August 21 which produced locally heavy rainfall. The trough lifted northeast on August 22 resulting in the moisture being pushed out of our area and drier conditions prevailed for the remainder of the week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported portions of southbound Interstate 5 from the Grapevine scales to Lebec were completely flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, lightning strikes triggered the outbreak of the SKU August Lightning Complex.  The fire was located 2 miles south of Yreka, CA.  It was burning in timber and brush with energy infrastructure threatened.  As of the last report at 0530 on 08/26, the complex was 883 acres, 95 percent contained, did not destroy any structures, and cost $8M.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, lightning strikes triggered the outbreak of the SKU August Lightning Complex.  The fire was located 2 miles south of Yreka, CA.  It was burning in timber and brush with energy infrastructure threatened.  As of the last report at 0530 on 08/26, the complex was 883 acres, 95 percent contained, did not destroy any structures, and cost $8M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, lightning strikes triggered the outbreak of the Happy Camp Complex, with a total of 11 fires.  They were located 21 miles west of Yreka, CA.  It was burning in timber and heavy slash with several communities, and communications and energy infrastructure threatened.  As of 0530 on 09/01, the complex was 27,161 acres, 43 percent contained, destroyed 9 structures, and cost $50.4M.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, lightning strikes triggered the outbreak of the Happy Camp Complex, with a total of 11 fires.  They were located 21 miles west of Yreka, CA.  It was burning in timber and heavy slash with several communities, and communications and energy infrastructure threatened.  As of 0530 on 09/01, the complex was 27,161 acres, 43 percent contained, destroyed 9 structures, and cost $50.4M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hilary, the first tropical storm to impact Southern California in 84 years, brought heavy rain and significant flooding to Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Rainfall totals were very impressive with mountain areas receiving 3 to 9 inches of rainfall while coastal and valley areas received 2.50 to 5 inches of rainfall. Rainfall rates were also very impressive with rates around 1 inch in less than an hour reported at times. With such heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flash flooding as well as mud and debris flows were received. As for winds, most areas reported gusts in the 30 to 50 MPH with local gusts in excess of 80 MPH in some mountains locations in Los Angeles county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain generated flash flooding in the community of San Fernando. On Interstate 405, numerous vehicles were stranded in the flooded roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system which contained the remnant moisture from former Tropical Storm Eugene was situated off the California coast on August 12. This moisture helped fuel scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours over the Sierra Nevada. A lightning strike from one of the thunderstorms downed a large tree and started a small fire near Shaver Lake during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report that that a lightning strike hit a tree, started a small fire; and downed the tree blocking both lanes of Dinkey Creek Rd. near the SR 168 exchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved through an area near the McKinney wildfire in the late afternoon and evening, around 230 PM to 8 PM PST.  Rainfall was estimated to be at a rate of up to 1 inch per hour, with 1 to 3 inches total. This created 2 mud slides that closed portions of Highway 96. The first was reported 2 miles east of the community of Klamath River at 327 PM PST, and the second 2 miles southwest of Klamath River at 635 PM PST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved through an area near the McKinney wildfire in the late afternoon and evening, around 230 PM to 8 PM PST. Rainfall was estimated to be at a rate of up to 1 inch per hour, with 1 to 3 inches total. This created 2 mud slides that closed portions of Highway 96. The first was reported 2 miles east of the community of Klamath River at 327 PM PST, and the second 2 miles southwest of Klamath River at 635 PM PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure shifted westward from the four corners region into central California on August 14 resulting in a period of significant heat across the area between August 14 and August 17  High temperatures across much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and lower Sierra foothills ranged between 104 and 109 Degrees F each day during this period. The high weakened on August 18 which allowed for a cooling trend to take place beginning on August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 104 and 109 degrees each day between August 14 and August 17.

Rainfall totals were very impressive with mountain areas receiving 3 to 9 inches of rainfall while coastal and valley areas received 2.50 to 5 inches of rainfall. Rainfall rates were also very impressive with rates around 1 inch in less than an hour reported at times. With such heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flash flooding as well as mud and debris flows were received. As for winds, most areas reported gusts in the 30 to 50 MPH with local gusts in excess of 80 MPH in some mountains locations in Los Angeles county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain generated flash flooding near the community of Diamond Bar. Significant flooding was reported on Highway 57.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS SOUTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure shifted westward from the four corners region into central California on August 14 resulting in a period of significant heat across the area between August 14 and August 17  High temperatures across much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and lower Sierra foothills ranged between 104 and 109 Degrees F each day during this period. The high weakened on August 18 which allowed for a cooling trend to take place beginning on August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 104 and 109 degrees each day between August 14 and August 17.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 21-year-old male residing in Pacific Grove drowned in the sea off Carmel-by-the-Sea on Thursday August 31, 2023. The man had planned to go fishing north of Stewarts Cove on August 30 and did not return home that night as planned. Family members found his car in the Carmel River beach parking lot and reported him missing around 11 AM. The body was reported to authorities around 11:45 AM and recovered just after noon off Scenic Road. A small craft advisory for northwest winds 15 to 25 knots was in effect Wednesday, August 30 including during the evening until 9 pm. Lighter northwest winds and no small craft advisory were in effect Thursday morning, August 31, when the deceased male was found. Buoy 46042 reported wave heights of 6 to 9 feet on August 30, with periods of 7 to 9 seconds and wind gusts up to 25 knots. Wave heights at buoy 46239 ranged between 7 to 8.5 feet at 8 to 12 seconds, generally from the west or northwest. At Cabrillo Point, wave heights ranged from 3 to 3.5 feet at 8 to 12 seconds.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 73-year old resident of Hercules was swept into the sea at Point Reyes on November 10, where big waves and sneaker waves were breaking along the shore. Marin County firefighters responded to reports of a person struggling in the water around 9:07 AM, and six rescue swimmers, four helicopters, and three rescue watercraft were deployed from federal, state, and county authorities. The person was recovered motionless around 9:49 AM and was airlifted onshore, where he was pronounced dead and in cardiopulmonary arrest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 73-year old resident of Hercules was swept into the sea at Point Reyes on November 10, where big waves and sneaker waves were breaking along the shore. Marin County firefighters responded to reports of a person struggling in the water around 9:07 AM, and six rescue swimmers, four helicopters, and three rescue watercraft were deployed from federal, state, and county authorities. The person was recovered motionless around 9:49 AM and was airlifted onshore, where he was pronounced dead and in cardiopulmonary arrest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration heat wave impacted inland portions of Southern California July 7 to 29 with nearly continuous warnings and advisories. San Diego County public health reported 224 heat related incidents around their network of 23 hospitals. Numerous record highs were broken in the lower deserts where temperatures topped 115 for many days in a row, as well as across the inland valleys and mountains. Overnight low temperatures remained elevated in the lower deserts as lows only fell into the mid-80s to mid-90s, exacerbating the extreme heat risk. ||July 2023 was the hottest month on record at Palm Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long-duration, excessive heat wave impacted the Coachella Valley from July 11-22. Highs in the lower deserts generally ranged from 110-120 each day, with the highest temperatures occurring on Thursday, July 20 and Friday, July 21. The following are the highest temperatures recorded through the event, and the days on which they occurred:||Palm Springs AP: 120 (7/21/23); Palm Springs ranged from 113-120 through the duration of the event, with overnight lows of 83-93 degrees each day. Palm Springs broke a high temperature record of 119 (7/20), breaking the previous record of 118, and 120 (7/21), tying the previous record of 120.||Indio: 119 (7/20/23); Indio ranged from 111-119 through the duration of the event, with overnight lows of 90-96 degrees each day. Indio broke a highest minimum temperature record at 94 (7/18), breaking the previous record of 91. Indio broke a high temperature record of 119 (7/20), breaking the previous record of 117, and 118 (7/21), breaking the previous record of 117.||Thermal Airport: 118 (7/20/23 and 7/21/23) Thermal ranged from 111-118 through the duration of the event, with overnight lows of 77-93 degrees each day. Thermal broke a highest minimum temperature record at 89 (7/13), breaking the previous record of 88, at 92 (7/18), breaking the previous record of 86. Thermal broke a high temperature record of 117 (7/20), breaking the previous record of 115.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hilary made landfall across northern Baja California on August 20, and moved through Southern California that afternoon and evening. Torrential rainfall occurred across mountain and desert communities, resulting in catastrophic flash flooding and debris flows, damaged railways and bridges, and the closing of major interstates including I-8 in San Diego County and I-10 in Riverside County. Widespread tropical-storm force winds were recorded across much of Southern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swift water rescue was conducted on Box Canyon Road in Mecca.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of roadway flooding on I-10 in Cabazon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering moisture from a dissipated tropical system brought dry thunderstorms to Northwest California from the evening of August 14th through the morning of August 15th. Over 1,000 lightning strikes occurred in 24 hours with numerous new wildfires across the region. The Smith River Complex in Del Norte County developed into the most notable fire causing power outages to all of Del Norte County and the closure of a U.S. highway for several weeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry thunderstorms produced numerous lightning strikes across the Six Rivers National Forest. Twenty-seven fires developed within the Gasquet Ranger District creating the Smith River Complex. The complex grew in size rapidly in the first couple of weeks of the fire, jumping U.S. highway 199 causing it's closure for 3 weeks and resulting in the evacuation of the town of Gasquet. The fire also destroyed the high tension powerlines powering all of Del Norte County. The county was completely without power through August 25th when generators were installed to power the community. Main transmission lines were not repaired until September 10th when full power was restored. The fire continued to burn through September into October with fewer impacts to the community with a total of 95,000 acres burned plus 2 residences and 3 minor structures destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture with an unstable airmass caused afternoon mountain thunderstorms to develop over the Sierra. One of these thunderstorms brought hail as large as nickel size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CHP reported a truck accident on Interstate 80 near Blue Canyon that they thought was due to heavy rain and hail. The truck rain into a guard rail and overturned. The public reported nickel size hail from a location 1 mile northeast of Blue Canyon, by Putt Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eastern Pacific Hurricane Hilary made landfall on the Baja Peninsula early on the morning of the 20th and reached Southern California as a tropical storm that afternoon. The cyclone brought widespread flash flooding to southeast California, along with isolated high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slide of mud and gravel covered Highway 395 at Ash Creek Rd. Mud flowed heavily along the side of the highway and into part of the southbound lanes. Due to ongoing flooding in multiple places, Highway 395 was eventually closed all the way from Bishop to Pearsonville overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hilary made landfall across northern Baja California on August 20, and moved through Southern California that afternoon and evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with Tropical Cyclone Hilary impacted much of San Bernardino County starting late on August 19th. The heaviest rainfall occurred on August 20th which led to numerous reports of flash flooding and debris flows. Light rainfall continued through the 21st as well. The heaviest rainfall occurred in the San Bernardino County Mountains, where anywhere from about 2.00-6.00 of rain fell on average. Heavier amounts include 13.07 at Upper Mission Creek (UMCC1), 10.34 at Crestline (CA-SR-84), 9.71 at the Lytle Creek RAWS site, 8.23 at Running Springs, 6.92 at Wrightwood (CA-SR-69), and 6.44 at Forest Falls (FFPC1). Rainfall totals in the Apple and Lucerne Valleys generally ranged from 1.00-3.00. Notable higher totals in this area include Mojave Forks Dam southeast of Hesperia (MVDC1) receiving 6.43 of rain, and Phelan (CW6479) receiving 3.74. Rainfall totals farther south across the Inland Empire generally ranged from 1.50-3.50. The Devore Fire Station observed a localized higher rainfall total of 4.24.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Union Pacific rail tracks were undercut and washed out when the Whitewater River broke a division bern at the California and Colorado River Aqueduct. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at Mammoth Mountain Ski Base, CA, reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 6-7 inches from the main lodge to the summit. There was a 48 hour storm-total snowfall amount of 8.0-10.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure centered over the Great Basin brought a strong round of Santa Ana winds to Southern California on November 20-21. Numerous damage reports were observed on the morning of November 20th, with very strong peak winds observed starting late in the evening on the 20th and running through the morning of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred across the San Diego County Mountains starting on the evening of November 20th and lasting through the morning and early afternoon hours on the 21st. The Sill Hill San Diego Gas and Electric (SDGE) Mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust of 80 mph, while the Big Black Mountain site recorded a peak wind gust of 70 mph. Other high peak winds include 68 mph at the Boulder Creek SDGE site, 68 mph at Boucher Hill, and 66 mph at the Mount Laguna Observatory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat over a Mid-July weekend brought very hot temperatures to interior northern California and Major HeatRisk. Widespread triple digit temperatures were observed in the Valley and foothills. Redding Municipal Airport reached 111 degrees. Record high temperatures were set at Sacramento (109 degrees), Stockton (109) and Sacramento Executive Airport (107) for July 16th. Very warm overnight low temperatures were also observed, generally in the 70s to mid 80s. Records were set for overnight temperatures on July 17th, with Sacramento reaching 76 degrees, Sacramento Executive Airport reaching 72 degrees, and Modesto Airport reaching 75 degrees. Redding Municipal Airport tied it's previous record of 83 degrees for the morning low. Warmest lows were observed in northern Sacramento Valley and surrounding foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Redding Municipal Airport tied it's previous record of 83 degrees for the morning low. Across the zone, highs reached 105 to 110 degrees on July 15 and 16. Morning lows were in the upper 70s to mid 80s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread gusty Santa Ana winds impacted Southern California October 29-31, 2023. The strongest wind gusts were reported in the San Bernardino County Mountains through northern portions of the Inland Empire and the Santa Ana Mountains. Very windy and dry conditions led to an environment ripe for wildfire growth. Several small wildfires burned, and the larger Highland Fire burned for over a week in the southern Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Highland Fire started at 12:37 PM on October 30 and burned for eight days before full containment. The fire started under very windy and dry conditions near Aguanga. The fire burned 2487 acres, destroyed 13 structures, damaged 7 further structures and injured two people. Evacuation orders and warnings were issued for surrounding areas. The fire was 100% contained by 1:18 PM on November 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rip current at Martin's Beach in Half Moon Bay swept four people out to sea on Saturday November 25. The four had gotten too close to the water, lost their footing, and went into the water. Two of the people self-rescued, the other two, a 5-year-old girl and her grandfather (a 54-year-old man), were swept out to sea. The timing of the event is unclear, but the Coast Guard search started at 1:20 PM. The girl was recovered and taken to the hospital, where she was pronounced dead. The man remains missing, and the search was called off the next day. At the time of the incident, the SF Bar buoy (46237) reported wave heights of 7 to 8 feet at 18 to 20 seconds. Winds were offshore (out of the E/NE) generally around 10 mph near the Half Moon Bay coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rip current at Martin's Beach in Half Moon Bay swept two people, a 5-year-old girl and a 54-year-old man, out to sea on Saturday November 25. The two had gotten too close to the water, lost their footing, and were swept out by the current. The timing of the event is unclear, but the Coast Guard search started at 1:20 PM. The girl was recovered and taken to the hospital, where she was pronounced dead. The man remains missing, and the search was called off the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system which had been situated off the California coast for several days moved inland through California on November 18 resulting in a period of precipitation and increased winds across our area between the morning of November 18 and the morning of November 19. Precipitation amounts varied greatly because some thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 18th which brought locally heavy rainfall to some locations but many stations across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a tenth and a half on an inch of rain while much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between a quarter inch and an inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level remaining above 8000 feet. This system also produced some noticeably strong wind gusts along the Mojave Desert |Slopes and the Tehachapi area during the morning of November 19. Several stations measured gusts exceeding 50 mph and a few stations had gusts exceeding 70 mph. By the afternoon of the 19th, the precipitation had ended and the winds had diminished as the trough moved to the east of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cameron Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread gusty Santa Ana winds impacted Southern California October 29-31, 2023. The strongest wind gusts were reported in the San Bernardino County Mountains through northern portions of the Inland Empire and the Santa Ana Mountains. Very windy and dry conditions led to an environment ripe for wildfire growth. Several small wildfires burned, and the larger Highland Fire burned for over a week in the southern Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Escala Fire started in the mid-afternoon hours on October 30 at Battle Mountain Open Space east of I-15 near Rancho Bernardo. Evacuation warnings and orders were issued for nearby areas. The fire burned 5 acres and achieved 90% containment several hours after ignition. Two lanes of I-15 were closed due to close proximity of fire.

Highs were routinely in the upper 80s to upper 90s during this time. The hottest day was October 20th when Anaheim hit 97 degrees for a high, Yorba Linda hit 95, while Fullerton hit 92.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early November storm brought rain, thunderstorms, and high elevation snow to interior northern California on November 6th and 7th, 2023. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated amounts of 1-6 inches of snow over the northern Sierra and Lassen Park area, locally up to 8 inches of snow over the higher peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated snow amounts of 1 to 6 inches around the Lassen Park/western Plumas County area above 6500 feet, with local amounts up to 8 inches at the higher peaks including Lassen Peak. Caltrans District 10 closed mountain passes such as Highway 108 (Sonora Pass) and Highway 4 (Ebbetts Pass) due to winter weather conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture brought thunderstorms over the Sierra the afternoon and evening of September 9th. The Quarry Fire started from a lightning strike on September 9th in Stanislaus National Forest. It was one of 42 fire starts reported in the area. The fire was allowed to burn through October into early November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Quarry Fire started from a lightning strike on September 9th at 732 p.m. PDT in Stanislaus National Forest, one of 42 lightning starts in the area. It was located off Forest Road 3N01, east of Bourland Creek and near the Emigrant Wilderness west of Cherry Creek. This naturally started fire was allowed to burn within pre-determined boundaries. Winds were generally light over the area, around 1-5 mph with gusts to 10 mph. Humidity was around 100% with temperatures in the upper 50s. The fire reached 9,130 (reduced from the 12,935 acres preciously reported, due to more accurate mapping) by 11/9/23, when it was considered 100% contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal system moved through the area during the morning of November 6 bringing a short period of significant precipitation to Merced County and to the Sierra Nevada from Madera County northward. Precipitation amounts in these areas were generally between two and four tenths of an inch while further south amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less with Kern County remaining free of precipitation. This system was followed by another frontal system which moved through the area during the morning of November 7 which brought a tenth to two tenths of an inch of precipitation to the Sierra Nevada and a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall to the valley and foothill areas as well as to much of Kern County. Snowfall with these systems were  generally confined to areas above 8500 feet with 3 to 6 inches falling to the Yosemite area and 1 to 3 inches further south. In addition to the precipitation, gusty winds accompanied the second system and several locations in the Mojave Desert Slopes in Kern County recorded wind gusts exceeding 50 mph during the afternoon and evening. The system moved east of the area by the morning of November 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cottonwood Creek measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 15, 2023, lightning strikes triggered the outbreak of the Happy Camp Complex, with a total of 11 fires. They were located 21 miles west of Yreka, CA. It was burning in timber and heavy slash with several communities, and communications and energy infrastructure threatened. As of 0530 on 10/20, the complex was 21,725 acres, 100 percent contained, destroyed 9 structures, and cost 110.0 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moved through central California on November 15. A large offshore upper low pulled up some moisture which accompanied the frontal system as it moved through the area and widespread precipitation fell although amounts were light with generally a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills, a tenth to a quarter inch of rainfall in the Sierra foothills and a quarter to half inch in the Sierra Nevada with the snow level close to 10000 feet. The main impacts from this system; however, was a period of gusty winds most noticeably in the Grapevine area where a few locations measured gusts above 55 mph during the late morning and afternoon and also in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley which had some areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station 5NW Lebec measured a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and light winds over the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 17 allowed for areas of dense fog to form by daybreak as some low level moisture remained as a result of the system which moved through the area two days before. This was the first widespread dense fog event in the area as visibility was reduced to an eighth of a mile or less at several locations, and several school districts either opened late or delayed bus service for the first time this season as a result of the fog. The fog dissipated by late morning as another incoming system provided for some mixing to take place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Merced and Madera. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet. Several school districts opened late because of dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system which had been situated off the California coast for several days moved inland through California on November 18 resulting in a period of precipitation and increased winds across our area between the morning of November 18 and the morning of November 19. Precipitation amounts varied greatly because some thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 18th which brought locally heavy rainfall to some locations but many stations across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a tenth and a half on an inch of rain while much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between a quarter inch and an inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level remaining above 8000 feet. This system also produced some noticeably strong wind gusts along the Mojave Desert |Slopes and the Tehachapi area during the morning of November 19. Several stations measured gusts exceeding 50 mph and a few stations had gusts exceeding 70 mph. By the afternoon of the 19th, the precipitation had ended and the winds had diminished as the trough moved to the east of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska and into the Pacific Northwest on October 10 which resulted in a strengthening surface pressure gradient over the area. The gradients continued to strengthen as the trough dropped into the Great Basin on October 11. The strong gradients produced a period of strong wind gusts most noticeably over the south end of the Sierra Nevada and the Mojave Desert Slopes where several stations measures winds gusts exceeding 45 mph with a handful of stations reporting gusts above 60 mph. The winds decreased during the evening of October 11 as the trough moved east of the Great Basin and pressure gradients relaxed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft combined with the first Santa Ana wind event of the season to drive high temperatures across Southern California much higher than seasonal normals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures across inland portions of Orange County on October 4th through the 8th were notably hotter than seasonal normal. Highs were routinely in the middle to upper 90s, with some localized hotter locations. The hottest day was October 5th when Yorba Linda hit 101 degrees for a high, Anaheim hit 99, while Fullerton hit 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread gusty Santa Ana winds impacted Southern California October 29-31, 2023. The strongest wind gusts were reported in the San Bernardino County Mountains through northern portions of the Inland Empire and the Santa Ana Mountains. Very windy and dry conditions led to an environment ripe for wildfire growth. Several small wildfires burned, and the larger Highland Fire burned for over a week in the southern Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty Santa Ana winds occurred across the Santa Ana Mountains and Foothills from the morning on October 29 through the early morning hours on October 31. Widespread wind gusts over 35 mph were recorded in this area. The following are the strongest wind gusts that occurred in the Santa Ana Mountains/Foothills:||Fremont Canyon (1780'): 75 MPH|Pleasants Peak (3871'): 67 MPH|Upper Talega (2299'): 58 MPH|Trabuco Canyon (1501'): 57 MPH|Pleasants Peak (3895'): 56 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful atmospheric river brought heavy rainfall, widespread flooding and gusty winds to the area. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2-6 inches across the coastal and valley areas with 6-16 inches across the mountains and foothills. With the heavy rainfall, widespread flooding, especially across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties. Strong southerly winds, gusting up to 94 MPH, were reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding, south of the city of San Luis Obispo. Law enforcement reported a vehicle stuck in the mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A remote weather station located in Palisades Tahoe at Alpine Meadows (Ward Peak) provided a peak ridge top gust report of 154 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wildfires that developed after a dry lightning event in mid-August continued to burn in Del Norte, Humboldt, and Trinity counties in Northwest California. One fire, the Smith River Complex, was particularly damaging and is included here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry thunderstorms produced numerous lightning strikes across the Six Rivers National Forest. Twenty-seven fires developed within the Gasquet Ranger District creating the Smith River Complex. The complex grew in size rapidly in the first couple of weeks of the fire, jumping U.S. highway 199 causing it's closure for 3 weeks and resulting in the evacuation of the town of Gasquet. The fire also destroyed the high tension powerlines powering all of Del Norte County. The county was completely without power through August 25th when generators were installed to power the community. Main transmission lines were not repaired until September 10th when full power was restored. The fire continued to burn through September into October with fewer impacts to the community with a total of 95,000 acres burned plus 2 residences and 3 minor structures destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with an upper trough moved through central California on September 21. This system brought cooler temperatures to the area along with a period of increased winds to the area during the afternoon and evening hours. A few thunderstorms popped up ahead of the cold front during the afternoon over the Sierra Nevada. One thunderstorm over Yosemite Park produced a lightning strike which injured two hikers near Half Dome.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper article mentioned that two hikers were injured by a lightning strike while taking shelter in a cave near Half Dome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure center moved through northern California on September 30. This system brought widespread precipitation to the area although amounts were generally light. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between a quarter to a half inch of liquid precipitation with a tenth a a quarter of an inch of rain falling in the adjacent foothills and generally a tenth of an inch or less in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and in the Tehachapi Mountains. The snow level was around 9500 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of September 30 and lowered to around 8000 feet by the afternoon. Several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up between 2 and 5 inches of snowfall in what was the first significant winter storm of the season. An NWS incident meteorologist on dispatch to the Big Stump Redwood prescribed burn in Sequoia National Park sent a video of frequent lightning near the burn site during the morning of September 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture brought thunderstorms over the Sierra the afternoon and evening of September 9th. The Quarry Fire started from a lightning strike on September 9th in Stanislaus National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Quarry Fire started from a lightning strike on September 9th at 732 p.m. PDT in Stanislaus National Forest. It was located off Forest Road 3N01, east of Bourland Creek and near the Emigrant Wilderness west of Cherry Creek. This naturally started fire was allowed to burn within pre-determined boundaries. Winds were generally light over the area, around 1-5 mph with gusts to 10 mph. Humidity was around 100% with temperatures in the upper 50s. The fire reached 12,935 acres by 11/9/23, with an estimated containment date of 11/15/2023.

As of 0530 on 10/02, the complex was  21,725 acres, 87 percent contained, destroyed 9 structures, and cost $ 108M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed each afternoon during the weekend of 9-10 September, mainly over areas south of US-50. Subtropical moisture steadily flowing north along the western periphery of a longwave ridge centered over the US/Mexico border moistened mid-levels across southern portions of our CWA increased storm chances to 20-30%. Hazards included stronger outflow winds, frequent lightning, localized areas of heavy rainfall, and the increased chance for small hail in stronger storm cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (DEXC1-Dexter) located 13.0 mi ENE Crestview, CA, reported a storm total rainfall amount of 0.74 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet system brought periods of moderate to heavy rain with scattered thunderstorms. Lines of stronger thunderstorms brought local road flooding to portions of interior Northern California. There was also a confirmed EF-1 tornado touchdown in the Oroville area, with minor damage. Snow levels were high, with impacts limited to minor travel delays for Sierra pass levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Meteorologists surveyed storm damage in Oroville, and analyzed radar and video evidence. It was determined that an EF-1 tornado, associated with a line of thunderstorms, moved through for about 2 minutes before lifting, resulting in tree and minor structure damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong winter storm impacted Southwestern California. The storm brought winter storm conditions the local mountains with snowfall totals of 2 to 4 feet and southerly winds gusting in excess of 80 MPH. Other areas also experienced some strong winds. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions developed across the interior mountains of Santa Barbara county.  Snowfall totals of 1 to 2 feet were reported above 5500 feet. Additionally, there were some very strong southerly winds with the Tepusquet RAWS reporting gusts up to 59 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge built in to California on December 23 which provided for mainly clear skies and light wind across the area. With residual low level moisture handing around, areas of dense fog formed in along SR 58 in the Tehachapi area during the evening of December 23 and in portions of the southern San Joaquin Valley during the morning of December 24. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning which eroded by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Delano.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system approached the area on December 29 and crossed  central California on December 30. Winds picked up ahead of the system along the West Side Hills and in the Grapevine area of the Tehachapi Mountains on December 29 with several stations reporting gusts between 40 and 50 mph. The system also brought widespread precipitation to the area during the morning and afternoon of December 30 with much of Merced County and the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon picking up between a half and three quarters of an inch of rainfall while a quarter to a half of an inch fell further south in areas north of Kern County with many stations in the south end of the valley and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between a tenth and a quarter of an inch with a tenth of an inch or less in the desert areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway patrol reported an estimated 3 inches of new snow fell near Pine Mountain Club and road closures at the intersection of Mount Pinos Rd. and Cerro Noroeste Rd. and Cuddy Valley Rd. closed due to snow from Mount Pinos to Nordic Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant low level moisture in the area as a result of widespread precipitation over the previous few days combined with clear skies and light winds for areas of dense fog to form during the evening of December 22 along the SR 198 corridor between Lemoore and Visalia. Additional areas of fog formed overnight and toward morning in the central San Joaquin valley and in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts. with holiday travel being impacted during the morning of December 23. The fog dissipated in some areas and lifted into a low cloud deck in other areas by the late morning of December 23 with visibility generally improving to 3 to 6 miles by the early afternoon of December 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Madera.

Additional areas of fog formed overnight and toward morning in the central San Joaquin valley and in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts. with holiday travel being impacted during the morning of December 23. The fog dissipated in some areas and lifted into a low cloud deck in other areas by the late morning of December 23 with visibility generally improving to 3 to 6 miles by the early afternoon of December 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Reedley and Visalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low dropped slowly southward off the California coast on between December 19 and December 21. Moisture associated with this feature produced widespread precipitation across our area between the evening of December 19 and the morning of December 21 with most of the precipitation occurring during the afternoon and evening of December 20. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall while the West Side Hills picked up between an inch and an inch and a half. While amounts in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills varied, many locations picked up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. Several SNOTEL stations above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 3 to 8 inches of new snowfall while 1 to 3 inches of new snow accumulated between 7000 and 8000 feet. Elevations below 7000 feet generally picked up all rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported roadway flooding at the intersection of Lacey Blvd. and Santa Fe Ave. in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure off the California coast brought heavy rain and thunderstorms to Southern California on December 21st. The biggest impact was a microburst near the Carlsbad Airport, where a wind gust of 48 knots/55 mph was measured via ASOS. Signs at the airport were damaged in additional to several other reports of tree damage nearby. Several reports of street and flash flooding were also received across areas from the coast to as far east as Anza-Borrego State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding at SR-62 and Yucca Park with water flowing down the Morongo Grade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest flow behind an exiting trough allowed for low level moisture to bank up against the foothills in the southeastern San Joaquin Valley which resulted in a period of dense fog along the SR 65 corridor in Tulare County near daybreak. The fog was most noticeable in the Porterville area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Porterville.

Survey found a dozen trees downed, including a uprooted 3 foot wide eucalyptus tree, an uprooted 2 foot wide pine, and snapped 18 inch pepper tree. Many large branches and tops of trees were snapped off. Tree damage on both north and south sides of the CRQ airport which is on Palomar road, and most trees laying to the east. Yarrow, Kellogg, El Camino, Faraday, Camino Vida Roble and Corte Del Nogal roads affected. Airport reported a sign damaged near the tower.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft moved over California on December 10 while a cold airmass remained over the San Joaquin Valley. The resulting inversion along with clear skies and light winds allowed for some locally dense fog to form overnight in eastern Kings County. The fog dissipated shortly after daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued increased releases at Pine Flat dam due to heavy snow melt runoff resulted in a continuation of flooding near the Kings River downstream of Pine Flat Dam through most of May. The increased runoff produced flooding at Lindy's Landing campground near Reedley, and the Kings River Golf and Country Club near Kingsburg. The releases were cut back on May 30 which resulted in lowered impacts from flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresno, Kings and Tulare County Emergency Managers reported flooding along the Kings River downstream from Pine Flat Dam as a result of increased releases prompted by increased runoff from snow melt. Two young children who were swimming in the river with their mother on May 21 were swept away by the fast moving waters and were found dead shortly afterward. Neither child was wearing a life vest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that settled over southern NV/UT encouraged the return of greater thunderstorm coverage during this period  with near severe storms bringing heavy rain and flash flood concerns to the region. These strong storm cells also brought along gusty, erratic winds, small hail, and plenty of lightning. Slightly below normal temperatures continued before returning to near normal the middle of upcoming week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located  5.0 mi WNW of Susanville, CA, and a mesonet station (BKOC1-Black Mountain) located 22.0 mi WNW of Spaulding, CA, reported total rainfall amounts of 0.71 inches and 0.69 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate to strong southwest flow aloft and daytime heating enhanced afternoon and evening breezes with gusts in the 20-30 mph range with higher gusts in wind prone areas. These breezy conditions brought periods of isolated critical fire weather conditions, and choppy conditions across area lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA, reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous upper-level low pressure system moving in from the eastern Pacific moved across the west coast of the United States. This resulted in a tightening of the pressure gradient and thus strong westerly winds across portions of Imperial County, especially the western half of the county, where widespread gusts of between 50-70 mph along with areas of blowing dust significantly reducing visibilities were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather warm low pressure system supported by a moderate AR flow (IVT: 400-600 kgm-1s-1) anchored in the eastern Pacific offshore the western US, and initiated a stormy pattern over the region. This system swung multiple short wave troughs into California and Nevada that produced widespread  valley rain, higher mountain snow, some periods of freezing rain across northeast California to include lower valley areas of the Basin and Range, and breezy south-southwest winds. As snow levels dropped over a 48 hour period, moderate to heavy higher elevation snowfall increased travel difficulties over higher Sierra passes from northeast California to the Lake Tahoe Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations (LVTC1) located 1.1 mi S of Leavitt Meadows, CA (SNOTEL), (LELC1) located 3.7 mi S of Sonora Pass, CA (SNOTEL), and (LOBC1) located 8.4 mi SE of Walker, CA (SNOTEL) reported 24hr rainfall storm totals of 1.1 inches, 1.0 inches, and 0.5 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that was moving slowly east across northern California into the Great Basin late September into the early October brought increased shower and thunderstorm chances to northeast California, the Sierra Front and eastern Sierra to include parts of western Nevada. This included high elevation snow showers over parts of the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (DEXC1- Dexter) located 13.0 mi ENE of Crestview, CA, reported a total rainfall amount of 1.13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm complex known as a Mesoscale Convective Complex, which developed across southern Arizona during the evening hours on the 31st, moved westward into southwestern Arizona and eventually into southeast California during the late evening and overnight hours on the 30th-31st. Strong outflow winds originating from the complex moved westward into the Imperial Valley resulting in severe wind gusts as measured by the main surface observing sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 66 mph wind gust was report at the Imperial County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hilary brought abundant moisture along with strong winds to southern California on the 20th. In fact, Tropical Storm Warnings were issued for the first time in history along the southern California coast. On the 19th, a trough near the southern California coast promoted deep southerly flow that allowed deeper moisture to advect into the Southwest ahead of Hilary. Combined with jet dynamics, showers and thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours on the 19th, leading to an isolated instance of flash flooding in southeast California. Going into the 20th, very anomalous moisture accompanied Hilary with PWATs across southeast California climbing upwards of 2-2.5 and IVT values in excess of 15 normalized standard deviations from average. Thus, flash flooding was the primary impact across southern California with a very rare high risk of excessive rainfall being issued by WPC for part of the area. Rainfall totals across southeast California were as high as 2-3, while most areas across Imperial County and eastern Riverside County generally measured around 1.50 or less. However, much heavier rainfall amounts were observed closer to the southern California coast. In addition to flash flooding impacts, several instances of damaging winds occurred as strong low level winds upwards of 50-60 knots at 850 mb and 925 mb mixed to the surface in some cases. Several communities around the Salton Sea were under a voluntary evacuation order as a precaution for any potential impacts stemming from flash flooding and strong winds. Multiple reports of flooding impacts and wind damage primarily across Imperial County resulted from this event. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple unbridged areas along SR 111 between Niland and North Shore were impacted due to flash flooding. Flash flooding impacts spanned along this highway in Imperial and Riverside Counties. According to local media, there were at least 10 reports of vehicles that became stuck along the highway and needed assistance from Imperial County Fire Department. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep southerly fetch of rich moisture up from the Gulf of California led to scattered to widespread heavy rainfall in Imperial and eastern Riverside counties during the afternoon of the 1st. Moisture values were well above normal for the time of year with precipitable water values analyzed around 2.0-2.2 inches. A Flash Flood Watch was issued for much of the region. Favorable dynamic and orographic forcing helped lead to strong updrafts and training of storms over certain terrain areas. Radar estimated rain rates were up to around 2-3 inches per hour with locally higher rates over shorter periods. There was a RAWS station in eastern Imperial County that measured right around 3 inches in 1 hour. These high rain rates led to areas of high rainfall accumulation, with many areas seeing at least 1 inch of rain and isolated areas up to 3.5-4.5 inches, radar estimated. This resulted in numerous reports of considerable flash flooding around the region. The hardest hit was the town of Niland and immediate surrounding area. A few inches of rain fell east of Niland leading to a canal being overcome with water and silt. This exacerbated the flooding and several inches of water flowed through the town and surrounding areas. The major impacts led to an emergency proclamation by the County of Imperial. In addition to the road closures, there were several swift water rescues across Imperial and Riverside Counties, both by land and air. One of the air rescues was completed by the San Diego US Coast Guard. On top of all the flooding, the heavy mass loading and strong updrafts resulted in strong to severe downburst winds, which caused a couple semi truck rollovers, downed many power poles, and generated dense  blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ogilby Road, a flood-prone road, was closed between I-8 and Hwy-78 due to flooding. A later report said there were chunks of road washed out. A photo shared by Caltrans on September 2nd showed water still flowing over the road and a construction vehicle cleaning up large amounts of debris on the roadway.  No injuries or specific magnitude of damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system just off the California Coast and the subtropical high pressure system over the southern Plains led to strong southerly flow across southern California. This helped transport higher moisture content northward with precipitable water values reaching above 1.5. This led to moderate to strong instability across southeast California with MLCAPE values of approximately 1500-2000 J/KG. A thunderstorm complex developed across western portions of Joshua Tree National Park near Highway 177 and persisted for several hours as upslope flow led to training. Between 4-6 inches of rain fell according to doppler radar estimates, leading to overflowing washes. These washes intersect Highway 177 and thus led to the flooding, forcing the closure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 177 north of Desert Center closed due to significant flooding between approximately Mile Markers 13 to 18. No damages or injuries were reported.

A few inches of rain fell east of Niland leading to a canal being overcome with water and silt. This exacerbated the flooding and several inches of water flowed through the town and surrounding areas. The major impacts led to an emergency proclamation by the County of Imperial. In addition to the road closures, there were several swift water rescues across Imperial and Riverside Counties, both by land and air. One of the air rescues was completed by the San Diego US Coast Guard. On top of all the flooding, the heavy mass loading and strong updrafts resulted in strong to severe downburst winds, which caused a couple semi truck rollovers, downed many power poles, and generated dense  blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were reported knocked over and nearly in the road along Highway 111 between Aten and Worthington Roads. Winds estimated at 60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 1 to 2 inches of radar estimated rain fell in Niland and areas just east into the Chocolate Mountains. A large area of considerable runoff flowed through Niland and the surrounding area, as evident in reports and before and after Sentinel2 satellite images, with upwards of 6 inches to 1 foot of flowing water in some areas, per photos and videos. Major flash flooding impacts were experienced. The East Highline Canal east of Niland, was overcome with flood waters and silt, mainly north of of Flowing Wells Rd. The overflowing canal was first reported a little after 1600PDT and only exacerbated the flooding. Several roads became impassable, unsafe, and subsequently closed due to flood water. At least a few cars became stuck in flood water leading to swift water rescues by land and air. The exact number of rescues is unknown. In Niland, several photos and videos showed flooded streets and yards, with water coming up to homes and businesses. There were a few reports of water entering homes, but it is unknown how many homes were impacted. Also impacted in the area was agriculture, as several fields had flood water moving through them. It is unknown the extent of any damages to the agricultural fields or the exact number. Sentinel2 satellite images showed evidence of all the disturbed earth, deposited mud and silt, through Niland, the agricultural fields and surrounding areas. In a statement by Imperial Irrigation District, the impacts to East Highline Canal resulted in a delay of 133 water deliveries. Also the O Lateral canal, which branches from East Highline canal, suffered structural damage. The main highway through the area, Hwy-111, was flooded in several locations, which hampered relief efforts. The small town of Slab City, to the northeast of Niland, was cut off from the rest of the Imperial Valley due to the flooded roads. As a result of the significant impacts, the County of Imperial issued an emergency proclamation for additional and better resources. Red Cross assisted in relief and a couple shelters for evacuees were established. There were no injuries reported from this event and specifics on property and crop damage is unknown and thus grossly estimated.

A deep southerly fetch of rich moisture up from the Gulf of California led to scattered to widespread heavy rainfall in Imperial and eastern Riverside counties during the afternoon of the 1st. Moisture values were well above normal for the time of year with precipitable water values analyzed around 2.0-2.2 inches. A Flash Flood Watch was issued for much of the region. Favorable dynamic and orographic forcing helped lead to strong updrafts and training of storms over certain terrain areas. Radar estimated rain rates were up to around 2-3 inches per hour with locally higher rates over shorter periods. There was a RAWS station in eastern Imperial County that measured right around 3 inches in 1 hour. These high rain rates led to areas of high rainfall accumulation, with many areas seeing at least 1 inch of rain and isolated areas up to 3.5-4.5 inches, radar estimated. This resulted in numerous reports of considerable flash flooding around the region. The hardest hit was the town of Niland and immediate surrounding area. A few inches of rain fell east of Niland leading to a canal being overcome with water and silt. This exacerbated the flooding and several inches of water flowed through the town and surrounding areas. The major impacts led to an emergency proclamation by the County of Imperial. In addition to the road closures, there were several swift water rescues across Imperial and Riverside Counties, both by land and air. One of the air rescues was completed by the San Diego US Coast Guard. On top of all the flooding, the heavy mass loading and strong updrafts resulted in strong to severe downburst winds, which caused a couple semi truck rollovers, downed many power poles, and generated dense  blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy-111 was closed between the county line and North Shore due to several instances of flooded washes along the highway. No injuries or damages were reported.
